title: Proyección Financiera para Startups SaaS B2B (Plantilla a 3 Años)
prompt: Act as an elite Virtual CFO. Create a comprehensive 3-year P&L projection template for a B2B SaaS. Include variables for Customer Acquisition Cost (CAC), Lifetime Value (LTV), Monthly Recurring Revenue (MRR), Churn Rate, and Operating Expenses. Format it as an easy-to-read markdown table.
useCaseEn: Essential for startup founders preparing pitch decks to raise seed or Series A venture capital.
useCaseEs: Esencial para fundadores de startups que están preparando presentaciones para levantar capital semilla o de Serie A.
proTipEn: Feed the AI your current operating costs so the projection tables are perfectly tailored.
proTipEs: Consejo Pro: Dale a la IA tus gastos fijos actuales (servidores, nómina) para que la tabla sea matemáticamente exacta y aplicable hoy.

title: Guion de Cierre de Ventas High-Ticket (Manejo de Objeciones B2B)
prompt: Write a 3-step high-ticket closing script. Address the following objections: 'It's too expensive', 'We need to think about it', and 'We are already using a competitor'. Use the 'Feel, Felt, Found' neuro-linguistic framework.
exampleResult: [Objection: Price]\n'I completely understand how you feel... Many of our enterprise clients felt the same initially. However, what they found was that the ROI covered...'
useCaseEn: Perfect for Account Executives or Founders closing software deals over $10K/year on Zoom.
useCaseEs: Perfecto para Ejecutivos de Cuentas o Fundadores cerrando contratos de software B2B superiores a $10K/año vía Zoom.
proTipEn: Instruct the AI to adjust the tone to 'Consultative and Empathetic' to avoid sounding like a sleazy salesman.
proTipEs: Tip Exclusivo: Pídele a la IA que use un tono 'Consultivo y Empático', evitando sonar como 'un vendedor agresivo'. La retención a largo plazo comienza con la honestidad.

title: Lógica Base para Bot de Trading Algorítmico (Estrategia Reversión a la Media)
prompt: Act as a Quantitative Trading Analyst. Write the pseudocode and logic flow for a crypto algorithmic trading bot using a Mean Reversion strategy. Describe the entry indicators (RSI, Bollinger Bands), risk management (Stop Loss), and profit taking criteria.
exampleResult:
**Setup:**
- Timeframe: 15m
- Indicators: RSI (14), Bollinger Bands (20, 2)
**Entry Long:** When price closes below Lower BB and RSI crosses 30 from below...
**Risk:** 1% account risk per trade.
useCaseEn: Ideal for retail quants or crypto enthusiasts trying to automate volatile market swings in Python/MQL5.
useCaseEs: Ideal para quants minoristas o entusiastas cripto intentando automatizar reversiones de mercado volátiles en Python, PineScript o MQL5.
proTipEn: Ask the AI to convert the pseudocode directly into TradingView PineScript v5 so you can backtest it immediately.
proTipEs: Acelerador: Pídele a la IA que traduzca el pseudocódigo directamente a 'PineScript v5 de TradingView' para poder hacer backtesting de la estrategia inmediatamente.

title: Guion Viral de TikTok para Retención (Estructura Hook-Retain-Reward)
prompt: Act as an expert short-form video copywriter. Write a 45-second TikTok script for [PRODUCT/SERVICE]. Start with a pattern-interrupt hook, provide 3 actionable steps, and end with a strong CTA. Format with visual cues and screen text.
exampleResult: [Hook - Visual: Pointing at screen]: 'Stop scrolling if you want to double your reach...'\n[Step 1]: First, do X...\n[CTA]: Click the link in my bio to get the full checklist.
useCaseEn: Perfect for boosting watch time and algorithmic reach on TikTok and Reels.
useCaseEs: Perfecto para aumentar el tiempo de visualización y retención algorítmica en TikTok e Instagram Reels.
proTipEn: If the script feels too corporate, ask the AI to rewrite it in the tone of MrBeast or Alex Hormozi for higher engagement.
proTipEs: Si el guion se siente aburrido, pídele a la IA que lo reescriba con el tono hiperactivo de editores de TikTok modernos.

title: Página de Captura Optimizada para Conversión CVR (Fórmula PAS)
prompt: Write a high-converting landing page copy using the Problem-Agitation-Solution (PAS) framework for [PRODUCT]. Include a compelling headline, 3 benefit-driven bullet points, and a primary CTA that reduces purchase friction.
exampleResult:
Headline: Stop Wasting Money on Ineffective Ads...
[Problem]: You're spending thousands but seeing zero returns.
[Agitation]: Every day you wait, your competitors steal your market share.
[Solution]: Introducing our tool...
[CTA]: Start your 14-day free trial today (No credit card required).
useCaseEn: Ideal for Shopify dropshipping stores or high-ticket service funnels struggling with bounce rates.
useCaseEs: Ideal para tiendas Shopify o embudos de servicios high-ticket que luchan de conseguir tasas de conversión óptimas.
proTipEn: To maximize conversions, prompt the AI to add an 'Objection Handling' FAQ section at the end of the copy.
proTipEs: Para maximizar cierres, añádele al prompt: 'Crea una sección de FAQ orientada al manejo de objeciones detallada al final'.

title: Generador de Arquitectura Backend en Node.js (Microservicios)
prompt: Act as a Senior Cloud Architect. Design a scalable microservices architecture for a [TYPE OF APP] using Node.js, Docker, and AWS. Provide the folder structure, necessary dependencies, and a diagram description.
exampleResult:
**Folder Structure:**
/auth-service
/payment-service
/user-service
**Dependencies:** express, mongoose, amqplib...
**Architecture:** API Gateway routes traffic to individual Dockerized containers...
useCaseEn: Accelerates technical planning and documentation phases for SaaS MVPs.
useCaseEs: Acelera drásticamente las fases de planificación técnica, estandarización de código y documentación para software comercial B2B.
proTipEn: Ask the AI to also generate the 'docker-compose.yml' file to instantly launch your local development environment.
proTipEs: Ahorra tiempo: Pídele a la IA que también genere un archivo 'docker-compose.yml' estructurado para lanzar tu entorno micro-servicios local.

title: Estrategia Mensual de Contenido B2B para LinkedIn (Generación de Leads)
prompt: Act as a B2B LinkedIn Ghostwriter. Create a 4-week content calendar for a [ROLE/INDUSTRY]. Include 2 educational posts, 1 personal story, and 1 direct lead generation post per week. Provide specific hooks for each.
exampleResult:
Week 1:
- Monday (Educational): 3 mistakes founders make with X...
- Wednesday (Personal): How I lost my first big client...
- Friday (Lead Gen): I just opened 3 spots for consulting. Here is who I can help...
useCaseEn: Perfect for founders and consultants who need to build an inbound lead pipeline organically.
useCaseEs: Perfecto para C-levels corporativos o consultores SaaS que apuntan a conseguir contratos con empresas a través de marca personal.
proTipEn: Break down the 'Personal Story' prompt further by feeding the AI three specific challenges you faced last year.
proTipEs: Alimento clave: No dejes que la IA invente; dale 3 errores graves que cometiste en el pasado y dile que formule los posts de 'Historias Personales' basándose en ellos.

title: Auditor de Seguridad Lógica y Refactorización Python (OWASP)
prompt: Review the following Python snippet for OWASP Top 10 vulnerabilities. Identify potential SQL injections, XSS, or insecure deserialization. Provide the secure refactored code and an explanation of the fix: [CODE]
exampleResult: Observation: Line 45 uses string concatenation for a SQL query, making it vulnerable to SQL Injection.\nFix: Rewritten using parameterized queries... [Secure Code Snippet]
useCaseEn: Automates initial security reviews for freelance developers before pushing code to production.
useCaseEs: Limita riesgos legales automatizando revisiones de seguridad CISO iniciales en software financiero antes de empujarlo a producción en la nube.
proTipEn: If the output is too long or you hit token limits, add 'Review only the authentication methods first' to the prompt.
proTipEs: Control de contexto: Usa un formato por módulos diciendo: 'Primero refactoriza los modulos de autenticación, luego revisa el acceso a DB'.

title: Complete Blog Post Generator
prompt:
Act as an expert content writer and SEO strategist specializing in [NICHE] with 10+ years of experience creating high-ranking content.

Write a comprehensive, SEO-optimized blog post about "[TOPIC]".

INPUTS:
- Target keyword: [PRIMARY KEYWORD]
- Secondary keywords: [2-3 RELATED KEYWORDS]
- Tone: [professional / conversational / authoritative / witty]
- Target audience: [SPECIFIC AUDIENCE DESCRIPTION]
- Word count: ~1,500 words

REQUIREMENTS:
1. Headline: Use a power word + specific number or benefit. Keep under 60 characters.
2. Introduction (100-150 words): Open with a surprising statistic, bold claim, or relatable pain point. Include the primary keyword in the first sentence. End with a clear promise of what the reader will learn.
3. Body: 5-7 sections with H2/H3 subheadings that use keyword variations naturally. Each section must include at least one of: a real-world example, actionable step, data point, or expert insight.
4. Conclusion (100 words): Summarize the 3 most important takeaways and include a specific, compelling call to action.
5. Meta description: 155 characters max, includes primary keyword, ends with a CTA verb.

CONSTRAINTS:
- Do NOT use filler phrases like "In today's world," "It's important to note," or "In conclusion."
- Do NOT start more than 2 paragraphs with "I" or "You."
- Avoid passive voice except when emphasizing the object.
- Every paragraph must earn its place — if it doesn't teach, prove, or persuade, cut it.

Think step-by-step: first outline the structure, then write each section with intentional keyword placement.
useCaseEn: High-value utility for Marketing/Redes Sociales tasks.
useCaseEs: Utilidad de alto valor para tareas de Marketing/Redes Sociales.
proTipEn: After generating, ask: *"Review this post. Score each section 1-10 for value density, and rewrite any section below 7."*
proTipEs: Consejo Pro: After generating, ask: *"Review this post. Score each section 1-10 for value density, and rewrite any section below 7."*

title: Blog Post Outline Builder
prompt:
Act as a senior content strategist who builds outlines that consistently produce top-10 Google rankings.

Create a battle-tested blog post outline for: "[TOPIC]"

INPUTS:
- Target reader: [AUDIENCE — role, experience level, pain point]
- Content goal: [rank on Google / generate leads / educate / build authority]
- Primary keyword: [KEYWORD]
- Competitor URLs to beat: [LIST 1-3 IF AVAILABLE, or "none"]

DELIVER:
1. **3 headline options** — each uses a different formula:
   - One with a number (e.g., "7 Proven...")
   - One with a power word (e.g., "The Ultimate...")
   - One question-based (e.g., "Why Does... and How to Fix It")
   Rate each headline 1-10 for click-through potential.

2. **Introduction hook** — choose the best approach:
   - Story hook (personal anecdote angle)
   - Statistic hook (cite a compelling data point)
   - Provocative question hook
   Write a 2-sentence draft of the winning hook.

3. **7-10 main sections** with H2 headings containing keyword variations:
   - 2-3 sub-points under each section
   - Suggested word count per section
   - Content type per section: [tutorial / case study / data / opinion / comparison]

4. **Internal linking opportunities** — suggest 3 related articles to link to/from
5. **CTA for the conclusion** — specific action, not generic "subscribe"
6. **Featured snippet strategy** — identify 1-2 sections to format for position zero

CONSTRAINT: Each H2 must pass the "So What?" test — if a reader sees only the headings, they should understand the full value of the post.
useCaseEn: High-value utility for Marketing/Redes Sociales tasks.
useCaseEs: Utilidad de alto valor para tareas de Marketing/Redes Sociales.
proTipEn: Feed this outline into Prompt #1 for a complete article, then use Prompt #4 to repurpose across platforms.
proTipEs: Consejo Pro: Feed this outline into Prompt #1 for a complete article, then use Prompt #4 to repurpose across platforms.

title: Blog Post Title Generator
prompt:
Act as a headline copywriter who has written for BuzzFeed, HubSpot, and Copyblogger. You understand what makes people click AND what Google rewards.

Generate 20 blog post titles about "[TOPIC]" for [AUDIENCE].

STRUCTURE — include exactly:
- 4 How-to titles (e.g., "How to [Achieve X] Without [Pain Point]")
- 3 List posts (e.g., "11 [Things] That [Benefit] — #7 Is [Surprising]")
- 3 Question-based (e.g., "Is [Common Belief] Actually [Wrong]?")
- 3 Data-driven (e.g., "We Analyzed [X] [Things]. Here's What [Works]")
- 3 Contrarian takes (e.g., "Stop [Common Advice]. Do [This] Instead")
- 2 "Ultimate/Complete Guide" formats
- 2 Comparison titles (e.g., "[A] vs [B]: Which [Outcome] in [Year]?")

FOR EACH TITLE PROVIDE:
| # | Title | Type | SEO Score (1-10) | CTR Score (1-10) | Character Count |

RULES:
- Every title must be under 65 characters (Google truncation limit)
- Every title must contain at least one: power word, number, or emotional trigger
- Do NOT use clickbait that the content can't deliver on
- Avoid overused words: "amazing," "incredible," "game-changer"

Rank the top 5 and explain WHY they would outperform the rest.
useCaseEn: High-value utility for Marketing/Redes Sociales tasks.
useCaseEs: Utilidad de alto valor para tareas de Marketing/Redes Sociales.
proTipEn: Optimiza tus resultados iterando el prompt.
proTipEs: Consejo Pro: Optimiza tus resultados iterando el prompt.

title: Content Repurposer
prompt:
Act as a multi-platform content strategist who understands the unique culture, format, and algorithm of each social platform.

Take the following content and repurpose it into 6 platform-specific formats:

ORIGINAL CONTENT:
[PASTE BLOG POST, ARTICLE, OR DETAILED SUMMARY]

REPURPOSE INTO:

1. **Twitter/X Thread** (10-15 tweets)
   - Tweet 1: Standalone hook that works even without the thread
   - Each tweet: under 280 chars, delivers one distinct insight
   - Include 2-3 pattern interrupts ("Here's where it gets counterintuitive...")
   - Final tweet: CTA + 3-5 relevant hashtags
   - No hashtags in body tweets

2. **LinkedIn Post** (150-200 words)
   - Personal storytelling angle — start with "I" or a bold claim
   - Single-sentence paragraphs with line breaks for mobile readability
   - End with an open-ended question to drive comments
   - 3-5 hashtags at the bottom

3. **Instagram Carousel** (10 slides)
   - Slide 1: Bold headline + hook (this is the cover — must stop the scroll)
   - Slides 2-9: One key insight per slide (headline + 2-3 lines)
   - Slide 10: CTA ("Save this for later" / "Follow for more")
   - Include visual direction notes for each slide

4. **60-Second TikTok/Reels Script**
   - HOOK (0-3 sec): Pattern interrupt or bold claim
   - BODY (3-50 sec): 3 rapid-fire points with visual cues
   - CTA (50-60 sec): Follow, comment, or link in bio
   - Include [TEXT OVERLAY] directions

5. **Email Newsletter Version** (250-350 words)
   - Subject line: 3 options (curiosity, benefit, urgency)
   - Preview text (40-90 characters)
   - Personal tone — like writing to one smart friend
   - One core insight + one actionable takeaway
   - Single CTA

6. **3 Pinterest Pin Descriptions** (150-200 words each)
   - Keyword-rich for Pinterest SEO
   - Benefit-driven with emotional language
   - Strong CTA to click through

CONSTRAINT: Maintain the core message but radically adapt tone, length, and angle for each platform. A LinkedIn post should NOT read like a tweet. A Pinterest description should NOT read like an email.
useCaseEn: High-value utility for Marketing/Redes Sociales tasks.
useCaseEs: Utilidad de alto valor para tareas de Marketing/Redes Sociales.
proTipEn: Optimiza tus resultados iterando el prompt.
proTipEs: Consejo Pro: Optimiza tus resultados iterando el prompt.

title: SEO Content Brief
prompt:
Act as a Head of SEO at a content marketing agency. You create briefs that consistently produce top-5 ranking articles.

Create a comprehensive SEO content brief for the keyword: "[KEYWORD]"

DELIVER THE FOLLOWING:

1. **Keyword Intelligence**
   - Primary keyword + estimated monthly search volume
   - 10-15 secondary and LSI keywords with volume estimates
   - Search intent classification: informational / commercial / transactional / navigational
   - Keyword difficulty assessment: easy / medium / hard

2. **Competitor Analysis**
   - Analyze the top 3 ranking articles for this keyword
   - Average word count of top results
   - Common H2/H3 patterns across competitors
   - Content gaps: what top results are missing that we can exploit

3. **Content Architecture**
   - Recommended word count (justified by competitor analysis)
   - Suggested H2 and H3 headings (8-12 total)
   - Featured snippet opportunity: which section to optimize, and format (paragraph / list / table)
   - "People Also Ask" questions to answer (5-8)

4. **Technical SEO Specs**
   - Meta title (under 60 chars, includes keyword)
   - Meta description (under 155 chars, includes keyword + CTA)
   - URL slug recommendation
   - Schema markup type recommendation
   - Internal linking targets (3-5 suggested anchor texts)
   - External authority sources to link (3-5)

5. **Content Quality Guidelines**
   - E-E-A-T signals to include (Experience, Expertise, Authoritativeness, Trust)
   - Original data, examples, or case studies to add
   - Visual content needs: charts, screenshots, diagrams

CONSTRAINT: Do NOT recommend generic headings like "What is [Topic]" unless the search intent specifically requires a definition. Prioritize unique angles.

Think step-by-step: analyze intent first, then structure the content to best satisfy that intent.
useCaseEn: High-value utility for Marketing/Redes Sociales tasks.
useCaseEs: Utilidad de alto valor para tareas de Marketing/Redes Sociales.
proTipEn: Optimiza tus resultados iterando el prompt.
proTipEs: Consejo Pro: Optimiza tus resultados iterando el prompt.

title: Viral Twitter/X Thread
prompt:
Act as a Twitter/X growth strategist who has written threads with 10M+ impressions. You understand virality mechanics: hooks, pacing, and shareability.

Write a Twitter/X thread on "[TOPIC]" with viral potential.

THREAD STRUCTURE (12-15 tweets):
- Tweet 1: The HOOK — choose the strongest format:
  [ ] Controversial take that challenges conventional wisdom
  [ ] Mind-blowing statistic with source
  [ ] "I spent [X hours/years] studying [TOPIC]. Here's what nobody tells you:"
  MAX 280 characters. Must work as a standalone post.

- Tweets 2-4: Context and setup (the "why should I care?" section)
- Tweets 5-10: Core value — one distinct, specific insight per tweet with:
  - Concrete numbers or examples (not vague claims)
  - Analogies that make complex ideas simple
- Tweet 11-12: The "plot twist" or counterintuitive insight
- Tweet 13-14: Actionable takeaway (what should the reader DO?)
- Tweet 15: CTA + 3-5 hashtags (ONLY in this last tweet)

RULES:
- Every single tweet must deliver standalone value if screenshotted
- Include 2-3 pattern interrupts: "But here's what surprised me..." "This is where it gets wild..."
- Use specific numbers: "73% of startups" not "most startups"
- Short sentences. No jargon. No corporate speak.
- Do NOT use emojis as bullet points
- Do NOT start tweets with "Thread:" or "1/"

TONE: [witty and sharp / authoritative and data-driven / storytelling and personal]
useCaseEn: High-value utility for Marketing/Redes Sociales tasks.
useCaseEs: Utilidad de alto valor para tareas de Marketing/Redes Sociales.
proTipEn: Ask the AI to write 3 alternative hooks for Tweet 1, then pick the most provocative one.
proTipEs: Consejo Pro: Ask the AI to write 3 alternative hooks for Tweet 1, then pick the most provocative one.

title: LinkedIn Thought Leadership Post
prompt:
Act as a LinkedIn content strategist who helps executives and founders build personal brands that generate inbound leads.

Write a LinkedIn post about [TOPIC] that positions me as a thought leader in [INDUSTRY].

MY ROLE: [YOUR JOB TITLE / FOUNDER OF / EXPERT IN]
TARGET AUDIENCE: [CEOs / marketers / developers / HR leaders / etc.]

STRUCTURE (150-200 words — the engagement sweet spot):
1. **Line 1 (THE HOOK):** Must stop the scroll. Choose one:
   - Bold, specific personal statement: "I [did something unexpected]. Here's what happened."
   - Contrarian opinion: "[Common belief] is wrong. Here's proof."
   - Vulnerable admission: "I failed at [X]. It taught me [Y]."
   This line appears above the "see more" fold — it determines 90% of engagement.

2. **Body (short paragraphs, 1-2 sentences each):**
   - Share a personal anecdote, case study, or observation
   - Include ONE specific data point or result
   - Use white space aggressively — no walls of text
   - Add 1-2 line breaks between paragraphs for mobile readability

3. **Closing question:** End with an open-ended question that invites comments (not yes/no)

4. **Hashtags:** 3-5 relevant hashtags at the very bottom

CONSTRAINTS:
- Do NOT use corporate jargon: "synergy," "leverage," "ecosystem"
- Do NOT start with "I'm excited to share..." or "Thrilled to announce..."
- Do NOT use motivational quotes from famous people
- Write like a real human having a conversation, not a press release
useCaseEn: High-value utility for Marketing/Redes Sociales tasks.
useCaseEs: Utilidad de alto valor para tareas de Marketing/Redes Sociales.
proTipEn: Optimiza tus resultados iterando el prompt.
proTipEs: Consejo Pro: Optimiza tus resultados iterando el prompt.

title: Social Media Content Calendar
prompt:
Act as a social media director managing content for a growth-stage brand. You plan strategically, not randomly.

Create a 30-day social media content calendar for [BUSINESS/BRAND] in [INDUSTRY].

INPUTS:
- Platforms: [Instagram, Twitter/X, LinkedIn, TikTok — pick 2-3]
- Posting frequency: [X posts/week per platform]
- Content pillars: [LIST 3-5 THEMES, e.g., education, behind-the-scenes, social proof, product, community]
- Brand voice: [DESCRIBE IN 3 ADJECTIVES]
- Current month/season: [MONTH YEAR]

FOR EACH DAY PROVIDE:
| Day | Platform | Format | Pillar | Topic/Hook (1 sentence) | Best Post Time | Notes |

WEEKLY THEMES:
- Week 1: Educational value (establish expertise)
- Week 2: Behind-the-scenes + personal brand (build trust)
- Week 3: Social proof + user stories (create FOMO)
- Week 4: Promotional + CTA-focused (convert)

- Every post must pass the "Would I stop scrolling for this?" test
- Include a mix of formats: static, carousel, reel/video, story, text-only
- Do NOT suggest posting the same content across platforms — adapt for each

title: YouTube Video Script
prompt:
Act as a YouTube scriptwriter who has written for channels with 1M+ subscribers. You understand retention curves, pattern interrupts, and the YouTube algorithm.

Write a complete YouTube video script about "[TOPIC]" for a channel focused on [NICHE].

TARGET LENGTH: [8 / 12 / 15] minutes
TONE: [energetic / calm and thoughtful / educational / entertaining]
TARGET AUDIENCE: [DESCRIBE VIEWER]

SCRIPT STRUCTURE:

**HOOK (0:00-0:10)** — The first 10 seconds determine 50% of retention:
Choose the strongest hook type for this topic:
- Bold promise: "By the end of this video, you'll know exactly how to [OUTCOME]"
- Shocking stat: "[STAT] — and nobody is talking about why"
- Pattern interrupt: Show/say something unexpected
Write the exact words to say.

**INTRO (0:10-0:30):**
- Brief context (why this matters NOW)
- Credibility line (why I'm qualified to talk about this)
- Video roadmap: "I'll cover [3 things] — and #3 is the one most people get wrong"
- [B-ROLL: relevant establishing shot]

**MAIN CONTENT (0:30-[END-2:00]):**
Organize into 5-7 key sections. For EACH section:
- Section headline (what appears as a chapter)
- Opening hook for the section (mini pattern interrupt)
- Core teaching point with a specific example or story
- [B-ROLL SUGGESTION] in brackets
- Transition to next section

**ENGAGEMENT PROMPTS** — naturally insert 2-3 throughout (NOT forced):
- "If you're finding this useful, smash that like button" (place after a strong insight)
- "Drop a comment: what's YOUR experience with [topic]?" (place after a relatable point)
- Subscribe reminder (after delivering the biggest value moment)

**OUTRO (last 60 seconds):**
- Quick summary: 3 key takeaways
- Strong CTA: "Watch this video next → [RELATED TOPIC]"
- End screen suggestion (which video to link)

INCLUDE:
- Estimated timestamps for chapter markers

title: YouTube Shorts / TikTok / Reels Script
prompt:
Act as a short-form video strategist who creates content with 90%+ retention rates. You understand that every second counts — viewers decide to stay or scroll in under 2 seconds.

Write 5 short-form video scripts (30-60 seconds each) about [TOPIC] for [PLATFORM: TikTok / Reels / Shorts].

STYLE: [talking head / voiceover + footage / text-on-screen with music]
AUDIENCE: [DESCRIBE TARGET VIEWER]

FOR EACH SCRIPT:

**HOOK (0-2 seconds):**
- [TEXT OVERLAY]: Bold text that appears on screen
- [SPOKEN]: First words out of your mouth
- Must create instant curiosity, shock, or "wait, what?"
- Do NOT start with "Hey guys" or "In this video"

**BODY (3-45 seconds):**
- Deliver ONE clear insight (not three, not five — ONE)
- Use either: story arc / myth-busting / step-by-step/ before-after
- Include [VISUAL CUE] directions: zoom, cut, text pop
- Pacing: vary between fast and slow moments (monotone = scroll)

**TWIST (35-50 seconds):**
- Unexpected element that rewards viewers who stayed
- "But here's the part nobody mentions..."

**CTA (50-60 seconds):**
- [TEXT OVERLAY]: "Follow for more [TOPIC]" or "Save this"
- [SPOKEN]: Natural, not forced — tie it to the content

ALSO PROVIDE FOR EACH SCRIPT:
- Trending audio suggestion (describe the type/mood)
- 3 caption options (with hashtags)
- Best posting time recommendation

CONSTRAINT: If the viewer could scroll away at any point and not feel like they're missing something, the script needs a rewrite. Design for ZERO dead seconds.

title: Podcast Episode Outline
prompt:
Act as a podcast producer who has produced top-100 shows on Apple Podcasts. You design episodes that listeners complete AND share.

Create a detailed podcast episode outline for:
- Episode title: "[EPISODE TITLE]"
- Show name: [PODCAST NAME]
- Niche: [TOPIC/INDUSTRY]
- Format: [solo / interview / co-hosted]

TARGET LENGTH: [30 / 45 / 60] minutes

EPISODE STRUCTURE:

1. **Cold Open / Teaser (30-60 seconds)**
   - Pull the most compelling quote or insight from the episode
   - "On today's episode: [preview the key takeaway]"
   - This should make someone who's about to skip... press play

2. **Introduction (2-3 minutes)**
   - Context: Why this topic, why now
   - What the listener will walk away with (specific promise)
   - If interview: guest intro (credibility, not biography)

3. **Main Segments (3-4 segments, 8-12 min each)**

   - 3-4 talking points with specific angles
   - Transition phrase to next segment
   - "Golden quote" moment to aim for (the clip-worthy insight)

4. **If Interview — 5 powerful questions:**
   - 1 ice-breaker that reveals personality
   - 2 deep questions no one else has asked them
   - 1 "spicy" question they'll want to answer
   - 1 closer: "What's the one thing you'd tell someone who…"

5. **Listener Engagement Segment (3-5 min)**
   - Listener Q&A prompt or submitted question
   - "Call to action" moment: review, share, submit a question

6. **Sponsor Read Placement:** Mark 2 optimal positions (mid-roll)

7. **Outro (2-3 min)**
   - Recap the #1 takeaway
   - CTA: subscribe, leave a review
   - Next episode teaser (create anticipation)

CONSTRAINT: Do NOT create a lecture outline. Create a CONVERSATION outline — even solo episodes should feel like a dialogue with the listener.

title: Email Welcome Sequence
prompt:
Act as an email marketing strategist who designs welcome sequences that achieve 60%+ open rates and generate revenue by Day 7.

Write a 5-email welcome sequence for new subscribers to [BRAND/PRODUCT].

CONTEXT:
- Lead magnet / signup incentive: [WHAT THEY SIGNED UP FOR]
- Product/service to eventually sell: [PRODUCT] at $[PRICE]
- Brand voice: [DESCRIBE TONE]
- Target subscriber: [WHO ARE THEY, WHAT DO THEY WANT]

SEQUENCE:

**Email 1 — Welcome (Day 0, send immediately)**
- Subject line: 3 options
- Deliver the lead magnet (link + instructions)
- Set expectations: what they'll get, how often
- One sentence about who you are (credibility, not autobiography)
- Quick win: one micro-tip they can use RIGHT NOW
- CTA: "Reply and tell me: what's your biggest struggle with [TOPIC]?"

**Email 2 — Connection (Day 1)**
- Subject line: 3 options
- Your origin story — WHY you care about this topic
- Make it vulnerable and specific (not generic hero's journey)
- Bridge: how your experience connects to THEIR situation
- CTA: read your most popular blog post / resource

**Email 3 — Massive Value (Day 3)**
- Subject line: 3 options
- Your single best piece of advice on [TOPIC]
- Make it so good they'd feel guilty NOT paying for it
- Framework, template, or step-by-step they can implement today
- CTA: share this email with someone who needs it

**Email 4 — Social Proof (Day 5)**
- Subject line: 3 options
- Case study or testimonial (specific results with numbers)
- "Before → After" storytelling structure
- Subtle seed: mention your product as part of the transformation
- CTA: read the full case study

**Email 5 — Soft Pitch (Day 7)**
- Subject line: 3 options
- Bridge from the value you've given → the problem your product solves
- Introduce [PRODUCT] naturally (not a hard sell)
- 3 bullet points: what it includes, who it's for, key outcome
- Risk reversal: money-back guarantee or free trial
- CTA: "Learn more" or "Get started" (link to sales page)
- P.S. line with urgency or bonus offer

FOR EACH EMAIL: subject line (3 options), preview text, full body (150-250 words), one CTA.

CONSTRAINT: No email should be longer than 250 words. Respect inbox fatigue. Every sentence must earn its place.

title: Cold Email Template
prompt:
Act as a B2B sales copywriter who achieves 25%+ open rates and 5%+ reply rates on cold outreach.

Write 3 cold email variations to [TARGET: e.g., marketing directors at SaaS companies with 50-200 employees].

GOAL: [book a discovery call / get a reply / pitch a service]
MY OFFER: [DESCRIBE WHAT YOU SELL/DO]
KEY RESULT: [SPECIFIC OUTCOME YOU DELIVER, e.g., "increase demo bookings by 30%"]

VARIATION 1 — "The Quick Question" (Curiosity-Based)
- Subject line: short, intriguing, no salesy words
- Body: 3-4 sentences MAX
- Opens with a relevant question about their business
- Hints at a result without revealing the full method
- CTA: soft ask — "Worth a quick chat?"

VARIATION 2 — "The Case Study" (Proof-Based)
- Subject line: references a specific result
- Body: 4-5 sentences
- Leads with: "I helped [SIMILAR COMPANY] achieve [SPECIFIC RESULT] in [TIMEFRAME]"
- One sentence on how
- CTA: "Would this be relevant for [THEIR COMPANY]?"

VARIATION 3 — "The Value-First" (Give Before Ask)
- Subject line: offers something useful
- Body: 4-5 sentences
- Opens with a specific observation about THEIR business (shows research)
- Offers one free, specific insight or recommendation
- CTA: "I have 2 more ideas — happy to share if you're interested"

FOR EACH VARIATION ALSO WRITE:
- Follow-up email (sent 3 days later if no reply) — max 2 sentences
- Breakup email (sent 7 days later) — acknowledge you're moving on, leave the door open

CONSTRAINTS:
- Each email: UNDER 100 words (shorter = higher reply rates)
- Include personalization placeholders: [FIRST NAME], [COMPANY], [SPECIFIC DETAIL]
- Do NOT use: "I hope this finds you well," "Just following up," "Checking in"
- Do NOT open with your name or company — lead with THEIR problem
- No attachments, no images, no HTML — plain text only

title: Landing Page Copy
prompt:
Act as a direct-response copywriter trained in the frameworks of David Ogilvy, Gary Halbert, and Joanna Wiebe. You write copy that converts visitors into buyers.

Write complete landing page copy for: [PRODUCT/SERVICE]

INPUTS:
- Price: $[PRICE]
- Target audience: [SPECIFIC DESCRIPTION — who, what they struggle with, what they want]
- USP: [WHAT MAKES THIS DIFFERENT FROM ALTERNATIVES]
- Tone: [confident but not pushy / urgent and energetic / warm and trustworthy]

SECTIONS:

1. **HERO SECTION:**
   - Pre-headline: Call out the audience (e.g., "For [ROLE] who [STRUGGLE]")
   - Headline: Big benefit + specificity (numbers, timeframe, or outcome)
   - Subheadline: Expand on the HOW (mechanism or unique approach)
   - CTA button text: Action-oriented (not "Submit" — something like "Get Instant Access")
   - Social proof micro-line: "[X]+ customers" or "★★★★★ rated"

2. **PROBLEM SECTION:**
   - 3 specific pain points written in the customer's own language
   - Use "you" language — this section should feel like reading their diary
   - Agitate: show the cost of NOT solving this problem

3. **SOLUTION SECTION:**
   - Transition: "What if there was a way to [desired outcome]?"
   - Introduce the product as the bridge from pain to desire
   - 1-2 sentences on HOW it works (the mechanism)

4. **FEATURES → BENEFITS** (5-6 items):
   Format: "Feature Name — [what it is] so that [what it means for them]"
   Each feature must answer: "So what? Why should I care?"

5. **SOCIAL PROOF:**
   - 3 testimonial templates with [PLACEHOLDERS]: name, role, specific result, quote
   - Format: "[Result achieved] — here's the quote about the experience"

6. **FAQ** (5 questions):
   - Each question is a disguised objection
   - Each answer reframes the objection into a buying reason
   - Include: pricing justification, guarantee, comparison, timeline, support

7. **FINAL CTA SECTION:**
   - Stack the value: list everything they get with individual values
   - Total value: $[HIGH NUMBER]
   - Actual price: $[PRICE] (show the gap)
   - Guarantee: specific, generous, no-questions-asked
   - Urgency element: limited-time price, bonus, or scarcity
   - CTA button + reassurance line beneath it

CONSTRAINTS:
- Do NOT use superlatives without proof: "the best" → "rated #1 by [SOURCE]"
- Do NOT write paragraphs longer than 3 lines on mobile
- Every section should be scannable — headers, bullets, bold key phrases
- Write for someone who SKIMS first, then reads

title: Value Proposition Generator
prompt:
Act as a brand strategist who has developed value propositions for startups that went on to raise $10M+ in funding. You know that a great value prop is the foundation of ALL marketing.

Help me craft a compelling value proposition for [PRODUCT/SERVICE].

GUIDE ME THROUGH THIS FRAMEWORK:

**Step 1 — Customer Clarity:**
- Who is this for? (Be hyper-specific — one person, not a market segment)
- What's their #1 frustration with current solutions?
- In their own words, how would they describe their problem?

**Step 2 — Solution Clarity:**
- How does your product solve it? (mechanism, not features)
- What's the specific, measurable outcome?
- How long does it take to see that outcome?

**Step 3 — Differentiation:**
- Why this over the 3 closest alternatives?
- What's the ONE thing competitors literally cannot offer?
- What would a customer say if asked "Why did you choose [product]?"

**THEN SYNTHESIZE INTO:**

1. **One-line value prop** (under 10 words):
   "[OUTCOME] for [AUDIENCE] without [PAIN]"
   — 3 variations

2. **One-paragraph value prop** (3 sentences):
   Sentence 1: Who it's for + the problem
   Sentence 2: What it does + how it's different
   Sentence 3: The specific result
   — 3 variations

3. **Landing page headline + subheadline combo:**
   Headline: Big promise (emotional)
   Subheadline: How it works (logical)
   — 3 variations

4. **Elevator pitch** (30 seconds spoken):
   "You know how [AUDIENCE] struggle with [PROBLEM]? We [SOLUTION] so they can [OUTCOME]. Unlike [ALTERNATIVES], we [DIFFERENTIATOR]."

Rate each variation 1-10 for clarity and persuasiveness. Recommend the winner.

CONSTRAINT: Do NOT use jargon, buzzwords, or abstract language. If a 12-year-old can't understand it, it's not clear enough.

title: Competitor Content Analysis
prompt:
Act as a competitive intelligence analyst specializing in content marketing. You find the gaps that become goldmines.

Analyze the content strategy of my top competitors and find opportunities.

MY BUSINESS: [DESCRIBE YOUR BUSINESS/NICHE]
COMPETITORS: [COMPETITOR 1], [COMPETITOR 2], [COMPETITOR 3]

ANALYSIS FRAMEWORK:

1. **Content Audit per Competitor:**
   - What topics do they ALL cover? (saturated — avoid or differentiate)
   - What's their primary content format? (blog, video, podcast, social)
   - What's their posting frequency?
   - What's their content quality like? (surface-level or deep?)
   - What's their distribution strategy?

2. **Gap Analysis — The Gold:**
   - What topics are UNDERSERVED that I could own?
   - What content FORMATS are they NOT using that I could leverage?
   - What AUDIENCE SEGMENTS are they ignoring?
   - What ANGLES are missing? (contrarian takes, beginner content, advanced content)
   Rank gaps by: opportunity size × ease of execution

3. **Tone & Positioning Analysis:**
   - What tone/angle could differentiate me from all of them?
   - What would make someone choose my content over theirs?

4. **10 Content Pieces to Create:**
   Specific titles/concepts that NONE of my competitors have done well, with:
   - Format recommendation
   - Estimated search volume/demand
   - Why this would win

5. **90-Day Content Plan:**
   Week-by-week plan to establish authority in the identified gaps.
   - Month 1: Foundation (pillar content in gap areas)
   - Month 2: Depth (supporting content and backlink-worthy pieces)
   - Month 3: Distribution (promotion and amplification)

CONSTRAINT: Base analysis on what competitors actually do, not what you think they might do. Be specific — cite actual content types and topics, not vague observations.

title: Press Release Writer
prompt:
Act as a PR communications specialist who writes press releases that get picked up by journalists — not ignored.

Write a professional press release for [COMPANY] announcing [NEWS: product launch / funding / partnership / milestone / hire / event].

INPUTS:
- Company: [NAME + one-line description]
- News: [WHAT'S BEING ANNOUNCED]
- Spokesperson: [NAME, TITLE]
- Key stat/proof point: [NUMBER OR FACT THAT MAKES THIS NEWSWORTHY]
- Target media: [INDUSTRY PUBLICATIONS / MAINSTREAM / LOCAL]

PRESS RELEASE STRUCTURE:

1. **Headline:** Newsworthy, specific, active voice. Not a marketing slogan.
   Bad: "Acme Corp Launches Amazing New Product"
   Good: "Acme Corp's New AI Tool Cuts Content Production Time by 73%"

2. **Subheadline:** One sentence that adds context or a secondary angle

3. **Dateline:** [CITY, STATE — Month Day, Year]

4. **Paragraph 1 (The Lead):**
   - Answer: WHO, WHAT, WHEN, WHERE, WHY in the first 2 sentences
   - Include the most important fact/number
   - Must be compelling enough to stand alone — many journalists only read P1

5. **Paragraph 2-3 (Supporting Details):**
   - Expand on the significance, context, and how it works
   - Include a specific data point, customer impact, or market context
   - Why this matters to the TARGET PUBLICATION's readers

6. **Quote from [SPOKESPERSON]:**
   - Sound like a human, not a marketing brochure
   - Include a forward-looking vision or customer-centric statement
   - 2-3 sentences max

7. **Details & Availability:**
   - When it launches, how to access it, pricing if applicable
   - Where to learn more

8. **Boilerplate — "About [COMPANY]":**
   - 3-4 sentences: what they do, key stats, founded when, mission
   - Website, social links

9. **Media Contact:**
   - [NAME], [TITLE], [EMAIL], [PHONE]

LENGTH: 400-500 words total
STYLE: AP Style guidelines. No exclamation marks. No marketing superlatives.

CONSTRAINT: Every sentence must pass the journalist test: "Would I care about this if I weren't involved with this company?" If no — rewrite it.

title: Case Study Template
prompt:
Act as a B2B content marketer who writes case studies that sales teams actually use to close deals. Your case studies generate pipeline, not just pageviews.

Write a compelling case study for [COMPANY/PRODUCT] featuring the Results of [CLIENT/USER].

INPUTS:
- Client industry: [INDUSTRY]
- Client size: [EMPLOYEES / REVENUE / STAGE]
- Problem they faced: [DESCRIBE]
- Solution used: [YOUR PRODUCT/FEATURES]
- Key result: [SPECIFIC METRIC IMPROVEMENT]
- Timeframe: [HOW LONG TO ACHIEVE THE RESULT]

CASE STUDY STRUCTURE:

1. **Title:** "[Result achieved] — How [Client] Used [Product] to [Outcome]"
   Format: Number + outcome + mechanism
   Example: "312% More Qualified Leads: How Acme Used [Product] to Transform Their Pipeline"

2. **Snapshot Box (scannable overview):**
   | Company | Industry | Challenge | Solution | Result | Timeframe |

3. **The Challenge (200 words):**
   - Specific problem with NUMBERS (not vague "they struggled")
   - What was at stake if they didn't solve it?
   - What had they tried before? Why didn't it work?
   - Emotional context: how the team felt about the problem

4. **The Search (100 words):**
   - What criteria did they use to evaluate solutions?
   - What alternatives did they consider?
   - Why did they choose [YOUR PRODUCT]? (in their words)

5. **The Solution (200 words):**
   - Specifically HOW they implemented [your product]
   - Which features they used and why
   - Implementation timeline and effort
   - Any unexpected benefits discovered during rollout

6. **The Results (200 words):**
   - 3-5 quantifiable outcomes with exact numbers:
     ✅ "[X]% increase in [METRIC]"
     ✅ "$[X] saved per [timeframe]"
     ✅ "[X] hours saved per week"
   - Before → After comparison
   - Quote from the client about the impact

7. **Client Quote / Testimonial:**
   Template: "[The specific result]. [What surprised them]. [What they'd tell others considering this solution]."
   — [Name], [Title], [Company]

8. **Key Takeaways (3 bullets):**
   What readers in similar situations should learn from this story

ALSO PROVIDE:
- Data visualization suggestions (which numbers to chart)
- Suggested distribution: email, sales deck, blog, social

CONSTRAINT: No fluff, no corporate speak. Every sentence should contain a fact, a number, or an emotion. If you can remove a sentence and nothing is lost — remove it.
useCaseEn: High-value utility for Marketing/Redes Sociales tasks.
useCaseEs: Utilidad de alto valor para tareas de Marketing/Redes Sociales.

title: Whitepaper / Long-Form Report Writer
prompt:
Act as a senior research writer who produces whitepapers for B2B companies that generate $500K+ in attributed pipeline revenue.

Write a comprehensive whitepaper / long-form report on [TOPIC].

INPUTS:
- Industry: [YOUR INDUSTRY]
- Target reader: [C-SUITE / VP / MANAGER / PRACTITIONER]
- Goal: [thought leadership / lead generation / sales enablement]
- Word count: ~3,000-5,000 words
- Brand/company: [YOUR COMPANY NAME]

WHITEPAPER STRUCTURE:

1. **Title Page:** "[Title that promises specific insight] — A [COMPANY] Report"
   - 3 title options with a data-driven or outcome-driven hook

2. **Executive Summary (250 words):**
   - Key finding in one sentence
   - Why it matters to the reader's business
   - 3 main takeaways
   - Must stand alone — many readers only read this section

3. **Introduction (300 words):**
   - Industry context and why this topic matters NOW
   - Scope: what this report covers and doesn't cover
   - Methodology (even if AI-assisted — state your research approach)

4. **Core Sections (3-5 chapters, 500-800 words each):**
   FOR EACH CHAPTER:
   - Chapter title (insight-driven, not generic)
   - Key finding or argument
   - Supporting data points (cite real or realistic statistics)
   - Real-world example or case study
   - Visual suggestion: chart, diagram, or infographic concept
   - Pull quote (highlight-worthy sentence for design)

5. **Key Findings Summary (300 words):**
   - 5-7 numbered findings, each in one sentence
   - Implications for the reader's strategy or operations

6. **Recommendations (300 words):**
   - 3-5 actionable recommendations
   - Organize by: quick wins vs. strategic investments
   - Each recommendation: what to do, why, and expected impact

7. **Conclusion (150 words):**
   - Forward-looking perspective
   - CTA: next step the reader should take

8. **About [COMPANY]:**
   - 100-word description positioning your company as the expert on this topic

CONSTRAINTS:
- Write for executives who have 10 minutes, not academics who have 10 hours
- Use data to support every major claim — no unsupported assertions
- Break up text: use headers, pull quotes, bullet lists, and visual placeholders
- Do NOT write like a blog post — this should feel like institutional-grade research

title: Content Localization Guide
prompt:
Act as a content localization strategist who adapts content for global markets while maintaining brand voice and cultural sensitivity.

Adapt the following content for [TARGET MARKET/LANGUAGE]:

ORIGINAL CONTENT:
[PASTE CONTENT OR DESCRIBE WHAT NEEDS ADAPTING]

LOCALIZATION INPUTS:
- Source market: [US / UK / OTHER]
- Target market: [SPECIFIC COUNTRY/REGION]
- Target language: [LANGUAGE + DIALECT if relevant]
- Brand voice: [DESCRIBE — should tone shift for this market?]
- Cultural sensitivity level: [low / medium / high]

DELIVER:

1. **Cultural Audit of Original:**
   - Identify cultural references that won't translate (idioms, humor, examples)
   - Flag potentially offensive or irrelevant content
   - Note measurement, currency, and format differences (date, time, address)

2. **Localized Version:**
   - Fully adapted content (not just translated — LOCALIZED)
   - Replace examples with locally relevant ones
   - Adjust tone if the target culture expects more/less formality
   - Adapt humor style (or remove humor that doesn't cross borders)

3. **Localization Decisions Log:**
   | Original Element | Issue | Localized Replacement | Reasoning |

4. **SEO Adaptation:**
   - Target keywords in [LANGUAGE] (don't translate English keywords literally)
   - Meta title and description for local search behavior
   - Local link-building opportunities

5. **Visual/Design Notes:**
   - Any image or design changes needed (gestures, symbols, colors with different meanings)
   - Text expansion: [LANGUAGE] typically runs [X%] longer/shorter than English

CONSTRAINT: Translation ≠ localization. A word-for-word translation is a failure. The localized version should feel like it was WRITTEN for that market — not translated from another.
useCaseEn: High-value utility for Marketing/Redes Sociales tasks.
useCaseEs: Utilidad de alto valor para tareas de Marketing/Redes Sociales.

title: Thought Leadership Article
prompt:
Act as a ghostwriter for Fortune 500 executives. You write articles that get published in Harvard Business Review, Fast Company, and Forbes.

Write a thought leadership article for [PERSON/ROLE] in [INDUSTRY] about [TOPIC].

INPUTS:
- Author: [NAME], [TITLE] at [COMPANY]
- Target publication: [HBR / Forbes / LinkedIn / industry blog]
- Audience: [PEER EXECUTIVES / INDUSTRY PROFESSIONALS / GENERAL BUSINESS]
- Key argument: [THE MAIN POINT OR THESIS]
- Author's unique perspective: [WHAT MAKES THEIR VIEW DIFFERENT]

ARTICLE STRUCTURE (800-1,200 words):

1. **Opening (100 words):** Start with ONE of:
   - A counterintuitive observation from direct experience
   - A trend everyone is misinterpreting
   - A specific moment or event that crystallized an insight
   Do NOT start with "In today's rapidly changing..." or any generic opener.

2. **The Argument (400-500 words):**
   - State the thesis clearly in one sentence
   - Support with 2-3 evidence types: personal experience, data, case study
   - Address the strongest counter-argument (shows intellectual honesty)
   - Use concrete examples — name specific companies, technologies, or events

3. **The Implications (200-300 words):**
   - "This means that..." — what should leaders/readers DO differently?
   - 3 specific, actionable takeaways
   - At least one recommendation that is bold or uncomfortable

4. **Closing (100 words):**
   - Circle back to the opening
   - Leave the reader with a question or challenge that lingers

CONSTRAINTS:
- Write in the author's authentic voice — this should sound like THEM, not an AI
- No platitudes or motivational generalities ("The future belongs to those who adapt")
- Every paragraph must contain either: a specific fact, a personal observation, or a concrete example
- Do NOT include a bio paragraph — the byline handles that
- Academic papers cite; thought leadership SHOWS through stories and specific insights

title: Webinar / Live Event Script
prompt:
Act as an event content strategist who designs webinars that maintain 80%+ attendance through the end and convert 15%+ of attendees into customers.

Write a complete webinar script for "[WEBINAR TITLE]."

INPUTS:
- Topic: [TOPIC]
- Duration: [45 / 60 / 90] minutes
- Format: [solo presentation / interview / panel / demo]
- Goal: [educate / sell / launch / build authority]
- Product to promote (if any): [PRODUCT] at $[PRICE]
- Audience: [WHO WILL ATTEND]

WEBINAR FLOW:

**Pre-Webinar (5 min before start):**
- Countdown slide content
- Welcome chat messages to warm up attendees
- Quick poll to engage early joiners

**Opening (0:00-5:00):**
- Hook: Bold promise of what attendees will learn (specific outcome)
- Housekeeping: how long, Q&A process, recording availability
- Credibility: 2-sentence background (results-focused, not autobiography)
- Agenda preview: "Here's what we'll cover..." (create anticipation for each section)

**Main Content (5:00-35:00):**
3-4 teaching segments, each following this pattern:
- Segment headline
- Key insight with supporting story, data, or demo
- Interactive moment: poll, chat question, or live example
- Transition to next segment

**The Pivot (35:00-40:00):** — Only if selling
- Bridge from teaching to product: "Now, everything I just showed you can be done manually... or you can [PRODUCT]"
- Frame as a tool that accelerates what they just learned
- NOT a hard sell — a logical next step

**Q&A (40:00-50:00):**
- Pre-seed 3 questions that address common objections
- Handle live questions with succinct, value-driven answers

**Closing & CTA (50:00-55:00):**
- Recap top 3 takeaways
- Special offer (if applicable): limited-time pricing, bonus for attendees
- Exact CTA: "Go to [URL] right now and [ACTION]"
- Urgency: "This offer expires at midnight tonight / in 48 hours"

ALSO INCLUDE:
- Slide content suggestions (title + bullet points per slide)
- Chat engagement prompts (5 moments to ask the audience something)
- Registration page copy: headline + 3 bullet points + urgency element

CONSTRAINT: No slide should have more than 6 words of text. Webinar slides support the speaker — they don't replace them.

title: Brand Storytelling Framework
prompt:
Act as a brand narrative strategist who builds stories that make companies iconic — not just recognized, but FELT.

Create a comprehensive brand storytelling framework for [BRAND/BUSINESS].

INPUTS:
- Business: [WHAT YOU DO]
- Founder story: [BRIEF ORIGIN — why did you start this?]
- Target audience: [WHO YOU SERVE]
- Brand personality: [3-5 ADJECTIVES]
- Mission: [YOUR PURPOSE BEYOND MAKING MONEY]
- Key differentiator: [WHAT MAKES YOU DIFFERENT]

FRAMEWORK DELIVERABLES:

1. **Brand Origin Story (300-500 words):**
   Using the Hero's Journey adapted for business:
   - The Ordinary World: What life was like before
   - The Call: The moment you realized something needed to change
   - The Challenge: What you faced (make it real — include failure and doubt)
   - The Transformation: What you built and why it matters
   - The Mission: What drives you forward every day
   Write this as a compelling narrative, not a resume.

2. **Brand Messaging Pillars (5):**
   For each: one-sentence message + supporting proof point + emotional appeal
   These are the 5 things you want people to KNOW and FEEL about your brand.

3. **Customer Hero Story Template:**
   - Before state: [PAIN/SITUATION]
   - Discovery: How they found you
   - Transformation: What changed
   - After state: [DESIRED OUTCOME]
   - Their words: Quote that captures the feeling
   Provide 3 variations for different customer types.

4. **Signature Story Collection (5 stories to tell repeatedly):**
   - The origin story (how it started)
   - The breakthrough story (first big win or customer success)
   - The values story (a moment where you chose principles over profit)
   - The vision story (where you're headed and why it matters)
   - The community story (how your customers changed something together)

5. **Tone of Voice Guide:**
   - 3 words your brand IS + 3 words your brand is NOT
   - Writing samples: how you'd write a social post, email subject line, error message, and apology
   - Vocabulary: 10 words to USE and 10 words to AVOID

CONSTRAINT: Brand stories must be TRUE. If the founder didn't overcome adversity, don't invent it. Authenticity converts; fabrication destroys trust.

title: Tutorial / How-To Guide Writer
prompt:
Act as a technical content writer who creates tutorials that readers can follow step-by-step and succeed on their first attempt — zero confusion, zero ambiguity.

Write a comprehensive tutorial: "How to [TASK/GOAL]"

INPUTS:
- Topic: [WHAT THE TUTORIAL TEACHES]
- Target reader: [WHO — skill level and context]
- Format: [blog post / documentation / video script / all three]
- Estimated completion time: [X MINUTES]
- Tools/software required: [LIST]

TUTORIAL STRUCTURE:

1. **Title:** "How to [Achieve X]: A Step-by-Step Guide [for AUDIENCE]"

2. **Introduction (100 words):**
   - What they'll be able to DO after completing this tutorial (specific outcome)
   - Prerequisites: what they need before starting (software, accounts, knowledge)
   - Estimated time to complete
   - Difficulty level: [Beginner / Intermediate / Advanced]

3. **Step-by-Step Instructions:**
   FOR EACH STEP:
   - Step number and clear title: "Step 3: Configure the API Connection"
   - What to do (exact instructions, not vague guidance)
   - WHY this step matters (context prevents confusion)
   - Expected result: "You should now see [X] on your screen"
   - [SCREENSHOT PLACEHOLDER: describe what the image should show]
   - ⚠️ Common mistake at this step + how to fix it
   - Code snippet or command (if applicable), with comments

4. **Troubleshooting Section:**
   | Problem | Likely Cause | Solution |
   List the 5 most common issues readers will face.

5. **Verification:**
   - How to confirm it worked correctly
   - What the final result should look like

6. **Next Steps:**
   - 2-3 related tutorials to continue learning
   - Advanced techniques to try after mastering the basics

CONSTRAINTS:
- NEVER say "simply" or "just" — what's simple to you is not simple to the reader
- NEVER skip a step — if the reader needs to click a button, SAY which button
- Include error states: "If you see [ERROR], do [FIX]"
- Test your instructions mentally — could someone with ZERO context follow them?

title: Guest Post Pitch
prompt:
Act as a successful freelance writer who consistently lands guest posts on high-authority publications (DA 50+) in competitive niches.

Write 3 guest post pitch emails for [MY NICHE/EXPERTISE] targeting different publication tiers.

INPUTS:
- My expertise/topic: [WHAT I WRITE ABOUT]
- My credentials: [RELEVANT EXPERIENCE OR CREDENTIALS]
- Published work links: [LIST 2-3 OR "building portfolio"]
- Goal: [backlink / authority / traffic / all three]

**PITCH 1 — Top-tier publication** (e.g., HBR, Forbes, Fast Company):
- Subject line: concise, not salesy
- Opening: 1 sentence showing you READ their publication (reference a specific recent article)
- The pitch: Article title + 3-sentence summary explaining the unique angle
- Why NOW: timely hook or trend that makes this topic urgent
- Credentials: 1-2 sentences, results-focused
- Closing: Professional, not desperate
- Total length: UNDER 150 words

**PITCH 2 — Mid-tier industry blog** (e.g., industry-specific, DA 30-60):
- Same structure, more casual tone
- Include 2-3 headline options (shows flexibility)
- Offer to customize the angle for their audience
- Mention a specific article of theirs you could build upon or complement

**PITCH 3 — Niche community blog or newsletter** (e.g., Substack, Medium publications):
- Conversational tone
- Focus on value to their readers (not your resume)
- Offer a specific, tactical article (step-by-step guides win at this tier)
- Include a brief outline (4-5 bullet points)

FOR EACH: Include a follow-up email template (sent 5-7 days later, max 2 sentences).

CONSTRAINTS:
- Do NOT open with "I hope this finds you well" or "My name is..."
- Do NOT pitch topics that have been covered recently by that publication
- Lead with the VALUE of your article, not your biography
- Keep the entire email visible without scrolling on a phone screen

title: Interactive Content Designer
prompt:
Act as a content marketing strategist who specializes in interactive content — the type that gets 2x more engagement and 4x more shares than static content.

Design an interactive content experience about [TOPIC] for [AUDIENCE].

INPUTS:
- Content goal: [lead generation / engagement / education / virality]
- Platform: [website / email / social media]
- Technical resources: [can build custom / need no-code tools / embed only]

CREATE 3 INTERACTIVE CONTENT CONCEPTS:

**Concept 1 — Quiz/Assessment:**
- Title: "[Discover your X type]" or "How well do you [topic]?"
- 8-12 questions with answer options
- 3-5 result categories (each with a description and personalized CTA)
- Lead capture point: "Enter your email to get your detailed results"
- Shareability hook: result image/badge they'll want to share

**Concept 2 — Calculator/Tool:**
- Describe the calculation or tool logic
- Input fields needed (3-5 max for ease)
- Output format: number, comparison, recommendation
- Personalization: how results change based on inputs
- CTA: what to do with their results

**Concept 3 — Interactive Infographic or Timeline:**
- Content structure: what unfolds as users interact
- Click/hover/scroll triggers
- Data points to reveal progressively
- Mobile adaptation strategy

FOR EACH CONCEPT INCLUDE:
- Estimated creation time and recommended tool (Typeform, Outgrow, Calconic, custom)
- Promotion strategy: how to drive traffic to it
- KPIs to measure success
- Expected conversion rate benchmark

CONSTRAINT: Interactive content must provide GENUINE value — not just gamify data collection. The user should feel smarter or more informed after completing it.

title: Ebook Chapter Writer
prompt:
Act as a published nonfiction author who writes chapters that people highlight, bookmark, and reference repeatedly.

Write a complete chapter for my ebook: "[EBOOK TITLE]"

INPUTS:
- Chapter title: [CHAPTER TITLE]
- Chapter position: [X of Y total chapters]
- Previous chapter summary: [WHAT THE READER JUST LEARNED]
- Next chapter preview: [WHAT'S COMING NEXT]
- Target reader: [WHO AND THEIR KNOWLEDGE LEVEL]
- Total chapter word count: ~2,000-3,000 words
- Voice/tone: [authoritative / conversational / academic / storytelling]

CHAPTER STRUCTURE:

1. **Opening Hook (100-150 words):**
   - Start with a story, surprising fact, or provocative question
   - Connect to what they learned in the previous chapter
   - Create a clear "promise" for this chapter

2. **Core Content (1,500-2,500 words):**
   - Organize into 3-5 subsections with clear headers
   - Each subsection: concept → example → implication → action
   - Include at least: 1 case study/story, 2 actionable frameworks, 3 specific tips
   - Use "you" language — this is a conversation, not a lecture

3. **Practical Exercise (200 words):**
   - A hands-on activity the reader can do right now
   - Clear instructions with expected outcomes
   - Connects theory to their specific situation

4. **Key Takeaways Box:**
   - 3-5 bullet points summarizing the most important ideas
   - Each takeaway: actionable and specific, not vague

5. **Chapter Transition (50 words):**
   - Bridge to the next chapter
   - Create curiosity: "Now that you know [this], the next question is [that]..."

CONSTRAINTS:
- Do NOT pad with filler — every paragraph must teach, prove, or inspire
- Do NOT repeat concepts from previous chapters without adding new depth
- Include specific numbers, names, and examples — vagueness kills credibility
- Read-level: aim for Flesch-Kincaid Grade 8-10 (clear and accessible, never dumbed down)

title: Annual/Quarterly Report Content
prompt:
Act as a corporate communications writer who creates investor and stakeholder reports that are clear, compelling, and confidence-building.

Write content for [COMPANY]'s [ANNUAL / QUARTERLY] report.

INPUTS:
- Reporting period: [DATES]
- Company: [NAME + brief description]
- Audience: [investors / board / employees / public]
- Key metrics: [LIST 5-8 KEY METRICS WITH NUMBERS]
- Major accomplishments: [LIST 3-5]
- Challenges faced: [LIST 2-3 — be honest]
- Forward outlook: [KEY PRIORITIES FOR NEXT PERIOD]

SECTIONS:

1. **CEO/Founder Letter (400-500 words):**
   - Opening: the ONE achievement or milestone that defines this period
   - Context: market conditions and how the company navigated them
   - Performance highlights: 3-5 metrics with narrative context
   - Honest acknowledgment: 1-2 challenges + how you're addressing them
   - Vision: where the company is headed and why it matters
   - Tone: confident but not arrogant, honest but not anxious

2. **Performance Dashboard:**
   | Metric | This Period | Last Period | % Change | Status |
   Include trend narratives for each metric (1-2 sentences each)

3. **Highlights Section (5 achievements):**
   For each: headline, 2-3 sentence description, supporting metric

4. **Strategic Outlook (200-300 words):**
   - 3-5 priorities for the next period
   - Each priority: what, why, expected impact
   - Market opportunities to capitalize on

5. **Financial Summary (if applicable):**
   - Revenue, expenses, profit/loss with YoY comparison
   - Narrative explaining the numbers (not just tables)

CONSTRAINTS:
- Be transparent about challenges — stakeholders respect honesty more than spin
- Every metric should have context (is 25% growth good or bad for this industry?)
- Do NOT use corporate jargon: "synergize," "leverage," "ecosystem"
- Keep paragraphs under 4 lines — executives are scanning, not reading

title: Course Marketing Copy
prompt:
Act as an online education marketer who has launched courses generating $500K+ in revenue. You know that selling a course is selling a transformation, not information.

Write complete marketing copy for an online course: "[COURSE TITLE]."

INPUTS:
- Course topic: [TOPIC]
- Target student: [WHO — be specific about their situation]
- Price: $[PRICE]
- Course length: [X hours / modules]
- Key transformation: [WHAT THEY'LL BE ABLE TO DO AFTER]
- Instructor credibility: [RELEVANT EXPERIENCE OR RESULTS]

DELIVER:

1. **Sales Page Copy:**
   - Pre-headline: "For [AUDIENCE] who [SITUATION]"
   - Headline: Promise the transformation, not the information
   - Sub-headline: How + timeline + differentiator
   - Hero section CTA button text

2. **Problem Agitation Section (200 words):**
   - 3 specific pain points in THEIR language
   - Cost of NOT learning this (money, time, career impact)
   - "Sound familiar?" moment of recognition

3. **Curriculum Overview:**
   FOR EACH MODULE: catchy name + "By the end, you'll be able to [OUTCOME]"

4. **Instructor Bio (100 words):**
   Results-focused, not resume-focused. What have you DONE, not where did you study.

5. **FAQ Objection Handlers (6 questions):**
   Each secretly addresses: too expensive, no time, already know this, will it work for me, can I get a refund, how is this different

6. **Social Proof Section:**
   - 3 testimonial templates (results-driven, not "great course!")
   - Student transformation stats: "Over [X] students enrolled, [Y]% completion rate"

7. **Pricing Section:**
   - Value stack (individual values totaling 5-10x the price)
   - Guarantee: specific, generous, risk-reversing
   - Urgency: limited enrollment, early bird, bonus deadline

8. **Launch Emails (3):**
   - Email 1: Story + announcement
   - Email 2: Curriculum reveal + testimonial
   - Email 3: Last chance + urgency

CONSTRAINT: Sell the OUTCOME, not the curriculum. Nobody wants "12 modules and 47 videos." They want "the confidence to [RESULT] within [TIMEFRAME]."

title: Microcopy & UX Writing
prompt:
Act as a UX writer at a top-tier product company (Stripe, Notion, or Apple level). You write interface copy that is invisible when it works and only noticed when it's wrong.

Write microcopy for [APP/PRODUCT/FEATURE].

INPUTS:
- Product type: [SaaS / e-commerce / mobile app / website]
- Brand voice: [DESCRIBE IN 3 ADJECTIVES]
- Feature/flow: [DESCRIBE WHAT THE USER IS DOING]

DELIVER ALL OF THE FOLLOWING:

1. **Empty States (5 screens):**
   - State: what the screen looks like with no data
   - Headline: empathetic, not generic ("No results found" → "We didn't find any matches — try adjusting your filters")
   - Body: helpful next step
   - CTA button: action-oriented

2. **Error Messages (5 types):**
   | Error Type | Bad Version | Good Version | Why It's Better |
   Types: form validation, payment failed, network error, permission denied, 404

3. **Success States (3):**
   - Celebratory but not overdone
   - Clear next step: what should they do now?

4. **Onboarding Copy (5 screens):**
   - Welcome, setup step, personalization, key feature highlight, completion
   - Each: headline (5-8 words) + body (1-2 sentences) + CTA button

5. **Button Labels (10):**
   | Generic Version | Better Version | Context |
   Transform weak CTAs into specific, action-driving labels

6. **Loading States (3):**
   - Creative alternatives to generic spinners
   - Messages that reduce perceived wait time

7. **Tooltip Copy (5):**
   - Complex feature explanations in under 20 words each

CONSTRAINTS:
- Every piece of copy must be under 25 words unless it's body text
- Do NOT use exclamation marks excessively (max 1 per screen)
- Do NOT blame the user for errors: "Invalid input" → "Please enter a valid email address"
- Test: read every piece of copy out loud — if it sounds robotic, rewrite it

title: Content Audit Analyzer
prompt:
Act as a content marketing analyst performing a strategic audit. You don't just catalog content — you find what's working, what's dying, and what gold is being left on the table.

Perform a content audit for [WEBSITE/BRAND].

AUDIT FRAMEWORK:

1. **Content Inventory:**
   | # | Title | Type | Date Published | Traffic | Engagement | Conversion | Status |
   Status: 🟢 Evergreen | 🟡 Needs refresh | 🔴 Underperforming | ⚫ Archive/delete

2. **Performance Analysis:**
   - Top 10 performing pieces: what makes them work?
   - Bottom 10 pieces: why are they failing?
   - Common traits of winners vs. losers
   - Seasonal patterns or trends in performance

3. **Content Gap Analysis:**
   - Topics my audience searches for that I haven't covered
   - Formats I'm NOT using that competitors are leveraging
   - Funnel gaps: where in the buyer journey am I losing people?

4. **Optimization Opportunities:**
   Rank by effort vs. impact:
   - Quick wins: existing pieces to update for instant traffic boost (title, meta, add sections)
   - Merge candidates: thin content to combine into comprehensive guides
   - Repurpose gems: high performers to turn into new formats
   - Kill list: content to remove or noindex (hurting more than helping)

5. **90-Day Action Plan:**
   - Month 1: Update and optimize (list specific pieces)
   - Month 2: Fill gaps (list specific new content to create)
   - Month 3: Amplify winners (promotion and distribution plan)

CONSTRAINT: Every recommendation must include the expected impact (estimated traffic gain, conversion lift, or efficiency improvement). "Update old posts" is not actionable — "Update [SPECIFIC POST] by adding [SPECIFIC SECTION] to target [SPECIFIC KEYWORD]" is.

title: Ghostwriting Voice Matcher
prompt:
Act as a professional ghostwriter who can replicate any person's writing style so precisely that their own team can't tell the difference.

I need you to learn and replicate the writing voice of [PERSON/BRAND].

VOICE INPUT — provide ONE of:
- Option A: [PASTE 3-5 SAMPLES OF THEIR WRITING — blog posts, tweets, emails]
- Option B: [DESCRIBE THEIR VOICE — adjectives, comparisons, examples]
- Option C: [PUBLIC FIGURE — name and context]

ANALYSIS — FIRST, deconstruct the voice:

1. **Vocabulary Profile:**
   - Favorite words and phrases they use repeatedly
   - Words they NEVER use
   - Jargon level: technical, accessible, or mixed
   - Sentence structure: short and punchy? Long and flowing? Mixed?

2. **Style Fingerprint:**
   - Average sentence length
   - Paragraph style: dense or airy?
   - Use of: metaphors, humor, data, stories, rhetorical questions
   - Formality spectrum: 1 (casual text) → 10 (academic paper)
   - Emotional range: warm/distant, confident/careful, provocative/safe

3. **Pattern Detection:**
   - How they open pieces (story? question? bold claim?)
   - How they close pieces (CTA? question? summary?)
   - Transition style between ideas
   - Signature moves: things only THEY do

THEN, demonstrate mastery:
Write 3 SHORT pieces in their voice:
- A 3-tweet thread on [TOPIC]
- A 150-word LinkedIn post on [TOPIC]
- An email newsletter intro on [TOPIC]

After each piece, include a [VOICE CHECK] section explaining which specific voice elements you matched.

CONSTRAINT: Matching a voice doesn't mean caricature. Capture the ESSENCE, not the quirks. If you exaggerate their tics, it reads like parody — not ghostwriting.

title: Content Scoring Rubric
prompt:
Act as a content quality director who reviews thousands of pieces annually. Your rubric has increased average content quality by 40% across teams.

Create a comprehensive content scoring rubric for [CONTENT TYPE: blog posts / social media / emails / videos / all].

RUBRIC DESIGN:

1. **Scoring Categories (weighted):**
   | Category | Weight | Score (1-5) | Criteria |
   Include at least these categories:
   - Strategic alignment (does it serve a business goal?)
   - Audience relevance (does it solve a real problem?)
   - Originality (does it add something new?)
   - Structure and readability
   - Writing quality (grammar, style, voice)
   - SEO optimization (if applicable)
   - Call to action effectiveness
   - Visual/formatting quality
   - Emotional impact / engagement potential

   - 1 = Needs complete rewrite (specific examples of what earns a 1)
   - 3 = Acceptable, meets minimum standards (specific examples)
   - 5 = Exceptional, sets the standard (specific examples)

3. **Quality Tiers:**
   - 90-100%: Publish immediately, showcase
   - 75-89%: Minor revisions, approve within 1 round
   - 60-74%: Major revisions needed, return to writer
   - Below 60%: Reject and reassign

4. **Self-Review Checklist:**
   15-item checklist writers complete before submitting (yes/no):
   Each item must be specific and actionable, not vague

5. **Editor Feedback Template:**
   Structured format for giving feedback that's clear, constructive, and efficient:
   - What works well (2-3 specific points)
   - What needs work (prioritized issues)
   - Suggested fixes (not just problems — solutions)

CONSTRAINT: The rubric must be usable in under 10 minutes per piece. If it takes longer, it won't be used. Prioritize the criteria that matter most for YOUR content type.

   - [ ] Preserved original URL (do NOT change the slug)
   - [ ] Updated internal links pointing to this article
   - [ ] Social re-promotion plan for the updated version

CONSTRAINT: Updating ≠ rewriting. Keep what works, fix what's broken, add what's missing. The goal is to make Google re-evaluate this page favorably — not to create a new article.

- [27-30s] CTA: Time-sensitive offer

title: Data-Driven Storytelling
prompt:
Act as a data journalist who transforms raw numbers into compelling narratives that people share, remember, and act upon.

Help me turn this data into a story:

DATA: [PROVIDE YOUR DATA: statistics, survey results, internal metrics, research findings, or describe what data you have]

DELIVER:

1. **Story Angle Discovery:**
   - What's the MOST surprising finding in this data?
   - What's the finding that challenges conventional wisdom?
   - What's the finding with the most practical implications?
   - Pick the strongest angle and explain why it's the hook

2. **Narrative Structure:**
   - **Lead:** The one sentence that makes people stop and pay attention
   - **Context:** Why should the reader care? What's the scale of impact?
   - **Evidence:** Present data in order of persuasiveness, not chronologically
   - **Human Element:** How does this data affect real people? (story, example, quote)
   - **Implication:** "This means that..." — what should change as a result?
   - **CTA:** What should the reader do with this information?

3. **Data Visualization Recommendations:**
   For each key finding:
   - Best chart type (and why alternatives are worse)
   - Title for the chart (insight-driven, not label-driven)
   - Annotation suggestions (callouts that guide interpretation)
   - Color strategy (what to emphasize, what to mute)

4. **Multiple Format Outputs:**
   - Blog post version (500 words)
   - Social media version (Twitter thread structure)
   - Executive summary version (3 bullets)
   - Infographic outline (visual flow)

5. **Headline Options (5):**
   Each using a different data storytelling formula:
   - The counterintuitive: "Despite [ASSUMPTION], [DATA] shows..."
   - The trend: "[X] has [increased/decreased] by [Y]% — here's why"
   - The comparison: "[A] vs [B]: [DATA] reveals the winner"
   - The prediction: "If [TREND] continues, [CONSEQUENCE] by [DATE]"
   - The human impact: "[NUMBER] people [AFFECTED] — and the number is [GROWING]"

CONSTRAINT: Data tells; stories sell. Never present numbers without context. "Revenue grew 23%" means nothing. "Revenue grew 23% — 3x the industry average — despite cutting ad spend by half" is a story.

title: AI-Assisted Content Workflow Designer
prompt:
Act as a content operations architect who designs AI-human hybrid workflows that produce twice the content at twice the quality in half the time.

Design a complete content production workflow for [TEAM SIZE: solo / small team / department] creating [CONTENT TYPES: blog, social, email, video, podcast, all].

INPUTS:
- Monthly content volume target: [X PIECES]
- Current pain points: [DESCRIBE BOTTLENECKS]
- AI tools available: [ChatGPT / Claude / Midjourney / other]
- Human skills on team: [LIST ROLES AND STRENGTHS]
- Quality standard: [describe what "good content" looks like for you]

WORKFLOW DESIGN:

1. **Content Production Pipeline:**
   Map every stage from idea to published:
   | Stage | AI Task | Human Task | Tool | Time | Output |
   Stages: Ideation → Research → Brief → Draft → Edit → Design → Publish → Distribute

2. **AI Prompt Chains:**
   For each content type, create a prompt SEQUENCE (not just one prompt):
   - Prompt 1: Research and outline
   - Prompt 2: First draft from outline
   - Prompt 3: Self-critique and revision
   - Prompt 4: Platform adaptation
   Each prompt should reference the output of the previous one.

3. **Quality Control Checkpoints:**
   Define exactly WHERE humans intervene:
   - Fact-checking: what to verify before publishing
   - Voice check: does it sound like the brand, not like AI?
   - Value check: does this teach, prove, or persuade?
   - Legal/compliance check: any claims that need substantiation?

4. **Content Batching Strategy:**
   How to produce a week's content in one focused session:
   - Batching schedule: which days for which tasks
   - Template library: reusable formats that speed up production
   - Asset organization: file naming, storage, version control

5. **Efficiency Metrics:**
   | Metric | Before AI | After AI | Improvement |
   Track: time per piece, cost per piece, quality score, output volume

6. **Scaling Plan:**
   - When to add another writer vs. more AI automation
   - When to upgrade AI tools
   - When to create new content types

CONSTRAINT: AI is the accelerator, not the replacement. The workflow must produce content that is BETTER than what either AI or humans could do alone. If you can't tell if a human was involved, the workflow failed.
useCaseEn: High-value utility for Marketing/Redes Sociales tasks.
