title: Comparison Blog Post
prompt:
Act as a content strategist who writes comparison articles that rank for "[X] vs [Y]" keywords and convert readers into customers — by being genuinely helpful, not biased.

Write a comparison blog post: "[PRODUCT A] vs [PRODUCT B] vs [PRODUCT C]."

INPUTS:
- Products to compare: [YOUR PRODUCT + 2 COMPETITORS]
- Target keyword: "[PRODUCT A] vs [PRODUCT B]"
- Search volume: [ESTIMATED]
- Target reader: [WHO IS SEARCHING THIS — their stage in the buying journey]
- Your key differentiator: [WHERE YOU WIN]
- Honest weakness: [WHERE A COMPETITOR IS GENUINELY BETTER]

ARTICLE STRUCTURE:

1. **SEO Metadata:**
   - Title: "[PRODUCT A] vs [PRODUCT B] vs [PRODUCT C]: [YEAR] Comparison (Tested)"
   - Meta description (155 chars): compare key difference + invite to read
   - URL slug: product-a-vs-product-b-vs-product-c

2. **Introduction (200 words):**
   - Acknowledge the reader's decision: "Choosing between [X] and [Y] isn't easy"
   - State what this article covers
   - Quick verdict: "In short, [PRODUCT A] is best for [X], [PRODUCT B] for [Y]"
   - Disclose if you're affiliated with any product

3. **Quick Comparison Table:**
   | Feature | [PRODUCT A] | [PRODUCT B] | [PRODUCT C] |
   - Key features, pricing, best for, rating

4. **Detailed Sections (per product):**
   For each: overview, pros, cons, pricing, best for, screenshot

5. **Head-to-Head Comparisons:**
   - [A] vs [B] for [USE CASE 1]
   - [A] vs [C] for [USE CASE 2]
   - [B] vs [C] for [USE CASE 3]

6. **Who Should Choose What (decision guide):**
   - Choose [A] if: [SPECIFIC SCENARIOS]
   - Choose [B] if: [SPECIFIC SCENARIOS]
   - Choose [C] if: [SPECIFIC SCENARIOS]

7. **FAQ Section (schema-ready):**
   5-7 questions people also search for

8. **CTA:**
   Subtle: "If [YOUR PRODUCT] sounds like the right fit, [TRY IT FREE]"

CONSTRAINTS:
- Be HONEST about competitors' strengths — readers check, and honesty builds trust
- Include REAL screenshots and pricing (date-stamped)
- Write for the READER, not for Google — helpful content ranks
- Update QUARTERLY — outdated comparisons lose trust and rankings

title: Free Trial Optimization Strategy
prompt:
Act as a SaaS growth strategist who optimizes the trial-to-paid conversion funnel — because getting signups is only half the battle.

Create a free trial optimization strategy for [PRODUCT].

INPUTS:
- Product: [SaaS PRODUCT DESCRIPTION]
- Trial type: [FREE TRIAL WITH CREDIT CARD / WITHOUT CREDIT CARD / FREEMIUM]
- Trial duration: [7 / 14 / 30 DAYS]
- Current trial-to-paid rate: [X%]
- Target rate: [Y%]
- Aha moment: [KEY ACTION THAT PREDICTS CONVERSION]
- Drop-off points: [WHERE TRIAL USERS LOSE INTEREST]

OPTIMIZATION STRATEGY:

1. **Trial Signup Optimization:**
   | Element | Current | Optimized | Expected Impact |
   - Form fields: minimize (name, email, password — nothing else)
   - Value proposition on signup page
   - Social proof near signup button
   - Credit card requirement: pros/cons analysis
   
2. **Activation Metrics:**
   | Milestone | Action | % Who Complete | Target |
   - Signup complete
   - Profile setup
   - First key action (aha moment)
   - Second session (Day 2 return)
   - Habit formation (3+ sessions in first week)

3. **Onboarding Flow:**
   - In-app guided tour (3-5 steps to aha moment)
   - Progress indicator: "You're 60% set up!"
   - Contextual tooltips (not all at once)
   - Empty state design: show value even before data

4. **Trial Email Sequence:**
   | Day | Subject | Goal | Segment |
   - Day 0: Welcome + first step
   - Day 1: "Did you try [KEY FEATURE]?"
   - Day 3: Case study + usage tip
   - Day 5: "You're halfway through your trial"
   - Day 7: Conversion pitch (if activated) / Re-engagement (if not)
   - Day 10: Urgency + offer
   - Day 13: "Trial expires tomorrow"
   - Day 14: "Your trial has ended" + special extension offer

5. **Conversion Triggers:**
   | Trigger | Action | Channel |
   - Reached aha moment → show upgrade prompt
   - Used 80% of free limits → "You're running out" notification
   - Invited team members → team plan pitch
   - 2 days before expiry → countdown + incentive
   - Trial expired without converting → win-back sequence

6. **Pricing Page Optimization:**
   - Plan comparison table
   - Recommended plan highlighted
   - Annual discount prominent
   - FAQ addressing conversion objections
   - Guarantee / risk reversal

7. **Win-Back Campaign (post-trial):**
   | Timing | Message | Offer |
   - Day 1 post-expiry: "We saved your data — come back"
   - Day 7: Extended trial offer (3 more days)
   - Day 14: Discount offer (20% first 3 months)
   - Day 30: "What went wrong?" survey
   - Day 90: "We've improved — check out what's new"

CONSTRAINTS:
- The trial must deliver REAL value — not a teaser that frustrates
- Do NOT gate essential features — show the product's full power
- Activation emails must be BEHAVIORAL, not just time-based
- Do NOT extend trials indefinitely — urgency drives conversion
- Track cohorts: which signup source converts best? Focus there

title: Account-Based Marketing Playbook
prompt:
Act as a B2B marketing strategist who runs ABM programs that generate 6-figure pipeline from named accounts — because in enterprise sales, quality beats quantity every time.

Design an ABM playbook for [COMPANY] targeting [TARGET ACCOUNTS].

INPUTS:
- Your company: [WHAT YOU SELL AND TO WHOM]
- Target account list: [NUMBER OF ACCOUNTS AND SELECTION CRITERIA]
- Average deal size: $[AMOUNT]
- Sales cycle: [LENGTH]
- Sales team size: [NUMBER OF REPS]
- Current pipeline: [EXISTING RELATIONSHIPS WITH TARGETS]
- ABM tools: [AVAILABLE TECH STACK]

ABM PLAYBOOK:

1. **Account Selection:**
   | Criterion | Weight | How to Score |
   - Company size / revenue
   - Industry fit
   - Technology stack alignment
   - Current pain points (public signals)
   - Existing relationships
   - Budget indicators
   
   Tier system:
   - Tier 1 (5-10 accounts): fully personalized, 1:1 campaigns
   - Tier 2 (25-50 accounts): segment-personalized, 1:few
   - Tier 3 (100+ accounts): industry-personalized, 1:many

2. **Account Research Template:**
   | Data Point | Source | How to Use |
   - Key decision makers + their LinkedIn activity
   - Recent news, funding, hiring (buying signals)
   - Technology used (competitive displacement opportunity)
   - Company priorities (earnings calls, press releases)
   - Content they engage with

3. **Multi-Channel Campaign (per tier):**
   | Channel | Tier 1 | Tier 2 | Tier 3 |
   - Personalized email sequences
   - LinkedIn engagement + InMail
   - Targeted ads (LinkedIn, display)
   - Direct mail (physical gifts/packages)
   - Custom content (industry reports, ROI calculators)
   - Events/dinners (exclusive)

4. **Content Strategy:**
   | Content | Personalization Level | Stage |
   - Industry report → awareness
   - ROI calculator customized to their metrics → consideration
   - Custom proposal / demo → decision
   - Peer reference (similar company case study) → validation

5. **Sales & Marketing Alignment:**
   - Shared account plans
   - Joint meeting cadence: weekly pipeline review
   - Handoff criteria: when does marketing pass to sales?
   - Shared metrics: pipeline generated, meetings booked, revenue won

6. **Measurement:**
   | Metric | Target | Tracking Method |
   - Account engagement score (multi-touch)
   - Meetings booked with target accounts
   - Pipeline $ from ABM accounts
   - Deal velocity: ABM vs. non-ABM accounts
   - Win rate: ABM vs. non-ABM
   - ROI: revenue / ABM program cost

CONSTRAINTS:
- ABM is NOT sending the same email to a list — it's PERSONALIZED outreach at scale
- Quality over quantity — 10 deeply engaged accounts > 1,000 cold emails
- Sales and marketing MUST be aligned — if sales doesn't use your research, ABM fails
- ABM takes TIME: 3-6 months to see pipeline impact — don't kill it after Month 1
- Every touchpoint must demonstrate that you UNDERSTAND their specific business

title: "Why Us" Page Writer
prompt:

Write a "Why Us" page for [BUSINESS].

- Top 3 differentiators: [WHAT MAKES YOU GENUINELY DIFFERENT]
- Competitors: [WHO THEY'RE COMPARING YOU TO]
- Social proof: [WHAT EVIDENCE YOU HAVE — numbers, testimonials, awards]
- Common concerns: [WHAT MAKES PROSPECTS HESITATE]

PAGE STRUCTURE:

1. **Headline:** "[OUTCOME] — Here's Why [X]+ [CUSTOMERS] Choose [BRAND]"

2. **Opening (position the decision):**
   "You have options. [LIST 2-3 ALTERNATIVES INCLUDING DOING NOTHING]. Here's what makes [BRAND] different — and why it might (or might not) be right for you."

3. **Differentiator Sections (3-5):**
   For each:
   | Differentiator | What Others Do | What We Do | Why It Matters |
   - Specific, verifiable claim
   - Supporting evidence (data, example, testimonial)
   - "This means you get [SPECIFIC BENEFIT]"

4. **The "We're Not For Everyone" Section:**
   "We're the right choice if: [3 SCENARIOS]"
   "We're NOT the right choice if: [2 SCENARIOS]"
   (This builds enormous trust and qualifies leads)

5. **Social Proof:**
   - Key metrics: "[X] customers, $[Y] saved, [Z]% satisfaction"
   - 3 testimonials: diverse customers, specific results
   - Trust badges, certifications, awards
   - Media mentions or partnerships

6. **Risk Reversal:**
   - Guarantee details
   - Easy cancellation / no lock-in
   - Support accessibility

7. **CTA:**
   - Primary: "[START / TRY / BOOK] — See For Yourself"
   - Secondary: "Still deciding? [COMPARISON PAGE / CASE STUDIES / DEMO]"

CONSTRAINTS:
- Differentiate, don't denigrate — never trash-talk competitors
- Every claim must be VERIFIABLE — "best in class" is meaningless without proof
- Acknowledge that you're NOT for everyone — specificity builds trust
- This page should make the RIGHT prospects confident and the WRONG ones self-select out

   - Product/promotional (20%): launches, features, offers
   - Community/engagement (15%): UGC, polls, behind-the-scenes

ADVOCACY PROGRAM:

1. **Advocate Identification:**
   | Signal | Score | Source |
   - High NPS score (9-10)
   - Repeat purchases / long tenure
   - Organic social mentions
   - Support ticket complimenters
   - Feature request submitters (engaged users)
   - Referral history

2. **Advocate Tiers:**

   - Bronze: signed up for program → exclusive newsletter, early access
   - Silver: 2+ advocacy actions → swag, feature previews, community access

   - Speak at event / webinar
   - Create user-generated content

   - Join beta testing

4. **Reward System:**
   | Points | Reward |
   - Points-based currency for advocacy actions
   - Tiered rewards: from swag to exclusive experiences

   - Monthly advocate newsletter

title: Video Sales Letter (VSL) Script
prompt:
Act as a direct-response video copywriter who scripts VSLs that hold attention for 5-10 minutes and convert viewers into buyers — without feeling like an infomercial.

Write a Video Sales Letter (VSL) script for [PRODUCT].

INPUTS:
- Product: [WHAT IT IS AND WHAT IT DOES]
- Price: $[AMOUNT]
- Target viewer: [WHO IS WATCHING AND WHERE — ad, landing page, email]
- Video length: [5 / 7 / 10 MINUTES]
- Visual style: [TALKING HEAD / SLIDES / SCREEN RECORDING / ANIMATED]
- Main offer: [WHAT THEY GET WHEN THEY BUY]

VSL SCRIPT:

1. **Hook (0:00-0:30):**
   Open with a bold statement, question, or story that creates instant curiosity:
   - "[SURPRISING STAT ABOUT THEIR PROBLEM]"
   - "What if I told you that [COMMON BELIEF] is completely wrong?"
   - "In the next [X] minutes, I'm going to show you [SPECIFIC OUTCOME]"
   VISUAL: text on screen reinforcing the hook

2. **Problem (0:30-2:00):**
   - Describe THEIR daily frustration (use their language)
   - 3 pain points with emotional weight
   - "If this sounds familiar, you're not alone — [STAT]"
   - Make them feel understood, not judged
   VISUAL: relatable scenarios, b-roll of frustration

3. **Failed Solutions (2:00-3:00):**
   - "You've probably tried [COMMON SOLUTION A]... and it didn't work because..."
   - "Or maybe [SOLUTION B]... which fails because..."
   - "It's not your fault — these approaches are fundamentally flawed"
   VISUAL: crossing out bad solutions

4. **The Bridge (3:00-3:30):**
   - "What if there was a way to [DESIRED OUTCOME] without [BIGGEST OBJECTION]?"
   - Introduce the mechanism/framework (NOT the product yet)
   VISUAL: breakthrough moment

5. **Product Reveal (3:30-5:00):**
   - Name and introduce the product
   - Show exactly what's included
   - Demo or walkthrough of key features
   - Social proof: "[X] people already use this"
   VISUAL: product demo, screenshots, UI walkthrough

6. **Proof (5:00-6:00):**
   - 2-3 specific customer results with numbers
   - Video testimonial clips (if available)
   - Before/after comparison
   VISUAL: testimonial clips, results screenshots

7. **Offer Stack (6:00-7:30):**
   - What they get (list with values)
   - Bonuses (2-3 with dollar values)
   - Total value vs. actual price
   - Payment options
   VISUAL: offer stack building, prices appearing

8. **Risk Reversal (7:30-8:00):**
   - Guarantee details
   - "You literally have nothing to lose"
   VISUAL: guarantee badge

9. **Final CTA (8:00-end):**
   - "Click the button below to [START/GET/JOIN]"
   - Repeat the transformation promise
   - Final urgency element
   - P.S. moment: one more reason to act now
   VISUAL: CTA button, countdown (if applicable)

PRODUCTION NOTES:
- Pacing: vary energy to maintain attention
- Text overlays: key points appear on screen (many watch without sound)
- Music: subtle background, builds during offer section
- Cuts: change visual every 5-7 seconds (prevents attention drop)
- Captions: always (80%+ of social video is watched without sound)

CONSTRAINTS:
- The hook must be COMPELLING in 3 seconds — or they'll scroll/click away
- Do NOT spend more than 30% of the video on the pitch — 70% must be value/story
- Every claim must be DEMONSTRABLE — show, don't just tell
- Script must work WITHOUT audio (text overlays for key points)
- End with ONE clear CTA — don't give multiple options

   | Action | Channel | Content |
   - Order confirmation email (receipt + what to expect next)
   - In-app/on-site thank you page with next steps
   - If physical: shipping tracker + estimated delivery

2. **Onboarding (Day 1-7):**
   | Day | Action | Goal |
   - Day 1: "Getting started" guide or tutorial
   - Day 3: "How's it going?" check-in
   - Day 5: Advanced tip or hidden feature
   - Day 7: "You've made great progress!"

3. **Review Request (Day 7-14):**
   - Timing: after they've had time to experience value
   - Channel: email with 1-click rating
   - Template: "How would you rate [PRODUCT]? It takes 30 seconds"
   - Platform: direct link to Google / Trustpilot / G2
   - If 4-5 stars → "Would you mind sharing on [PLATFORM]?"
   - If 1-3 stars → "We're sorry — what can we improve?" (route to support)

4. **Cross-Sell / Upsell (Day 14-30):**
   | Timing | Offer | Trigger |
   - Day 14: complementary product recommendation
   - Day 21: upgrade offer (if applicable)
   - Day 30: replenishment reminder (if consumable)
   - Personalized based on what they bought and how they use it

5. **Referral Ask (Day 30+):**
   - "Love [PRODUCT]? Share it with a friend — you both get [INCENTIVE]"
   - Unique referral link
   - Pre-written social share options

6. **Long-Term Retention:**
   | Frequency | Content | Goal |
   - Monthly: value-add content (tips, updates, community)
   - Quarterly: exclusive offer for existing customers
   - Annually: renewal/loyalty offer
   - Re-engagement: if inactive for 60+ days

7. **Win-Back (if churning):**
   | Signal | Response |
   - No login for 30 days → "We miss you" email
   - Cancellation initiated → exit survey + retention offer
   - Cancelled → "Doors are always open" with incentive to return

CONSTRAINTS:
- The thank-you page is NOT a dead end — it's prime real estate for next-action prompts
- Review requests must be TIMED right — too early and they haven't experienced value
- Cross-sells must be RELEVANT — random product pushes feel spammy
- Every post-purchase email must add VALUE, not just ask for something
- If they have a problem, make resolution EASY — hard-to-reach support kills loyalty

title: SMS Marketing Templates
prompt:
Act as an SMS marketing specialist who writes text messages that drive action with 98% open rates — because SMS is the most direct channel you have, and you must respect it.

Create SMS marketing templates for [BUSINESS].

INPUTS:
- Business: [WHAT YOU SELL]
- SMS list size: [NUMBER OF OPTED-IN SUBSCRIBERS]
- Message frequency: [PER WEEK / PER MONTH]
- Use cases: [PROMO / ABANDONED CART / REVIEW REQUEST / BACK IN STOCK / ORDER UPDATES]
- Brand voice: [CASUAL / PROFESSIONAL / PLAYFUL]
- Compliance: [TCPA COMPLIANT / NEEDS SETUP]

SMS TEMPLATES (10 categories):

For each template:
| Category | Message (160 chars max) | Best Send Time | Expected CTR |

1. **Welcome / Opt-In Confirmation:**
   "[BRAND]: Welcome! 🎉 Thanks for joining. Reply STOP to unsubscribe. Here's 10% off your first order: [LINK]"

2. **Promotional / Sale:**
   "[BRAND]: Flash sale! [X]% off everything for 24 hours only. Shop now: [LINK] Reply STOP to opt out"

3. **Abandoned Cart:**
   "Hey [NAME], you left something behind! Your cart is saved for the next 2 hours: [LINK]"

4. **Review Request:**
   "[NAME], how's your [PRODUCT]? We'd love a quick review (takes 30 sec): [LINK] Thank you! 🙏"

5. **Back in Stock:**
   "Good news, [NAME]! [PRODUCT] is back in stock. Grab yours before it sells out again: [LINK]"

6. **Order Update:**
   "[BRAND]: Your order #[NUMBER] has shipped! Track it here: [LINK]"

7. **Exclusive / VIP:**
   "[NAME], as a VIP you get early access to [OFFER]. Opens to public tomorrow: [LINK]"

8. **Re-Engagement:**
   "We miss you, [NAME]! Here's [INCENTIVE] to come back: [LINK] Valid 48 hours ⏰"

9. **Event / Webinar:**
   "Reminder: [EVENT] starts in 1 hour! Join here: [LINK] See you there! 🚀"

10. **Feedback / Survey:**
    "Quick question, [NAME]: How likely are you to recommend us? Reply 1-10. Your feedback shapes what we build next."

**Compliance Checklist:**
- [ ] Express written consent collected
- [ ] Opt-out in every message (STOP to unsubscribe)
- [ ] Business identification in every message
- [ ] Sending hours: 9am-9pm recipient's timezone
- [ ] Frequency cap: max 4-6 messages per month
- [ ] Immediate STOP processing

**Automation Flows:**
| Trigger | Message | Timing |
- Cart abandoned → SMS 1 (1 hour) → SMS 2 (24 hours)
- Purchase → Thank you (immediate) → Review request (Day 7)
- Re-engagement → Offer (Day 30 inactive) → Final (Day 60)

CONSTRAINTS:
- EVERY message must include opt-out language — it's the law (TCPA)
- Keep under 160 characters when possible — avoid multipart messages
- Do NOT send more than 4-6 messages per month — SMS is intimate, don't abuse it
- Personalize with [NAME] — it increases engagement 20%+
- ALWAYS disclose it's a marketing message — transparency is required and builds trust
- Test send times: typically 10am-12pm and 5pm-7pm perform best

title: Marketing Attribution Model
prompt:
Act as a marketing analytics expert who builds attribution models that answer the hardest question in marketing: "Which channels are actually driving revenue?"

Create a marketing attribution model for [BUSINESS].

INPUTS:
- Business: [WHAT YOU SELL AND HOW]
- Marketing channels: [LIST ALL ACTIVE CHANNELS]
- Monthly marketing budget: $[AMOUNT]
- Sales cycle: [LENGTH]
- Touchpoints before conversion: [AVERAGE NUMBER]
- Analytics tools: [GA4 / MIXPANEL / HUBSPOT / CUSTOM]
- Current attribution: [NONE / LAST-CLICK / FIRST-CLICK / MULTI-TOUCH]

ATTRIBUTION MODEL:

1. **Channel Inventory:**
   | Channel | Monthly Spend | Conversions (last-click) | Revenue | Current ROAS |
   - Paid search, paid social, organic, email, referral, direct, etc.
   - Identify the discrepancy between last-click and actual impact

2. **Customer Journey Mapping:**
   | Journey Stage | Typical Touchpoints | Data Source |
   - Awareness: first discovery (how do they find you?)
   - Consideration: research phase (what do they interact with?)
   - Decision: final conversion (what's the last touch?)
   - Post-purchase: retention triggers

3. **Attribution Model Options:**
   | Model | How It Works | Best For | Limitation |
   - Last-click: 100% credit to final touchpoint — simple but misleading
   - First-click: 100% to first touchpoint — values awareness but ignores nurture
   - Linear: equal credit to all touchpoints — fair but unsophisticated
   - Time-decay: more credit to recent touches — values closing channels
   - Position-based (U-shaped): 40% first, 40% last, 20% middle — balanced
   - Data-driven: ML-based, custom weights — most accurate, needs data volume
   
   RECOMMENDATION: [MODEL] because [REASON BASED ON THEIR DATA]

4. **Implementation:**
   | Step | Tool | Setup |
   - UTM parameter framework for all links
   - Cross-device tracking setup
   - CRM integration for offline conversions
   - Custom dashboard for attribution reporting

5. **Reporting Framework:**
   | Metric | By Channel | By Campaign | By Content |
   - First-touch attribution (awareness drivers)
   - Last-touch attribution (conversion drivers)
   - Multi-touch attribution (full picture)
   - Assisted conversions (channels that help but don't close)
   - Path analysis: most common conversion paths

6. **Budget Optimization:**
   | Channel | Current Spend | Attributed Revenue | True ROAS | Action |
   - Increase spend on undervalued channels
   - Decrease spend on overvalued channels
   - Test budget reallocation: shift 20% and measure impact

7. **Reporting Cadence:**
   | Report | Frequency | Audience | Key Questions |
   - Weekly: channel performance dashboard
   - Monthly: attribution analysis and budget recommendations
   - Quarterly: model accuracy review and recalibration

CONSTRAINTS:
- No attribution model is PERFECT — choose one that's USEFUL, not theoretically ideal
- Last-click attribution OVERVALUES bottom-of-funnel channels — always use multi-touch
- Attribution requires CLEAN DATA — garbage in, garbage out
- Multi-device tracking is imperfect — acknowledge the limitations
- Review and recalibrate the model quarterly — channel effectiveness changes over time

title: Freemium-to-Premium Conversion Strategy
prompt:
Act as a SaaS product marketing strategist who designs conversion strategies that turn free users into paying customers — without making the free tier useless or the upgrade feel forced.

Design a freemium-to-premium conversion strategy for [PRODUCT].

INPUTS:
- Product: [WHAT IT DOES]
- Free tier: [WHAT'S INCLUDED]
- Premium tier(s): $[PRICE] — [WHAT'S INCLUDED]
- Current free users: [NUMBER]
- Current free-to-paid rate: [X%]
- Target rate: [Y%]
- Activation metric: [KEY ACTION THAT PREDICTS UPGRADE]
- Average time from signup to upgrade: [DAYS]

CONVERSION STRATEGY:

1. **Free/Paid Boundary Analysis:**
   | Feature | Free | Premium | Why This Boundary |
   - Features that demonstrate value → FREE
   - Features that multiply value → PREMIUM
   - Usage limits that natural power users hit → UPGRADE TRIGGER
   
   The free tier must be VALUABLE enough that users love it. The premium tier must be NECESSARY when they outgrow free.

2. **Upgrade Triggers:**
   | Trigger | When It Fires | Message | Channel |
   - Hit usage limit (storage, exports, team members)
   - Attempted premium feature (natural discovery)
   - High engagement (power user pattern)
   - Time-based (after X days of active use)
   - Team growth (invited members hit free limit)

3. **In-App Upgrade Prompts:**
   | Location | Trigger | Message | Design |
   - Feature gate: "This is a Pro feature — upgrade to unlock"
   - Usage meter: "You've used 80% of your free allowance"
   - Upgrade page: benefit comparison, not just feature list
   - Success moment: "You just did [X] — imagine what you could do with [PREMIUM FEATURE]"

4. **Email Conversion Sequence:**
   | Day | Subject | Content | For Segment |
   - Active free users (using regularly):
     Week 2: show what they're missing (feature tease)
     Week 4: case study from similar free-to-paid user
     Week 6: limited-time upgrade offer
   - Inactive free users:
     Week 1: "Here's what you're not using yet"
     Week 2: re-engagement with new feature
     Week 3: "Is [PRODUCT] right for you?" (honest assessment)

5. **Pricing Page Optimization:**
   - Plan comparison: clear, visual, benefit-focused
   - Recommended plan highlighted
   - Annual discount prominent (save X%)
   - Social proof near pricing
   - FAQ addressing upgrade concerns
   - Risk reversal: 14-day money-back guarantee

6. **Conversion Experiments:**
   | Test | Hypothesis | Metric |
   - Test 1: paywall placement (which features to gate)
   - Test 2: upgrade messaging (benefit vs. scarcity vs. social proof)
   - Test 3: pricing (tiers, annual vs. monthly, anchoring)
   - Test 4: trial of premium (time-limited full access)

CONSTRAINTS:
- The free tier must be genuinely USEFUL — a crippled free tier doesn't create converts, it creates churners
- Upgrade prompts must feel HELPFUL, not annoying — "You might need this" not "PAY NOW"
- Premium features must be clearly MORE valuable — not just "more of the same"
- Don't gate features that are table stakes in your industry — that feels punitive
- Track free-to-paid COHORTS — which signup source, industry, or use case converts best?

title: Co-Marketing Partnership Plan
prompt:
Act as a partnership marketing manager who designs co-marketing campaigns that double both partners' reach — because the best partnerships create value that neither could alone.

Write a co-marketing campaign plan with [PARTNER TYPE].

INPUTS:
- Your product: [WHAT YOU SELL]
- Partner type: [COMPLEMENTARY PRODUCT / SERVICE / INFLUENCER / MEDIA]
- Shared audience: [WHO YOU BOTH SERVE]
- Campaign goal: [LEADS / SALES / CONTENT / BRAND AWARENESS]
- Budget: $[YOUR CONTRIBUTION]
- Timeline: [CAMPAIGN DURATION]

CO-MARKETING PLAN:

1. **Partnership Alignment:**
   | Dimension | Your Brand | Partner Brand | Overlap |
   - Audience demographics and interests
   - Brand values and tone
   - Goal alignment
   - Distribution channels
   - Competitive conflicts (ensure you're complementary, not competitive)

2. **Campaign Concept (3 options):**
   | Concept | Format | Value to Audience | Your Role | Partner Role |
   - Co-created content: webinar, ebook, research report
   - Bundle/cross-promotion: combined offer, mutual discounts
   - Event: virtual summit, workshop, networking
   - Product integration: technical partnership, feature collaboration

3. **Execution Plan:**
   | Phase | Timeline | Your Tasks | Partner Tasks | Shared Tasks |
   - Planning: align on goals, audience, messaging
   - Creation: content production, design, landing pages
   - Promotion: email, social, paid distribution
   - Measurement: tracking setup, reporting

4. **Promotional Calendar:**
   | Date | Channel | Your Promotion | Partner Promotion |
   - Email announcements to both lists
   - Social media cross-posting schedule
   - Blog/content publication dates
   - Paid amplification (if applicable)

5. **Lead Sharing Agreement:**
   | Scenario | Your Leads | Partner Leads | Shared Leads |
   - How leads are captured (co-branded landing page)
   - How leads are shared (CSV, CRM sync, integration)
   - Lead ownership and follow-up responsibility
   - Privacy compliance (GDPR, CCPA)

6. **Co-Branded Assets:**
   - Landing page (both logos, both CTAs)
   - Email templates (joint announcement)
   - Social media posts (tagged, co-branded)
   - Content piece (both contributors credited)

7. **Measurement:**
   | Metric | Your Target | Partner Target | Combined |
   - Leads generated per partner
   - Revenue attributed to partnership
   - Audience reach expansion
   - Content engagement metrics
   - Partnership NPS (would you partner again?)

CONSTRAINTS:
- Both partners must BENEFIT EQUALLY — lopsided partnerships don't last
- Brand alignment is NON-NEGOTIABLE — don't partner with brands your audience doesn't trust
- Get EVERYTHING in writing — lead sharing, promotion commitments, content rights
- Start SMALL — test with a single campaign before committing to a long-term partnership
- Measure BOTH direct revenue AND relationship value — some partnerships pay off indirectly

title: Email Re-Engagement Campaign
prompt:
Act as an email deliverability expert who designs re-engagement campaigns that recover inactive subscribers — because a clean, engaged list outperforms a large, dead one every time.

Design an email re-engagement campaign for inactive subscribers of [BRAND].

INPUTS:
- Brand: [WHAT YOU SELL]
- List size: [TOTAL SUBSCRIBERS]
- Inactive definition: [NO OPENS IN X DAYS — typically 90-180 days]
- Inactive segment size: [NUMBER / PERCENTAGE]
- Last re-engagement attempt: [WHEN AND WHAT HAPPENED — or "never"]
- Value proposition for staying: [WHY SHOULD THEY WANT YOUR EMAILS]

RE-ENGAGEMENT CAMPAIGN:

1. **Segmentation:**
   | Segment | Criteria | Approach |
   - Warm inactive: no opens in 60-90 days → gentle re-engagement
   - Cold inactive: no opens in 90-180 days → value + urgency
   - Dead: no opens in 180+ days → sunset sequence

2. **Re-Engagement Sequence (4 emails):**

   **Email 1 — "We Miss You":**
   Subject: "Still interested in [TOPIC]?" (curiosity, not guilt)
   Body: remind them WHY they signed up + share your best recent content
   CTA: "Click here to stay subscribed" (1-click re-opt-in)

   **Email 2 — "Here's What You've Missed" (Day 5):**
   Subject: "Your top 3 missed updates"
   Body: curate your 3 best pieces of content from the past 90 days
   CTA: engage with any piece of content

   **Email 3 — "Exclusive Offer" (Day 10):**
   Subject: "A gift for coming back ❤️"
   Body: special offer, discount, or free resource ONLY for re-engagers
   CTA: claim the offer

   **Email 4 — "Last Chance" (Day 15):**
   Subject: "Should we stop emailing you?"
   Body: honest, respectful — "We don't want to clutter your inbox"
   CTA: "Yes, keep me subscribed" / "No, unsubscribe me"
   If no action → automatic removal from active list

3. **Post-Campaign Actions:**
   | Outcome | Action |
   - Re-engaged (opened/clicked) → move to active segment, send welcome-back email
   - Partially engaged (opened only) → move to low-frequency segment
   - No engagement → unsubscribe and suppress (improves deliverability)

4. **List Hygiene Schedule:**
   | Action | Frequency |
   - Run re-engagement campaign: quarterly
   - Remove hard bounces: immediately
   - Segment by engagement: monthly
   - Monitor deliverability metrics: weekly

CONSTRAINTS:
- Do NOT guilt-trip subscribers — "We're sad you're leaving" is manipulative
- Unsubscribing inactive users IMPROVES your metrics — don't be afraid to clean the list
- Make re-subscribing ONE click — don't send them to a form
- The offer in Email 3 must be GENUINELY good — not a weak 5% discount
- A smaller, engaged list ALWAYS outperforms a large, inactive one

title: Cold LinkedIn DM Outreach
prompt:
Act as a LinkedIn social selling expert who books meetings through DMs — not by spamming pitches, but by starting genuine conversations that naturally lead to business.

Write a cold LinkedIn DM outreach sequence for [SERVICE].

INPUTS:
- Service: [WHAT YOU OFFER]
- Target: [JOB TITLE AT COMPANY TYPE]
- Value proposition: [THE #1 BENEFIT YOU PROVIDE]
- Price range: $[AMOUNT]
- Social proof: [RESULTS YOU'VE ACHIEVED]
- Your LinkedIn profile: [OPTIMIZED? WHAT'S YOUR HEADLINE?]

3-MESSAGE SEQUENCE:

**Message 1 — Connection Request (300 char max):**
"Hey [NAME], saw your [SPECIFIC POST/ARTICLE/ROLE]. [GENUINE OBSERVATION — not flattery]. I work with [SIMILAR COMPANIES] on [RELATED TOPIC]. Would love to connect."

DO: reference something specific from their profile
DON'T: pitch in the connection request (kills acceptance rate)

**Message 2 — Day 2-3 (after accepted):**
"Thanks for connecting, [NAME]! Quick question — are you dealing with [SPECIFIC CHALLENGE] at [COMPANY]?

I ask because we recently helped [SIMILAR COMPANY] [SPECIFIC RESULT], and I noticed you might be facing something similar based on [OBSERVATION].

No pitch — just curious if it resonates."

DO: ask a relevant question, share a relevant insight
DON'T: send a paragraph about your company and services

**Message 3 — Day 5-7 (if no reply):**
"[NAME], not trying to be annoying — I know you're busy.

I put together a quick [RESOURCE: case study / guide / video] on [TOPIC RELEVANT TO THEIR ROLE]. Thought it might be useful regardless of whether we chat: [LINK]

Either way, happy to have you in my network!"

DO: provide value with no strings attached
DON'T: pressure for a meeting or get passive-aggressive

**If They Respond Positively:**
"Glad it resonated! Would a quick 15-minute call make sense? I can share exactly how we approached [SPECIFIC CHALLENGE] for [SIMILAR COMPANY].

Here's my calendar: [LINK] — or just tell me a time that works."

**If They Say "Not Now":**
"Totally get it — timing is everything. Mind if I check back in [TIMEFRAME]? Happy to share relevant content in the meantime."

**Pre-Outreach Engagement (1-2 weeks before DM):**
| Action | Frequency | Goal |
- Like their posts: 3-5 times
- Leave thoughtful comments: 2-3 times (not "Great post!")
- Share their content with your insight added
- This warms them up before you ever send a message

CONSTRAINTS:
- NEVER pitch in the connection request — it's the #1 reason people decline
- NEVER send paragraph-length messages — keep under 100 words
- NEVER use "I'd love to pick your brain" (it's a red flag for an incoming pitch)
- Personalize EVERY message — LinkedIn users can spot copy-paste templates instantly
- LinkedIn limits connection requests: stay under 100 per week to avoid restrictions
- Your PROFILE matters more than your outreach — optimize headline, about, and banner first

title: Podcast Sponsorship Strategy
prompt:
Act as a media buyer who specializes in podcast advertising — the most trusted ad format with 93% listener attention rates.

Create a podcast sponsorship strategy for [PRODUCT/SERVICE].

INPUTS:
- Product: [WHAT YOU SELL]
- Budget: $[MONTHLY/QUARTERLY BUDGET]
- Target audience: [WHO YOU WANT TO REACH]
- Goal: [BRAND AWARENESS / DIRECT RESPONSE / BOTH]
- Previous podcast ads: [WHAT YOU'VE TRIED — or "first campaign"]
- Landing page: [URL FOR TRACKING]

STRATEGY:

1. **Target Podcast Identification (10 shows):**
   | # | Podcast | Downloads/Episode | Audience Fit | Ad Rate | CPM |
   Selection criteria:
   - Audience alignment (demographics, interests, buying power)
   - Download numbers (minimum 5K per episode for measurable results)
   - Host credibility (do listeners trust their recommendations?)
   - Ad format offered (host-read vs. produced)
   - Competitor presence (are competitors already sponsoring?)

2. **Ad Format & Placement:**
   | Format | Cost | Effectiveness | Best For |
   - Pre-roll (15-30 sec): cheapest, lowest attention → brand awareness
   - Mid-roll (60 sec): most expensive, highest attention → direct response
   - Post-roll (15-30 sec): cheapest, decent attention → retargeting/CTA
   - Host-read: most effective (host's voice = trust transfer)
   - Produced: consistent messaging, less authentic

3. **Ad Script (host-read, 60 seconds):**
   - Opening hook: personal connection to the product (15 sec)
   - Problem: "If you've ever struggled with [PAIN]..." (10 sec)
   - Solution: what the product does and why it's different (15 sec)
   - Social proof: "I've been using it myself and..." (10 sec)
   - CTA: "Go to [URL] or use code [CODE] for [OFFER]" (10 sec)
   
   Provide 3 script variations for host to choose most natural.

4. **Tracking & Attribution:**
   | Method | How It Works |
   - Vanity URL: [BRAND].com/[PODCAST] → redirect to landing page
   - Promo code: unique per podcast (e.g., "CODE: [PODCAST NAME]")
   - Post-purchase survey: "How did you hear about us?"
   - UTM parameters on links
   - Pixel-based attribution (if podcast network supports)

5. **Testing Plan:**
   | Phase | Duration | Shows | Budget | Goal |
   - Phase 1 (test): 2-3 shows, 4 weeks, $[X] each
   - Phase 2 (optimize): double down on winners, cut losers
   - Phase 3 (scale): add shows in similar categories

6. **ROI Calculation:**
   | Metric | Target |
   - Cost per acquisition (CPA) from podcast
   - Revenue per podcast dollar
   - Brand lift (surveys, branded search volume increase)
   - LTV of podcast-acquired customers vs. other channels

CONSTRAINTS:
- Host-read ads ALWAYS outperform produced ads — pay extra for host endorsement
- Mid-roll placement is worth the premium — listeners are most engaged
- Test for at least 4 episodes before judging results — podcast ads have a cumulative effect
- Track EVERYTHING — without attribution, you're spending blind
- Negotiate: podcast ad rates are ALWAYS negotiable, especially for multi-episode deals

title: Content Syndication Strategy
prompt:
Act as a content distribution strategist who ensures every piece of content reaches 10x more people — because creating great content without distributing it is like winking in the dark.

Design a content syndication strategy for [BRAND].

INPUTS:
- Brand: [WHAT YOU DO]
- Content types: [BLOG / VIDEO / PODCAST / NEWSLETTER / SOCIAL]
- Current distribution: [WHERE YOUR CONTENT IS PUBLISHED NOW]
- Team bandwidth: [HOW MUCH TIME FOR DISTRIBUTION]
- Goal: [TRAFFIC / LEADS / BRAND AWARENESS / SEO]
- Top-performing content: [YOUR 5 BEST PIECES]

SYNDICATION STRATEGY:

1. **Content Audit:**
   | Content | Performance | Evergreen? | Syndication Potential |
   - Identify top 20% of content (drives 80% of results)
   - Prioritize: high-performing + evergreen = syndication gold

2. **Syndication Channels:**
   | Channel | Content Format | Audience | How to Submit | Frequency |
   - LinkedIn Articles: republish with "Originally published on..."
   - Medium: repost with canonical link (SEO-safe)
   - Industry newsletters: pitch for inclusion
   - Reddit: share in relevant subreddits (no self-promotion — add value)
   - Quora: answer questions with linked content
   - Email newsletters: partner publications
   - Content aggregators: Flipboard, SmartNews, Apple News
   - Podcast appearances: repurpose blog content as talking points

3. **Platform-Specific Adaptation:**
   | Original Content | LinkedIn | Twitter/X | Newsletter | Video |
   - Blog post → LinkedIn article + Twitter thread + email highlight + video summary
   - Video → Blog post + social clips + podcast audio + LinkedIn carousel
   - Podcast → Blog recap + audiogram clips + quote graphics + newsletter feature

4. **Syndication Schedule:**
   | Day | Original Publish | Syndication Actions |
   - Day 0: publish original on your site
   - Day 1: share on owned social channels
   - Day 3: republish on Medium with canonical
   - Day 5: create LinkedIn article version
   - Day 7: pitch to industry newsletters
   - Day 14: repurpose as different format (video, infographic)
   - Day 30: reshare on social with new angle

5. **SEO Considerations:**
   - ALWAYS use canonical tags when republishing (prevents duplicate content penalty)
   - ALWAYS link back to original (drives authority to your domain)
   - Wait 3-7 days before syndication (let Google index original first)
   - Never give exclusive rights — always retain ownership

6. **Measurement:**
   | Metric | By Channel | Monthly |
   - Referral traffic from syndication channels
   - New leads from syndicated content
   - Email subscribers gained
   - Backlinks acquired through syndication

CONSTRAINTS:
- ALWAYS use canonical URLs when republishing — duplicate content kills SEO
- Do NOT just copy-paste — adapt content to each platform's style and audience
- Do NOT syndicate EVERYTHING — only your best, most relevant content
- Give credit: "Originally published on [YOUR SITE]" with link
- Quality over quantity: 1 well-distributed piece > 10 poorly shared ones

title: Product Comparison Chart
prompt:
Act as a product marketing analyst who creates comparison charts that are fair, thorough, and help buyers make confident decisions — because clarity converts better than confusion.

Create a product comparison chart with feature-by-feature analysis.

INPUTS:
- Your product: [NAME, VERSION, PRICING]
- Competitor A: [NAME, VERSION, PRICING]
- Competitor B: [NAME, VERSION, PRICING]
- Comparison purpose: [SALES TOOL / WEBSITE PAGE / INTERNAL STRATEGY]
- Key decision factors: [WHAT MATTERS MOST TO BUYERS]
- Data sources: [HOW YOU'LL VERIFY COMPETITOR INFO]

COMPARISON CHART:

1. **Overview Table:**
   | Attribute | [YOUR PRODUCT] | [COMPETITOR A] | [COMPETITOR B] |
   - Year founded / product maturity
   - Target market
   - Starting price
   - Free tier / trial
   - Overall rating (G2, Capterra, Trustpilot)

2. **Feature-by-Feature Comparison:**
   Organize by category:
   
   **Core Features:**
   | Feature | [YOUR PRODUCT] | [COMPETITOR A] | [COMPETITOR B] | Winner |
   Use: ✅ Included / ⚡ Limited / ❌ Not available / 🔜 Coming soon
   
   **Advanced Features:**
   | Feature | Details per product |
   
   **Integrations:**
   | Integration | Availability per product |
   
   **Support & Resources:**
   | Feature | Response times, channels, documentation |
   
   **Security & Compliance:**
   | Feature | Certifications, standards per product |

3. **Pricing Deep Dive:**
   | Tier | [YOUR PRODUCT] | [COMPETITOR A] | [COMPETITOR B] |
   - Feature breakdown per tier
   - Per-user vs. flat pricing
   - Annual vs. monthly discount
   - Hidden costs (onboarding, overages, support tiers)
   - Total Cost of Ownership for typical use case:
     | Scenario | 1 User | 10 Users | 50 Users | 100 Users |

4. **Strengths & Weaknesses Summary:**
   For each product:
   | Product | Top 3 Strengths | Top 3 Weaknesses |
   Include your own product's weaknesses (builds credibility)

5. **Use Case Recommendations:**
   | Use Case | Best Choice | Why |
   - "Best for small teams" → [PRODUCT]
   - "Best for enterprise" → [PRODUCT]
   - "Best value for money" → [PRODUCT]
   - "Best for [SPECIFIC NEED]" → [PRODUCT]

6. **Methodology Note:**
   - Date of comparison: [DATE]
   - Data sources: official websites, product demos, G2 reviews
   - Pricing verified as of: [DATE]
   - "This comparison will be updated quarterly"

CONSTRAINTS:
- EVERY claim must be VERIFIABLE — include source links or dates
- Be HONEST about your weaknesses — credibility matters more than looking perfect
- DATE-STAMP the comparison — pricing and features change constantly
- Update at least QUARTERLY — an outdated comparison damages trust
- If you're unsure about a competitor's feature, say "Unverified" — never guess

title: Meeting Notes Synthesizer
prompt:
Act as an executive assistant who transforms chaotic meeting notes into actionable intelligence — because meetings are only valuable if their decisions actually get executed.

Analyze these meeting notes and create a structured action package.

INPUTS:
- Meeting notes: [PASTE RAW NOTES]
- Meeting type: [TEAM STANDUP / STRATEGY SESSION / CLIENT CALL / BOARD MEETING / 1:1]
- Attendees: [LIST OF PEOPLE AND THEIR ROLES]
- Meeting purpose: [WHY THIS MEETING WAS HELD]

DELIVERABLES:

1. **Meeting Overview (2-3 sentences):**
   What was discussed, what was decided, what's next.

2. **Key Decisions Made:**
   | # | Decision | Made By | Impact | Effective Date |
   - Only ACTUAL decisions, not discussions or opinions
   - Flag any decisions that need formal approval

3. **Action Items:**
   | # | Action | Owner | Deadline | Priority | Dependencies | Status |
   - Every action must have a SPECIFIC owner (not "the team")
   - Every action must have a SPECIFIC deadline (not "soon")
   - Flag actions that are blocked or have dependencies

4. **Discussion Summary:**
   | Topic | Key Points | Outcome | Follow-Up Needed? |
   - Separate discussions from decisions
   - Note disagreements or unresolved tensions

5. **Open Questions:**
   | Question | Who Should Answer | Deadline for Answer |
   - Questions raised but not resolved in the meeting

6. **Parking Lot:**
   Topics mentioned but deferred — with suggested meeting/time to address them.

7. **Follow-Up Email Draft:**
   Subject: "[MEETING NAME] — Summary & Action Items [DATE]"
   - Brief, scannable, action-focused
   - Action items bolded with owners and dates
   - Link to full notes document
   - Next meeting date/time

CONSTRAINTS:
- Action items must be SPECIFIC and MEASURABLE — not "follow up on the thing"
- Every action needs ONE owner — shared ownership means no ownership
- If the notes are unclear, flag what needs clarification rather than guessing
- Keep the follow-up email under 200 words — nobody reads long recap emails
- Separate FACTS from OPINIONS in the summary

title: Agenda Builder
prompt:
Act as a meeting facilitator who designs agendas that keep meetings on track, on time, and actually productive — because a meeting without an agenda is just a group of people sitting in a room.

Create a meeting agenda for [MEETING PURPOSE].

INPUTS:
- Meeting purpose: [WHAT NEEDS TO BE ACCOMPLISHED]
- Duration: [30 / 45 / 60 / 90 MINUTES]
- Attendees: [LIST WITH ROLES]
- Decision(s) to make: [WHAT MUST BE DECIDED BY END OF MEETING]
- Pre-work needed: [WHAT ATTENDEES SHOULD PREPARE]
- Recurring or one-time: [FREQUENCY]

AGENDA:

1. **Pre-Meeting Package (sent 24-48 hours before):**
   - Agenda with timing
   - Pre-read documents (with specific sections to focus on)
   - Questions to consider before the meeting
   - "Come prepared to share: [SPECIFIC INPUT]"

2. **Agenda Structure:**
   | Time | Duration | Topic | Owner | Type | Output |
   Types: 🔵 Information | 🟡 Discussion | 🔴 Decision | ⚡ Action
   
   - Opening (2-3 min): purpose, desired outcomes, ground rules
   - Topic 1 ([X] min): [TOPIC] — [OWNER] — [TYPE]
   - Topic 2 ([X] min): [TOPIC] — [OWNER] — [TYPE]
   - Topic 3 ([X] min): [TOPIC] — [OWNER] — [TYPE]
   - Parking lot review (3 min): deferred items
   - Action items & next steps (5 min): capture commitments
   - Close (2 min): feedback, next meeting date

3. **Facilitator Guide:**
   - Time management: "If we're over by 2 minutes on any topic, I'll flag it"
   - Decision-making method: [CONSENSUS / MAJORITY / LEADER DECIDES]
   - How to handle tangents: parking lot + redirect script
   - Engagement tactics: round-robin, silent brainstorming, dot voting
   - Energy management: standing, movement, or break for meetings 60+ min

4. **Meeting Ground Rules:**
   - Cameras on (if remote)
   - One speaker at a time
   - "Is this a meeting question or a Slack question?"
   - Decisions are final unless new information emerges
   - Action items are commitments, not suggestions

5. **Post-Meeting Template:**
   - Decisions made
   - Action items with owners and deadlines
   - Parking lot items
   - Next meeting date and topics

CONSTRAINTS:
- Every topic must have a TIME LIMIT — Parkinson's Law applies to meetings
- Every topic must have an OWNER who drives the discussion
- No meeting should be longer than 90 minutes — schedule a second meeting instead
- "Information only" topics should be an EMAIL, not a meeting agenda item
- If there are no decisions to make, consider whether this meeting is necessary at all

title: Email Draft Writer
prompt:
Act as a communication strategist who writes emails that get read, understood, and acted on — because the average professional gets 121 emails daily and yours needs to stand out.

Write a professional email for [SITUATION].

INPUTS:
- From: [YOUR ROLE/NAME]
- To: [RECIPIENT ROLE/NAME]
- Context: [BACKGROUND SITUATION]
- Goal: [WHAT YOU WANT TO HAPPEN AFTER THEY READ THIS]
- Tone: [FORMAL / FRIENDLY / URGENT / DIPLOMATIC / ASSERTIVE]
- Relationship: [BOSS / PEER / CLIENT / VENDOR / COLD CONTACT]
- Sensitivity: [ROUTINE / DELICATE / HIGH-STAKES]

DELIVERABLES:

1. **Subject Line (3 options):**
   | Option | Style | Best When |
   - Action-required: "[ACTION NEEDED] [TOPIC] by [DATE]"
   - Informational: "[UPDATE] [TOPIC] — [KEY DETAIL]"
   - Conversational: "[TOPIC] — quick question"
   Each under 50 characters for mobile.

2. **Version A — Concise (under 100 words):**
   - Opening: 1 sentence context
   - Body: key message in 2-3 sentences
   - CTA: exactly what you need them to do
   - Close: professional sign-off

3. **Version B — Detailed (150-250 words):**
   - Opening: context and why you're writing
   - Body: full explanation with bullet points
   - Supporting details: data, links, attachments
   - CTA: specific action with deadline
   - Close: next steps and availability

4. **Formatting Guidelines:**
   - Bold the CTA and deadline
   - Use bullets for multiple items (never paragraphs for lists)
   - One ask per email (if multiple, number them)
   - White space between sections (scanability)

5. **Follow-Up Sequence (if no response):**
   - Day 3: gentle bump (forward original + "Bumping this up")
   - Day 7: reframe with new angle or urgency
   - Day 14: final follow-up with deadline

CONSTRAINTS:
- Get to the point in the FIRST sentence — no "I hope this email finds you well" padding
- One email = one topic — don't bundle unrelated asks
- CTA must be SPECIFIC: "Please approve by Friday at 3pm" not "Let me know your thoughts"
- Never write more than 5 sentences without a visual break (bullet, header, space)
- If the email requires more than 300 words, it should be a meeting or a document with a short email linking to it

title: Difficult Conversation Prep
prompt:
Act as an executive coach who prepares leaders for high-stakes conversations — because the words you choose in difficult moments define your relationships and reputation.

Help me prepare for a difficult conversation about [TOPIC].

INPUTS:
- Person: [WHO — their role, personality, communication style]
- Topic: [WHAT THE CONVERSATION IS ABOUT]
- Context: [WHAT HAPPENED AND WHY THIS CONVERSATION IS NEEDED]
- Relationship: [BOSS / DIRECT REPORT / PEER / CLIENT / PARTNER]
- Desired outcome: [IDEAL RESULT]
- Worst case: [WHAT YOU WANT TO AVOID]
- Your emotional state: [NERVOUS / FRUSTRATED / SAD / UNCERTAIN]

CONVERSATION PREP:

1. **Pre-Conversation Mindset:**
   - Reframe: "This is not a confrontation. It's a [PROBLEM-SOLVING SESSION / CLARITY CONVERSATION / ALIGNMENT DISCUSSION]"
   - Your goal: [RESTATE IN NON-ADVERSARIAL TERMS]
   - Their likely perspective: [WHAT DO THEY PROBABLY THINK/FEEL]
   - Common ground: [WHAT DO YOU BOTH WANT]

2. **Opening Statement (script):**
   Non-confrontational opener that sets the tone:
   "I'd like to talk about [TOPIC] because [REASON IT MATTERS TO BOTH OF YOU]. My goal is [SHARED POSITIVE OUTCOME]. Is now a good time?"

3. **Key Points to Communicate (3-5):**
   | Point | What to Say | What NOT to Say | Why |
   - Use "I" statements: "I've noticed..." not "You always..."
   - Be specific: cite examples, dates, impacts
   - Focus on behavior and impact, not character

4. **Anticipated Reactions & Responses:**
   | Their Reaction | Your Response |
   - If defensive: "I understand this is hard to hear. I'm bringing this up because I value [RELATIONSHIP/WORK]"
   - If angry: "I can see you're upset. Would you like to take a moment?"
   - If deflecting: "I hear you, and I'd like to address that. Can we first talk about [THE MAIN ISSUE]?"
   - If silent: "I'd really like to hear your perspective. What are your thoughts?"
   - If emotional: "Your feelings are valid. Take your time."

5. **Questions to Ask:**
   | Question | Purpose |
   - "How do you see the situation?" — understand their perspective
   - "What would feel fair to you?" — collaborative solution
   - "What do you need from me?" — show willingness to adapt
   - "How can we prevent this going forward?" — future-focus

6. **Phrases to Avoid → Better Alternatives:**
   | Avoid | Use Instead | Why |
   | "You always..." | "I've noticed a pattern of..." | Removes blame |
   | "That's not true" | "I see it differently" | Validates their experience |
   | "You need to..." | "Would you be willing to..." | Empowers choice |
   | "With all due respect" | [Just make the point directly] | It signals disrespect |
   | "Don't take this personally" | [Don't say this at all] | Guarantees they will |

7. **Desired Outcome & Fallback:**
   - Best case: [IDEAL OUTCOME]
   - Acceptable: [MINIMUM ACCEPTABLE OUTCOME]
   - If no agreement: "I appreciate you hearing me out. Can we schedule a follow-up in [TIMEFRAME] to revisit?"

8. **Follow-Up Plan:**
   - Send summary email within 24 hours (agreed actions)
   - Schedule check-in in 2 weeks
   - Document for HR/records if necessary

CONSTRAINTS:
- The goal is UNDERSTANDING, not winning — if you win the argument but lose the relationship, you lost
- Never have a difficult conversation over email or text — face-to-face or video only
- Listen MORE than you speak — aim for 30/70 talk ratio
- Never surprise someone with a difficult conversation — "Can we schedule time to discuss X?"
- If emotions are too high (yours or theirs), it's okay to pause and reschedule

title: Weekly Planning System
prompt:
Act as a productivity coach who designs planning systems that actually get used — because the best system is the one you'll consistently follow, not the most elaborate one.

Design a weekly planning system for a [ROLE].

INPUTS:
- Role: [JOB TITLE AND KEY RESPONSIBILITIES]
- Working hours: [SCHEDULE AND FLEXIBILITY]
- Key responsibilities: [TOP 5 AREAS OF FOCUS]
- Current challenges: [WHAT'S NOT WORKING ABOUT YOUR CURRENT APPROACH]
- Tools: [WHAT YOU USE — calendar, task manager, notes]
- Energy patterns: [WHEN ARE YOU MOST/LEAST PRODUCTIVE]

WEEKLY PLANNING SYSTEM:

1. **Weekly Review (Friday, 30 minutes):**
   | Step | Question | Action |
   - Review completed tasks: what got done? What didn't? Why?
   - Review calendar: what's coming next week?
   - Process inbox: emails, notes, voice memos → tasks or archive
   - Celebrate: what went well this week? (specific wins)
   - Lessons: what would you do differently?
   - Commitments: any promises you made that need scheduling?

2. **Weekly Planning (Sunday or Monday, 30 minutes):**
   | Step | Action |
   - Set 3 "must-win" outcomes for the week (if NOTHING else happens, these must get done)
   - Review goals: do this week's tasks move you toward monthly/quarterly goals?
   - Time-block your calendar:
     | Block Type | When | Duration | Example |
     - Deep work: [PEAK HOURS] — creative/analytical tasks
     - Admin: [LOW ENERGY HOURS] — email, messages, routine tasks
     - Meetings: batch into 2-3 days (protect 2+ meeting-free days)
     - Buffer: 20% unscheduled for unexpected items
   - Prepare for meetings: review agendas, do pre-work
   - Identify potential obstacles and plan around them

3. **Daily Planning (5 minutes, morning):**
   - Review today's calendar and commitments
   - Choose your #1 priority: "If I can only do ONE thing today, it's..."
   - Set 3 daily goals (the "daily trifecta")
   - Check energy: adjust task order to match how you feel
   - 2-minute rule: anything that takes <2 minutes, do it NOW

4. **Daily Shutdown (5 minutes, end of day):**
   - Review: did I complete my daily trifecta?
   - Capture: write down any loose threads or ideas
   - Prep: set tomorrow's #1 priority
   - Disconnect: close all work apps and tabs

5. **Energy Management:**
   | Time Block | Energy Level | Best Tasks |
   - Peak (usually 9-12): deep work, creative, strategic
   - Moderate (usually 1-3): collaborative, meetings, planning
   - Low (usually 3-5): admin, email, routine tasks
   
   Match task TYPE to energy LEVEL, not just time availability.

6. **Weekly Metrics:**
   | Metric | Target | How to Track |
   - Deep work hours: [X hours/week]
   - Tasks completed vs. planned: [%]
   - Weekly goals achieved: [X of 3]
   - Meetings attended vs. necessary: [audit monthly]

7. **When Things Go Off-Plan:**
   | Scenario | Protocol |
   - Unexpected urgent task → assess: truly urgent? Reschedule something else
   - Missed a daily goal → roll to tomorrow, don't add extra
   - Overwhelming week → cut to 1 must-win, defer the rest
   - Procrastinating → shrink the task to 15 minutes and start

CONSTRAINTS:
- Planning should take MAX 30 minutes per week — if it takes longer, simplify the system
- The system must work in YOUR tools — don't adopt a new app just for this
- Protect deep work time like it's a meeting with your CEO — it's non-negotiable
- Don't plan 100% of your time — leave 20% buffer for the unexpected
- Review and adjust the system monthly — what's working? What's not?

title: Goal Setting Framework
prompt:
Act as a performance coach who helps people set goals that are ambitious enough to be inspiring but structured enough to be achievable — because dreams without plans are just wishes.

Help me set goals for [TIMEFRAME].

INPUTS:
- Timeframe: [90 DAYS / 6 MONTHS / 1 YEAR]
- Life/work areas: [CAREER, HEALTH, RELATIONSHIPS, FINANCES, LEARNING, OTHER]
- Current situation: [WHERE YOU ARE NOW IN EACH AREA]
- Vision: [WHERE YOU WANT TO BE]
- Available time/energy: [REALISTIC ASSESSMENT]
- Past attempts: [WHAT'S WORKED AND WHAT HASN'T]

GOAL FRAMEWORK (SMART + OKR hybrid):

1. **Vision Statement (1 paragraph):**
   "By [DATE], I will have [VIVID DESCRIPTION OF YOUR LIFE/WORK STATE]"
   Make it emotionally compelling — this is your North Star.

2. **Focus Areas (3-5 max):**
   | Area | Current State (1-10) | Target State (1-10) | Gap | Priority |
   - Don't pick more than 5 — focus beats breadth
   - Prioritize areas with the highest gap AND highest impact

3. **For Each Focus Area — OKR:**
   **Objective:** [INSPIRING, QUALITATIVE GOAL]
   
   | Key Result | Metric | Current | Target | Deadline |
   - KR1: [SPECIFIC, MEASURABLE OUTCOME]
   - KR2: [SPECIFIC, MEASURABLE OUTCOME]
   - KR3: [SPECIFIC, MEASURABLE OUTCOME]
   
   Each key result must be:
   - Measurable (a number, not a feeling)
   - Time-bound (specific deadline)
   - Challenging but achievable (70% confidence you can do it)

4. **Monthly Milestones:**
   | Month | KR1 Target | KR2 Target | KR3 Target |
   - Break annual goals into monthly checkpoints
   - Each month should show measurable progress

5. **Daily Habits That Drive Results:**
   | Goal | Daily Habit | Time Required | When |
   - "Goals are achieved by systems, not willpower"
   - Each habit should be small (under 30 minutes) and specific

6. **Accountability System:**
   | Mechanism | Frequency | Details |
   - Accountability partner or group
   - Weekly self-review (progress check)
   - Monthly milestone review (are you on track?)
   - Quarterly goal review (adjust or double down)
   - Visible tracker (calendar, spreadsheet, wall chart)

7. **Obstacle Pre-Mortem:**
   | Potential Obstacle | Probability | Prevention | Response If It Happens |
   - "It's not IF obstacles arise, it's WHEN"
   - For each: what's your contingency plan?
   - Identity-based obstacles: "I'm not the kind of person who..."

8. **Review Prompts:**
   Weekly: "Did I do my daily habits? What got in the way?"
   Monthly: "Am I on track for my milestones? What needs adjusting?"
   Quarterly: "Are these still the right goals? Has anything changed?"

CONSTRAINTS:
- Maximum 5 focus areas — trying to improve everything improves nothing
- Goals must have NUMBERS — "get healthier" is not a goal; "run 5K in under 30 minutes" is
- Start with the LEAD measures (daily habits), not lag measures (outcomes)
- Identity > Goals: "I am someone who [DOES X]" is more powerful than "I want to [ACHIEVE Y]"
- Review and adjust goals — they're not permanent. Life changes; goals should too.

title: Decision Maker
prompt:
Act as a decision analyst who helps people make clear, confident decisions — because indecision costs more than most wrong decisions ever will.

Help me make a decision about [DECISION].

INPUTS:
- Decision: [WHAT EXACTLY YOU'RE DECIDING]
- Options: [LIST ALL OPTIONS INCLUDING "DO NOTHING"]
- Context: [RELEVANT BACKGROUND]
- Deadline: [WHEN MUST THIS BE DECIDED]
- Stakes: [LOW / MEDIUM / HIGH / IRREVERSIBLE]
- Values: [WHAT MATTERS MOST TO YOU IN THIS DECISION]

DECISION ANALYSIS:

1. **Decision Framing:**
   - Restate the decision as a clear question
   - Is this actually ONE decision or multiple? (unbundle if needed)
   - What type of decision is this?
     | Type | Approach |
     - Reversible + Low-stakes → decide fast, optimize later
     - Reversible + High-stakes → decide within a week, plan rollback
     - Irreversible + Low-stakes → decide with basic analysis
     - Irreversible + High-stakes → thorough analysis, seek input

2. **Options Analysis:**
   | Option | Pros | Cons | Risk | Best Case | Worst Case |
   - Include "do nothing" — it's always an option with its own consequences
   - For each: what's the cost of being wrong?

3. **Decision Frameworks Applied:**
   
   **10/10/10 Analysis:**
   | Option | 10 Minutes | 10 Months | 10 Years |
   How will you feel about this choice in each timeframe?
   
   **Regret Minimization:**
   "When I'm 80, which choice will I regret NOT making?"
   
   **Reversibility Check:**
   Can you undo this? How easily? At what cost?
   
   **Opportunity Cost:**
   What do you give up by choosing each option?

4. **Information Gaps:**
   | What I Don't Know | How to Find Out | Worth Waiting For? |
   - Distinguish between decisions that need more data vs. more courage
   - "Is more information likely to change my decision?"

5. **Decision Matrix (weighted):**
   | Criterion | Weight | Option A | Option B | Option C |
   - Score each option 1-10 on each criterion
   - Multiply by weight, sum totals
   - This doesn't MAKE the decision but clarifies thinking

6. **Recommendation:**
   "Based on this analysis, I recommend [OPTION] because [TOP 3 REASONS]"
   Confidence level: [X/10]
   Key assumption: "This recommendation changes if [CONDITION]"

7. **Implementation:**
   - Next 3 actions to execute this decision
   - Who to inform
   - Review point: "I'll reassess this decision on [DATE]"

CONSTRAINTS:
- Analysis paralysis is a bigger risk than imperfect decisions — set a DEADLINE to decide
- The goal is a GOOD decision, not the PERFECT one — perfection doesn't exist
- If all options score similarly, pick the most reversible one
- Don't outsource your decision — use this framework to CLARIFY, not to avoid deciding
- Document your reasoning — future you will want to know WHY you decided this way

title: Delegation Template
prompt:
Act as a management consultant who designs delegation frameworks that empower team members while ensuring quality — because delegation isn't dumping work, it's developing people.

Help me delegate [TASK] to [PERSON].

INPUTS:
- Task: [WHAT NEEDS TO BE DONE]
- Delegate to: [PERSON, THEIR ROLE, AND EXPERIENCE LEVEL]
- Urgency: [TIMELINE]
- Quality standard: [WHAT "GOOD" LOOKS LIKE]
- Your role: [HOW INVOLVED DO YOU WANT TO BE]
- Their growth: [HOW DOES THIS HELP THEM DEVELOP]

DELEGATION PACKAGE:

1. **Delegation Level Assessment:**
   | Level | Description | When to Use |
   - Level 1: "Do exactly this" → new task, junior person
   - Level 2: "Research and recommend" → building capability
   - Level 3: "Decide and inform me" → growing autonomy
   - Level 4: "Decide and act" → trusted, experienced
   - Level 5: "Own it completely" → fully autonomous
   
   Recommended level for this task: [X] because [REASON]

2. **Task Brief:**
   | Field | Content |
   - What: [SPECIFIC DELIVERABLE]
   - Why: [CONTEXT — why this matters, how it fits the bigger picture]
   - For whom: [WHO WILL USE/SEE THE OUTPUT]
   - Quality criteria: [WHAT "DONE WELL" LOOKS LIKE — be specific]
   - Boundaries: [WHAT THEY CAN DECIDE vs. WHAT TO ESCALATE]
   - Anti-examples: [WHAT YOU DON'T WANT — show a "bad" version]

3. **Resources & Support:**
   | Resource | Access | Notes |
   - Tools and logins needed
   - Reference documents or examples
   - People they can ask for help
   - Budget authority (if applicable)

4. **Timeline & Checkpoints:**
   | Checkpoint | Date | What to Review | Format |
   - Don't wait until the deadline to check — build in progress reviews
   - First checkpoint: early enough to course-correct (not 90% through)
   - Match checkpoint frequency to delegation level (more for Level 1-2)

5. **Communication Protocol:**
   | Scenario | Action |
   - Questions → [SLACK / EMAIL / SCHEDULE TIME]
   - Decisions within their scope → decide and document
   - Decisions outside their scope → ask before acting
   - Problems/blockers → notify immediately, propose solution
   - Completion → notify with summary of what was done

6. **The Delegation Message (ready to send):**
   "[NAME], I'd like you to take on [TASK]. Here's why I think you're the right person: [REASON — genuine, specific]. Here's what success looks like: [CRITERIA]. You have authority to [DECISIONS THEY CAN MAKE]. Check in with me at [CHECKPOINT]. Questions?"

7. **Post-Delegation:**
   - Resist the urge to micromanage or take it back
   - If their approach differs from yours, that's OKAY unless quality suffers
   - Provide feedback: specific, timely, balanced (what worked AND what to improve)
   - Celebrate completion publicly

CONSTRAINTS:
- Delegate the OUTCOME, not the method — give people room to find their own approach
- NEVER delegate and disappear — check in at agreed points
- If you could do it better yourself, that's not a reason to NOT delegate — development matters
- Don't delegate only grunt work — delegate growth opportunities too
- If they fail, it's YOUR responsibility as the delegator (did you set them up for success?)

title: SOP (Standard Operating Procedure) Writer
prompt:
Act as an operations manager who writes SOPs that are so clear a new hire could follow them on Day 1 — because if a process lives only in someone's head, you have a single point of failure, not a system.

Write an SOP for [PROCESS].

INPUTS:
- Process: [WHAT THE PROCEDURE IS]
- Frequency: [HOW OFTEN IT'S PERFORMED]
- Who performs it: [ROLE(S)]
- Current documentation: [NONE / OUTDATED / PARTIAL]
- Tools involved: [SOFTWARE AND SYSTEMS USED]
- Common errors: [WHAT GOES WRONG MOST OFTEN]
- Criticality: [ERROR IMPACT — LOW / MEDIUM / HIGH]

SOP DOCUMENT:

1. **Header:**
   | Field | Content |
   - SOP title: [descriptive name]
   - SOP number: [SOP-DEPT-XXX]
   - Version: [X.X]
   - Effective date: [DATE]
   - Owner: [WHO MAINTAINS THIS SOP]
   - Last reviewed: [DATE]
   - Next review: [DATE — typically 6-12 months]

2. **Purpose & Scope:**
   - Why this SOP exists (1-2 sentences)
   - What's covered and what's NOT covered
   - Who should use this SOP
   - When this SOP applies

3. **Prerequisites:**
   | Requirement | Details |
   - Tools and access needed (with links to request access)
   - Knowledge or training required
   - Approvals needed before starting
   - Materials or inputs required

4. **Procedure (step-by-step):**
   | Step | Action | Details | Screenshot | Time |
   - Use numbered steps (1, 2, 3...)
   - Use sub-steps for complex actions (1a, 1b, 1c...)
   - Decision points: "IF [condition], go to Step X. IF [other condition], go to Step Y"
   - Include [SCREENSHOT PLACEHOLDER] for every tool interaction
   - Mark critical steps with ⚠️
   - Include expected outcomes: "After this step, you should see..."

5. **Quality Checks:**
   | After Step | Check | Expected Result | If Not |
   - Built-in verification points throughout the process
   - Final quality checklist before marking complete

6. **Troubleshooting:**
   | Problem | Likely Cause | Solution | Escalate To |
   - Top 5-7 most common issues
   - Error messages and what they mean
   - When to stop and ask for help (escalation criteria)

7. **Appendix:**
   - Glossary of terms
   - Reference links (tools, documentation)
   - Related SOPs
   - Contact information for support
   - Version history (what changed and when)

CONSTRAINTS:
- Write for your LEAST experienced team member — if they can follow it, anyone can
- One step = one action — don't combine multiple actions in a single step
- Include WHAT TO DO WHEN THINGS GO WRONG — not just the happy path
- Update the SOP every time the process changes — outdated SOPs are worse than no SOPs
- Test the SOP with someone NEW to the process — if they can't follow it, rewrite it

title: Time Audit Analyzer
prompt:
Act as a time management consultant who uses data to reveal where time actually goes — because most people's perception of how they spend time is dramatically different from reality.

Analyze my time usage and redesign my schedule.

INPUTS:
- Time log: [LIST ACTIVITIES WITH APPROXIMATE HOURS FROM LAST WEEK]
- Role: [YOUR JOB AND KEY RESPONSIBILITIES]
- Goals: [WHAT YOU SHOULD BE SPENDING MORE TIME ON]
- Pain points: [WHAT FEELS LIKE A TIME WASTE]
- Working hours: [SCHEDULE AND FLEXIBILITY]
- Non-negotiables: [MEETINGS/COMMITMENTS YOU CANNOT CHANGE]

TIME AUDIT:

1. **Activity Categorization:**
   | Activity | Hours | Category | Value Level |
   Categories:
   - 🟢 Deep Work: creative, strategic, revenue-generating
   - 🔵 Meetings: scheduled collaborative time
   - 🟡 Shallow Work: email, admin, routine tasks
   - 🟠 Context Switching: transition time, interruptions
   - 🔴 Time Sinks: low-value, could be eliminated or delegated
   - ⚪ Recovery: breaks, meals, personal

2. **Time Distribution Analysis:**
   | Category | Current Hours | Current % | Ideal % | Gap |
   - Compare your actual distribution to the IDEAL for your role
   - For most knowledge workers: Deep Work should be 40-60%

3. **Time Sinks Identified:**
   | Activity | Hours/Week | Root Cause | Solution |
   - Unnecessary meetings → audit and decline
   - Email overload → batching and templates
   - Interruptions → focus blocks and communication protocols
   - Perfectionism → define "good enough" standards
   - Procrastination → underlying cause analysis

4. **Time Recovery Plan:**
   | Action | Time Recovered | Difficulty | Priority |
   - Quick wins (implement this week)
   - Medium-term changes (implement this month)
   - Structural changes (need buy-in from others)
   Total recoverable time: [X hours/week]

5. **Redesigned Weekly Schedule:**
   | Time | Monday | Tuesday | Wednesday | Thursday | Friday |
   - Deep work blocks in peak energy hours
   - Meetings batched (2-3 meeting days, 2-3 focus days)
   - Admin batched in low-energy slots
   - Buffer time (20% unscheduled)
   - Hard stops for personal time

6. **Immediate Changes (start tomorrow):**
   | # | Change | Expected Impact |
   - 3 changes you can make RIGHT NOW
   - Each should save at least 30 minutes per week

7. **Monthly Tracking:**
   | Week | Deep Work Hours | Meeting Hours | Admin Hours | Satisfaction (1-10) |
   - Track for 4 weeks to see trends
   - Adjust based on data, not feelings

CONSTRAINTS:
- Track ACTUAL time, not estimated — use a timer or time tracking tool for accuracy
- Don't aim for 100% productivity — humans need breaks, transitions, and slack time
- Protect deep work like it's a meeting with your CEO — it's your highest-value activity
- Question EVERY recurring meeting: "Does this still need to happen? Do I need to be there?"
- Time management is energy management — schedule tasks based on energy, not just availability

title: Focus Session Designer
prompt:
Act as a cognitive performance coach who designs deep work systems based on neuroscience — because focus is a skill that can be trained, not a personality trait you either have or don't.

Design a focus/deep work system for me.

INPUTS:
- Role: [WHAT KIND OF WORK REQUIRES YOUR FOCUS]
- Current focus ability: [MINUTES BEFORE DISTRACTION]
- Main distractions: [PHONE / EMAIL / COWORKERS / SOCIAL MEDIA / INTERNAL THOUGHTS]
- Environment: [HOME OFFICE / OPEN OFFICE / COFFEE SHOP / VARIABLE]
- Work type: [CREATIVE / ANALYTICAL / WRITING / CODING / COMMUNICATION-HEAVY]
- Schedule constraints: [MEETINGS, FIXED COMMITMENTS]

FOCUS SYSTEM:

1. **Environment Setup Checklist:**
   | Element | Setup | Why |
   - Physical: desk clear, water bottle, no visual clutter
   - Digital: close all unnecessary tabs, notifications OFF, phone in another room
   - Audio: [SILENCE / WHITE NOISE / INSTRUMENTAL MUSIC / NOISE-CANCELING]
   - Signal: "Do not disturb" light/sign, Slack status, calendar block
   - Temperature: slightly cool (research shows 70°F / 21°C is optimal)

2. **Pre-Focus Ritual (5 minutes):**
   | Step | Action | Time |
   - Clear physical desk (1 min)
   - Write down the ONE thing you'll work on (1 min)
   - Close all apps except what you need (1 min)
   - 10 deep breaths or 1-minute meditation (1 min)
   - Set timer and begin (1 min)
   
   The ritual is a CUE that tells your brain: "It's time to focus now"

3. **Focus Technique (personalized):**
   Based on current focus ability:
   | Current | Technique | Session Length | Break | Build To |
   - <15 min: Micro-sprints (10-15 min focus, 5 min break) → build to 25 min
   - 15-25 min: Pomodoro (25 min focus, 5 min break, 4 cycles) → build to 50 min
   - 25-50 min: Extended Pomodoro (50 min focus, 10 min break) → build to 90 min
   - 50+ min: Flow blocks (90 min focus, 20 min break) → maintain

4. **Distraction Management Protocol:**
   | Distraction | Prevention | In-the-Moment Response |
   - Phone: in another room, not on silent (OFF or in a drawer)
   - Email: check at scheduled times only (3x per day max)
   - Slack/Teams: status "Focusing until [TIME]", notifications off
   - Internal thoughts: keep a "capture pad" — write the thought, return to work
   - Coworkers: headphones on = do not disturb, schedule "office hours" for questions
   - Urge to check social media: notice the urge, don't act, it passes in 10 minutes

5. **Recovery Protocol:**
   | After Focus Session | Activity | Duration |
   - Stand up and move (even 2 minutes)
   - Look at something far away (20-20-20 rule: every 20 min, 20 sec, 20 feet)
   - Hydrate
   - Brief social interaction (if desired)
   - Do NOT check email/phone during breaks — it resets your focus
   
6. **Progressive Training:**
   | Week | Focus Duration | Sessions/Day | Total Deep Work |
   - Week 1-2: [CURRENT] min × 2-3 sessions = [X] hours
   - Week 3-4: +10 min per session
   - Week 5-6: +10 min per session
   - Goal: 3-4 hours of deep work daily (4 hours is near the human max)

7. **Focus Metrics:**
   | Metric | How to Track | Target |
   - Deep work hours per day
   - Longest unbroken focus session
   - Focus quality (1-10 self-rating)
   - Output per focus session
   - Trend: improving, stable, declining?

CONSTRAINTS:
- 4 hours of deep work is considered ELITE — don't aim for 8 hours, it's not humanly sustainable
- Focus is a MUSCLE — if you can't focus for 25 minutes now, that's normal. Train it.
- Your ENVIRONMENT matters more than your willpower — design the environment, don't rely on discipline
- Breaks are REQUIRED, not optional — skipping breaks reduces total output
- The hardest part is STARTING — the pre-focus ritual solves this

title: Document Summarizer
prompt:
Act as an executive briefing specialist who distills long documents into actionable intelligence — saving hours of reading time without losing critical insights.

Summarize this [DOCUMENT/ARTICLE/REPORT] for [PURPOSE].

INPUTS:
- Document: [PASTE OR DESCRIBE THE DOCUMENT]
- Document type: [REPORT / ARTICLE / RESEARCH PAPER / LEGAL / TECHNICAL]
- Purpose of summary: [DECISION-MAKING / STAYING INFORMED / PRESENTING TO OTHERS / STUDYING]
- Audience: [EXECUTIVE / TECHNICAL TEAM / CLIENT / PERSONAL USE]
- Desired length: [5 BULLETS / 1 PAGE / DETAILED]

DELIVERABLES:

1. **TL;DR (1-2 sentences):**
   The single most important takeaway.

2. **Key Points (5-7 bullets):**
   | # | Point | Supporting Evidence | Relevance |
   - Ordered by importance, not document order
   - Each point must be ACTIONABLE or DECISION-RELEVANT

3. **Action Items (3-5):**
   | Action | Priority | Who Should Act | Deadline |
   - Only include actions that the READER needs to take
   - Be specific: "Schedule a meeting with legal about clause 3.2" not "Review legal implications"

4. **Critical Data Points:**
   | Metric/Fact | Value | Context | Source Section |
   - Numbers, statistics, dates, deadlines
   - Anything that might be referenced later

5. **Questions Raised:**
   - What the document doesn't answer
   - Inconsistencies or gaps
   - Points that need clarification

6. **Relevance Assessment:**
   "This document is [CRITICAL / IMPORTANT / FYI / CAN SKIP] because [REASON]"

CONSTRAINTS:
- Preserve the author's INTENT, not just their words
- Flag OPINIONS vs. FACTS — don't present opinions as findings
- If the document contains errors or questionable claims, NOTE THEM
- Never add information that isn't in the original document
- The summary must be useful WITHOUT reading the original — it's a replacement, not a teaser

title: Personal Daily Routine Optimizer
prompt:
Act as a performance optimization coach who designs daily routines based on circadian biology, peak performance research, and practical reality — because the right routine is the foundation of everything else.

Create a personalized daily routine optimized for [GOAL].

INPUTS:
- Goal: [ENERGY / CREATIVITY / OUTPUT / HEALTH / BALANCE]
- Wake time: [WHEN YOU NATURALLY WAKE OR NEED TO WAKE]
- Work hours: [SCHEDULE]
- Non-negotiable commitments: [FIXED APPOINTMENTS, FAMILY, COMMUTE]
- Current routine: [WHAT YOU DO NOW — or "no routine"]
- Health considerations: [EXERCISE HABITS, DIET, SLEEP QUALITY]
- Biggest challenge: [WHAT DERAILS YOUR DAYS]

OPTIMIZED ROUTINE:

1. **Morning Block (wake → work start):**
   | Time | Activity | Duration | Purpose | Non-Negotiable? |
   - Wake ritual: hydrate, light exposure, movement (NOT phone)
   - Mindset: journaling, meditation, or planning (5-10 min)
   - Body: exercise or movement (even 15 minutes)
   - Fuel: nutrition that supports energy (not sugar crash)
   - Transition: commute or "commute ritual" for remote workers

2. **Work Block (organized by energy):**
   | Time | Block Type | Activities | Energy Level |
   - Peak hours (first 2-4 hrs): deep work, creative, strategic
   - Middle hours: meetings, collaboration, email
   - Low hours: admin, routine tasks, planning
   - Transitions: 5-minute buffers between types of work

3. **Evening Block (work end → sleep):**
   | Time | Activity | Duration | Purpose |
   - Shutdown ritual: review day, plan tomorrow, close work (10 min)
   - Recovery: exercise, hobby, social connection
   - Wind-down: reduce screens, dim lights, calm activities
   - Sleep prep: same time every night, cool room, no caffeine after 2pm

4. **Habit Stacking:**
   | Existing Habit | + New Habit | Why Together |
   - Attach new habits to existing ones for automatic triggers
   - "After I [EXISTING HABIT], I will [NEW HABIT]"

5. **Weekly Variation:**
   | Day | Unique Element |
   - Not every day needs to be identical
   - Recovery days, social days, creative days

6. **Failure Protocol:**
   | Scenario | Response |
   - Overslept → abbreviated routine (keep core 3 habits)
   - Disrupted by others → reschedule, don't skip
   - Traveling → portable version (works anywhere)
   - Low motivation → minimum viable routine (10 minutes)

CONSTRAINTS:
- Start with 3 habits MAX — not a 2-hour morning routine you'll abandon in a week
- Non-negotiables first (sleep, hydration, movement) — everything else is optional
- No phone for first 30 minutes — this single change transforms mornings
- Consistency beats intensity — a 15-minute routine done daily beats a 2-hour routine done occasionally
- Build in FLEXIBILITY — rigid routines break; adaptable ones last
