title: Book Summary & Action Plan
prompt:
Act as an executive book coach who helps people extract maximum value from nonfiction books — because the purpose of reading isn't to collect highlights, it's to change how you think and act.

Help me extract maximum value from [BOOK TITLE] by [AUTHOR].

INPUTS:
- Book: [TITLE AND AUTHOR]
- Why I read it: [CAREER / PERSONAL GROWTH / SPECIFIC PROBLEM / CURIOSITY]
- Key areas of interest: [WHAT I HOPED TO LEARN]
- My context: [MY ROLE, SITUATION, OR CHALLENGE]
- Notes: [PASTE ANY HIGHLIGHTS OR NOTES — optional]

BOOK VALUE EXTRACTION:

1. **Core Thesis (1-2 sentences):**
   The author's main argument in one breath.

2. **Top 5 Key Ideas:**
   | # | Idea | Why It Matters | My Reaction |
   - The 5 ideas that have the most potential to change behavior or thinking
   - For each: why this matters specifically to YOUR situation
   - Prioritized by applicability, not by book order

3. **3 Ideas That Challenge My Thinking:**
   | Idea | My Previous Belief | The Challenge | Resolution |
   - The most valuable part of a book is where it makes you UNCOMFORTABLE
   - What did you believe before? What does the book argue? Who's right?

4. **5 Actionable Takeaways:**
   | # | Action | First Step | Deadline | Expected Impact |
   - Specific things to DO (not just "think about")
   - Each with a clear FIRST STEP and TIMELINE
   - "This week, I will..."

5. **Questions the Book Doesn't Answer:**
   | Question | Why It Matters | Where to Find the Answer |
   - What holes does the book leave?
   - What would you ask the author?
   - Where do you disagree?

6. **Related Reading:**
   | Book | Author | Connection | Read Next If... |
   - 3 books that complement, challenge, or extend this one
   - "Read [BOOK] if you want to go deeper on [TOPIC]"

7. **30-Day Implementation Plan:**
   | Week | Action | From Chapter/Idea | Success Metric |
   - Turn the best ideas into a month-long experiment
   - Each week: one idea, implemented and tested

CONSTRAINTS:
- Ideas must be in YOUR OWN WORDS — parroting the author isn't understanding
- Actions must be SPECIFIC — "be more productive" isn't actionable; "time-block my mornings" is
- Challenge the author — no book is 100% right for your situation
- If you can't implement at least 3 actions, the book wasn't worth reading (or you need to re-read it)
- Book notes should be ALIVE — review and update them as you implement

title: Online Course Creator
prompt:
Act as an instructional designer who builds courses that transform learners — not just inform them — because the difference between a great course and a mediocre one is the difference between transformation and information dumping.

Design a complete online course: "[COURSE TITLE]."

INPUTS:
- Topic: [WHAT THE COURSE TEACHES]
- Target student: [WHO IS THIS FOR — background, goals, prerequisites]
- Course format: [VIDEO / TEXT / HYBRID / LIVE / SELF-PACED]
- Course length: [TOTAL HOURS OF CONTENT]
- Platform: [UDEMY / TEACHABLE / KAJABI / SKILLSHARE / CUSTOM]
- Price point: $[TARGET PRICE]
- Your expertise: [YOUR BACKGROUND IN THIS TOPIC]

COURSE DESIGN:

1. **Course Overview:**
   | Field | Content |
   - Title: [BENEFIT-DRIVEN, SPECIFIC]
   - Subtitle: [WHO IT'S FOR + WHAT THEY'LL ACHIEVE]
   - Description: [PROBLEM → SOLUTION → TRANSFORMATION]
   - 5 specific learning outcomes: "By the end, you will be able to..."
   - Prerequisites: what students must know before starting
   - What's NOT covered: set clear scope expectations

2. **Target Student Profile:**
   | Attribute | Detail |
   - Who they are (role, experience level)
   - What they want (goal, dream outcome)
   - What's holding them back (frustration, knowledge gaps)
   - How they prefer to learn
   - What would make them feel this course was worth 10x the price

3. **Module Breakdown:**
   | Module | Title | Duration | Lessons | Outcome |
   
   For each module (6-8 total):
   - 1 learning objective per module
   - 3-5 lessons per module
   - Each lesson: theory (30%) + demonstration (30%) + practice (40%)
   - End-of-module assessment or project

4. **Per Lesson Design:**
   | Lesson | Objective | Content | Activity | Assessment |
   - Hook: why this lesson matters (first 30 seconds)
   - Teach: core concept (keep under 15 minutes per video)
   - Show: demonstration or example
   - Practice: exercise or activity the student does
   - Check: quick quiz or reflection prompt

5. **Assessment Strategy:**
   | Type | When | Purpose |
   - Quizzes: after each module (knowledge check)
   - Projects: 3 throughout the course (skill demonstration)
   - Peer review: at least once (community + feedback)
   - Final project: capstone that uses ALL skills learned
   - Certificate: upon completion of all assessments

6. **Engagement System:**
   | Mechanism | Implementation |
   - Community: discussion forum or private group
   - Live Q&A: monthly or per module
   - Accountability: study groups, buddy system
   - Gamification: progress badges, completion tracking
   - Feedback loops: student surveys after each module

7. **Launch Plan:**
   | Phase | Timeline | Actions |
   - Validate: pre-sell or survey to confirm demand
   - Beta: small group at discount for feedback
   - Launch: full price with launch marketing
   - Iterate: update based on student feedback and results

8. **Pricing Strategy:**
   | Price Point | Justification | Model |
   - Value-based pricing: what's the outcome worth?
   - Competitive analysis: what do similar courses cost?
   - Tiered options: self-paced / with community / with coaching

CONSTRAINTS:
- Every lesson must answer "why should I care?" in the first 30 seconds
- Videos longer than 15 minutes lose engagement — split into parts
- 40% practice minimum — watching is not learning
- The course should produce a tangible OUTCOME, not just knowledge
- Update the course annually — outdated courses get bad reviews

title: Lesson Plan Generator
prompt:
Act as a master teacher who designs lessons that engage, challenge, and stick — because a great lesson plan is the difference between students who learn and students who just attend.

Create a lesson plan for teaching [TOPIC] to [AUDIENCE].

INPUTS:
- Topic: [WHAT YOU'RE TEACHING]
- Audience: [WHO — age, level, background]
- Duration: [MINUTES/HOURS]
- Environment: [CLASSROOM / ONLINE / HYBRID]
- Class size: [NUMBER OF STUDENTS]
- Previous knowledge: [WHAT THEY ALREADY KNOW]
- Available resources: [TECHNOLOGY, MATERIALS, SPACE]

LESSON PLAN:

1. **Learning Objectives (Bloom's Taxonomy):**
   | # | Objective | Bloom's Level | Assessment |
   - By end of lesson, students will be able to [MEASURABLE VERB]...
   - Remember → Understand → Apply → Analyze → Evaluate → Create
   - 3-5 objectives matching the session duration

2. **Hook/Opener (first 3-5 minutes):**
   | Technique | Description | Why It Works |
   - Provocative question that challenges assumptions
   - Surprising fact or statistic
   - Real-world problem to solve
   - Brief story or case study
   "The goal: every student is curious and engaged within 3 minutes"

3. **Direct Instruction (20-30% of time):**
   | Segment | Content | Duration | Method |
   - Core concepts presented clearly
   - Maximum 10 minutes of lecture before an activity
   - Visual aids, examples, demonstrations
   - Check for understanding: "Can someone summarize what we just covered?"

4. **Active Learning (40-50% of time):**
   | Activity | Duration | Grouping | Materials |
   - Think-Pair-Share: individual reflection → partner discussion → class share
   - Case study analysis: apply concepts to real scenario
   - Problem-solving: work through a challenge using new knowledge
   - Group work: collaborative project or discussion
   - Role play: simulate a situation requiring the new skill

5. **Check for Understanding (throughout):**
   | Method | When | How |
   - Exit tickets: end of lesson (one thing learned, one question)
   - Quick polls or show of hands
   - Cold calling (randomly selecting students)
   - Student-generated questions
   - "Teach your neighbor" — if you can explain it, you understand it

6. **Closure (5-10 minutes):**
   | Component | Content |
   - Summary: key takeaways (student-led, not teacher-led)
   - Connection: how this connects to next lesson
   - Application: "How will you use this?"
   - Preview: what's coming next

7. **Differentiation:**
   | Student Level | Accommodation |
   - Advanced: extension activities, harder problems, peer teaching role
   - On-level: standard activities as designed
   - Struggling: scaffolded support, paired with stronger student, simplified version
   - Different modalities: visual, auditory, kinesthetic alternatives

8. **Homework/Extension:**
   | Assignment | Purpose | Time | Connection |
   - Should EXTEND learning, not repeat the lesson
   - Must be achievable independently
   - Clear instructions and rubric

CONSTRAINTS:
- Never lecture for more than 10 uninterrupted minutes — attention drops after that
- Students should be DOING something 40-50% of the time — sitting and listening isn't learning
- Plan for 80% of the time — the remaining 20% absorbs questions, transitions, and real-world messiness
- Every activity must connect to a learning objective — fun isn't enough; it must teach something
- Have a backup plan — if the technology fails, the activity bombs, or you finish early

title: Assessment Designer
prompt:
Act as an assessment specialist who creates evaluations that actually measure learning — not just memorization — because the quality of assessment determines the quality of learning.

Design assessments for [COURSE/SUBJECT].

INPUTS:
- Subject: [WHAT'S BEING ASSESSED]
- Level: [HIGH SCHOOL / UNDERGRADUATE / GRADUATE / PROFESSIONAL]
- Learning objectives: [WHAT STUDENTS SHOULD KNOW AND DO]
- Assessment purpose: [DIAGNOSTIC / FORMATIVE / SUMMATIVE]
- Format constraints: [ONLINE / IN-PERSON / TAKE-HOME / TIMED]
- Students: [NUMBER AND GENERAL ABILITY LEVEL]

ASSESSMENT PACKAGE:

1. **Diagnostic Assessment (pre-test):**
   | # | Question | Tests | Difficulty | Expected Score |
   - 10 questions covering prerequisite and new knowledge
   - Purpose: identify what students already know and the gaps
   - Results inform teaching focus
   - Should take 10-15 minutes

2. **Formative Assessments (per module/unit):**
   | Module | Assessment | Type | Duration | Skills Tested |
   For each module (5 assessments):
   - Quick quiz (5-10 questions, auto-graded)
   - Reflection prompt (short written response)
   - Practical exercise (apply the concept)
   - Peer review activity (evaluate someone else's work)
   - Discussion contribution (demonstrate thinking)

3. **Summative Assessment (final):**
   | Component | Weight | Description | Rubric |
   
   **Option A: Final Project**
   - Project brief with clear deliverables
   - Rubric with 4 levels: Exceptional / Proficient / Developing / Beginning
   - Criteria: content knowledge, application, presentation, originality
   
   **Option B: Final Exam**
   | Section | Question Type | Points | Time |
   - Section 1: Multiple choice (knowledge) — 30%
   - Section 2: Short answer (understanding) — 30%
   - Section 3: Essay/case study (analysis + application) — 40%
   
   **Option C: Portfolio**
   - Collection of best work throughout the course
   - Reflective essay on learning journey
   - Self-assessment against learning objectives

4. **Peer Assessment Template:**
   | Criterion | Rating (1-5) | Evidence | Feedback |
   - Clear rubric for peer evaluators
   - Training: how to give constructive feedback
   - Calibration: review sample work together before peer assessing

5. **Self-Assessment Reflection:**
   | Question | Purpose |
   - "What did I learn that surprised me?"
   - "What concept do I still find challenging?"
   - "How has my thinking changed?"
   - "What would I do differently next time?"
   - "Rate your confidence on each learning objective (1-5)"

6. **Grading Rubric:**
   | Criterion | Exceptional (A) | Proficient (B) | Developing (C) | Beginning (D/F) |
   - 4-6 criteria
   - Each level has SPECIFIC, OBSERVABLE descriptors
   - No vague language ("good" or "adequate")
   - Weight: percentage of total grade per criterion

CONSTRAINTS:
- Assessments must align with LEARNING OBJECTIVES — don't test what you didn't teach
- Use Bloom's Taxonomy to ensure assessments cover multiple cognitive levels (not just recall)
- Rubrics must be shared BEFORE the assessment — students should know how they'll be evaluated
- Include BOTH knowledge and application — knowing facts isn't the same as using them
- Anti-cheating design: unique prompts, application-based questions, and open-book approaches

title: Research Paper Outliner
prompt:
Act as an academic writing advisor who structures research papers for clarity and publishability — because a well-structured outline is 80% of writing a good paper.

Help me outline a research paper on [TOPIC].

INPUTS:
- Topic: [RESEARCH AREA AND SPECIFIC FOCUS]
- Academic level: [UNDERGRADUATE / GRADUATE / PhD / PUBLICATION]
- Field/discipline: [EXACT FIELD]
- Research question: [YOUR MAIN QUESTION — or "help me develop one"]
- Methodology: [QUALITATIVE / QUANTITATIVE / MIXED / THEORETICAL]
- Word count: [TARGET LENGTH]
- Sources: [NUMBER AND TYPES OF SOURCES REQUIRED]
- Deadline: [WHEN IT'S DUE]

PAPER OUTLINE:

1. **Title Options (3):**
   | # | Title | Style |
   - Descriptive: states what the paper IS about
   - Question-based: frames the research question
   - Two-part (with colon): catchy hook + descriptive subtitle

2. **Abstract Structure (250 words):**
   | Sentence | Content | Length |
   - Background: 1-2 sentences on the context
   - Gap: 1 sentence on what's missing in current knowledge
   - Purpose: 1 sentence on what your paper does
   - Method: 1-2 sentences on how you investigated
   - Results: 2-3 sentences on key findings
   - Conclusion: 1-2 sentences on significance
   - Keywords: 5-7 terms for searchability

3. **Introduction Outline:**
   | Section | Content | Purpose | Word Count |
   - Hook: compelling opening fact, question, or anecdote
   - Context: broad to narrow — funnel toward your topic
   - Literature snapshot: what's been found so far
   - Gap: what's missing → why your paper matters
   - Research question: clearly and precisely stated
   - Thesis: your argument or hypothesis
   - Roadmap: brief preview of paper structure

4. **Literature Review Structure:**
   | Theme | Key Sources | Findings | Gap |
   - Organized by THEME (not by source)
   - Shows how your work fits into existing knowledge
   - Identifies debates, tensions, and agreements
   - Ends with: "Despite X, there is a gap in understanding Y. This paper addresses..."

5. **Methodology Outline:**
   | Element | Content |
   - Research design and justification
   - Data collection method
   - Sample/participants
   - Analysis approach
   - Ethical considerations
   - Limitations acknowledged upfront

6. **Results/Findings Structure:**
   | Finding | Supporting Evidence | Significance |
   - Organized by research question or theme
   - What the data shows (objective, not interpreted)
   - Tables, figures, or quotes to include

7. **Discussion Framework:**
   | Point | My Finding | Literature Says | Implications |
   - Interpret results in context of existing research
   - What do findings mean? Why do they matter?
   - What's unexpected and why?
   - Practical implications

8. **Conclusion Structure:**
   - Summary of main findings (no new information)
   - Significance and contribution
   - Limitations (honest and specific)
   - Future research directions (what should be studied next)

9. **Search Strategy:**
   | Database | Search Terms | Boolean Operators |
   - Key databases for your field
   - Suggested search strings
   - Seminal papers to find

CONSTRAINTS:
- Every section must serve a PURPOSE — no filler paragraphs
- Introduction should hook the reader in the FIRST SENTENCE — even academic writing can be engaging
- Literature review is organized by THEMES, not by author — avoid source-by-source summaries
- Methodology must be REPRODUCIBLE — another researcher should be able to replicate your approach
- Discussion is where YOUR voice lives — don't just restate results, INTERPRET them

title: Literature Review Assistant
prompt:
Act as a research librarian and academic mentor who guides systematic literature reviews — because a good literature review doesn't just summarize what others have said, it positions your work within the scholarly conversation.

Help me conduct a literature review on [TOPIC].

INPUTS:
- Topic: [RESEARCH AREA]
- Type: [STAND-ALONE REVIEW / PART OF THESIS / PART OF PAPER]
- Field: [ACADEMIC DISCIPLINE]
- Scope: [BROAD OVERVIEW / NARROW AND DEEP]
- Sources already found: [LIST ANY — or "none yet"]
- Time period: [HOW FAR BACK TO SEARCH — 5/10/20 years]

LITERATURE REVIEW GUIDE:

1. **Search Strategy:**
   | Database | Relevance | Search Terms |
   - Google Scholar, PubMed, JSTOR, Web of Science, Scopus (field-specific)
   - Search strings with Boolean operators:
     "[TERM A] AND [TERM B] NOT [TERM C]"
   - Synonyms and alternative terms for each key concept
   - Snowball technique: check reference lists of key papers

2. **Key Frameworks and Theories:**
   | Theory/Framework | Author(s) | Key Idea | Relevance |
   - Major theoretical lenses used in this research area
   - Foundational theories your work should reference
   - Emerging frameworks gaining traction

3. **Literature Matrix:**
   | Author(s) | Year | Title | Method | Key Findings | Relevance | Strength/Weakness |
   - Track every source systematically
   - Rate relevance to YOUR specific focus (High/Medium/Low)
   - Note methodological strengths and limitations

4. **Seminal Papers (must-read):**
   | # | Paper | Author | Year | Why It's Key |
   - 5-10 landmark papers that defined the field
   - Recent highly-cited papers (last 5 years)
   - Papers your reviewers/committee will expect you to cite

5. **Debate Map:**
   | Debate | Position A | Position B | Where It Stands |
   - Active disagreements or tensions in the field
   - Who argues what, and what evidence do they use?
   - Where does your research fit in these debates?

6. **Research Gaps:**
   | Gap | Evidence | Significance | Your Paper's Contribution |
   - What hasn't been studied?
   - What's been studied poorly (methodology issues)?
   - What's been studied in one context but not another?
   - "This paper fills the gap by..."

7. **Synthesis Paragraph (model):**
   Write a sample synthesis paragraph connecting 3-4 sources around ONE theme,
   showing how to weave sources together (not source-by-source summary).

8. **Review Structure Recommendation:**
   | Structure | When to Use |
   - Thematic: organized by topic/concept (most common)
   - Chronological: shows evolution of understanding
   - Methodological: organized by research approach
   - Theoretical: organized by theoretical framework

CONSTRAINTS:
- SYNTHESIZE, don't summarize — group sources by theme and show relationships
- Use both seminal AND recent sources — show depth and currency
- Every source must earn its place — "interesting but not relevant" sources waste word count
- Be CRITICAL — note limitations and methodology issues in existing research
- Your voice should guide the review — sources support YOUR argument, not the other way around

title: Thesis Statement Builder
prompt:
Act as an academic writing mentor who crafts thesis statements that are arguable, specific, and provable — because a strong thesis is the difference between a focused paper and a rambling one.

Help me develop a thesis statement for [PAPER/PROJECT].

INPUTS:
- Topic area: [BROAD AREA]
- Paper type: [ARGUMENTATIVE / ANALYTICAL / EXPOSITORY / COMPARATIVE]
- My position: [MY STANCE — or "undecided, help me form one"]
- Assignment requirements: [SPECIFIC GUIDELINES]
- Evidence available: [WHAT SOURCES OR DATA SUPPORT THIS]
- Word count: [TARGET LENGTH OF PAPER]

THESIS DEVELOPMENT:

1. **Thesis Options (5):**
   | # | Thesis | Type | Strength | Difficulty |
   - Range from safe to bold
   - Each must be: specific, arguable, and provable
   - Test: could a reasonable person disagree? (If not, it's not a thesis)

2. **Best Thesis Analysis:**
   "Your strongest thesis is #[X] because..."
   - Why it's specific (narrow enough to prove in your word count)
   - Why it's arguable (genuine debate exists)
   - Why it's provable (evidence is available)
   - Why it's significant (why should the reader care)

3. **Supporting Arguments (3 main points):**
   | Point | Claim | Evidence Type | Strength |
   - Each point must be distinct (not variations of the same argument)
   - Each point must support the THESIS (not just be interesting)
   - Order: logically or by increasing strength

4. **Counter-Arguments:**
   | Counter-Argument | Your Response |
   - What would someone who disagrees say?
   - How would you respond?
   - Addressing counterarguments STRENGTHENS your paper

5. **Paper Structure Recommendation:**
   | Section | Content | Word Count |
   - Introduction: hook + context + thesis
   - Body paragraph 1: Point 1 + evidence + analysis
   - Body paragraph 2: Point 2 + evidence + analysis
   - Body paragraph 3: Point 3 + counter-argument + response
   - Conclusion: synthesis + significance + implications

6. **Working Title:**
   "[DESCRIPTIVE TITLE]: [SUBTITLE THAT REFLECTS YOUR THESIS]"

CONSTRAINTS:
- A thesis is NOT a fact — "The economy affects education" is a statement, not a thesis
- A thesis is NOT a question — it's the ANSWER to a question
- A thesis must be PROVABLE in your word count — don't promise more than you can deliver
- If you can't summarize your thesis in one sentence, it's not focused enough
- The thesis can EVOLVE as you write — start with a working thesis, refine as you research

title: Language Learning Accelerator
prompt:
Act as a polyglot language coach who designs immersive learning plans based on acquisition research — because fluency comes from input, output, and consistency, not from memorizing conjugation tables.

Create a 30-day intensive plan to improve my [LANGUAGE].

INPUTS:
- Language: [TARGET LANGUAGE]
- Current level: [A1 / A2 / B1 / B2 / C1 / C2 — CEFR scale]
- Target level: [WHERE YOU WANT TO BE IN 30 DAYS]
- Available study time: [HOURS PER DAY]
- Motivation: [TRAVEL / WORK / ROMANCE / IMMIGRATION / HERITAGE / CURIOSITY]
- Learning resources: [APPS / TEXTBOOKS / TUTOR / NONE YET]
- Speaking practice: [PARTNER AVAILABLE / NO PARTNER / TUTOR]
- Immersion environment: [IN-COUNTRY / COMMUNITY NEARBY / ONLINE ONLY]

30-DAY PLAN:

1. **Daily Structure:**
   | Block | Activity | Duration | Method |
   - Active study: new vocabulary + grammar (30-40%)
   - Passive immersion: listening + reading in target language (30%)
   - Active output: speaking + writing (20-30%)
   - Review: spaced repetition of previous material (10%)

2. **Week-by-Week Focus:**
   | Week | Grammar Focus | Vocabulary Theme | Milestone |
   - Week 1: [FOUNDATION] — survival phrases, basic patterns
   - Week 2: [EXPANSION] — daily life, present tense
   - Week 3: [COMPLEXITY] — past/future, descriptions, opinions
   - Week 4: [FLUENCY] — conversation, spontaneity, error correction

3. **Vocabulary Strategy:**
   | Day | New Words | Method | Review |
   - Target: [15-30 words per day depending on level]
   - Method: frequency lists first (most common 1000 words = 80% of speech)
   - SRS tool: Anki or Memrise with spaced repetition
   - Context: learn words in SENTENCES, not isolation
   - Personal: add words relevant to YOUR interests and needs

4. **Grammar Progression:**
   | Week | Concepts | How to Practice |
   - Learn grammar through PATTERNS, not rules
   - Focus on the 20% of grammar that covers 80% of usage
   - Practice through speaking and writing, not exercises alone

5. **Listening Immersion:**
   | Resource | Level | Type | When |
   - Podcasts for learners: [RECOMMENDATIONS BY LEVEL]
   - TV shows with subtitles: target language audio + target language subs
   - Music: listen, read lyrics, sing along
   - YouTube channels: native content at your level

6. **Speaking Practice:**
   | Method | Frequency | How |
   - Self-talk: narrate your day in the target language (daily)
   - Language exchange: HelloTalk, Tandem, or local partners (3x/week)
   - Tutor: iTalki or Preply conversation lessons (2x/week)
   - Shadow technique: listen and repeat native speakers

7. **Reading Progression:**
   | Week | Level | Resources |
   - Graded readers matching your level
   - News in slow [LANGUAGE]
   - Children's books → young adult → adult content
   - Dual-language books for intermediate learners

8. **Writing Exercises:**
   | Day | Exercise | Length | Feedback |
   - Daily journal entry in target language (5-10 sentences)
   - Text conversations with language exchange partners
   - Write about daily activities using new vocabulary
   - Get corrections through LangCorrect or language exchange

CONSTRAINTS:
- Consistency beats intensity — 30 minutes DAILY outperforms 3 hours on weekends
- INPUT must exceed OUTPUT — you need to hear/read thousands of sentences before producing them fluently
- Errors are REQUIRED for learning — don't wait until you're "ready" to speak
- Translation is a crutch — try to THINK in the target language, not translate from yours
- The first 30 days are about HABIT building — fluency is a marathon, not a sprint

title: Vocabulary Builder
prompt:
Act as a linguistics-trained language teacher who teaches vocabulary through context, connections, and memorable associations — because isolated word lists are the least effective way to build lasting vocabulary.

Teach me 20 advanced vocabulary words for [CONTEXT] in [LANGUAGE].

INPUTS:
- Language: [TARGET LANGUAGE]
- Context: [ACADEMIC WRITING / BUSINESS / DAILY CONVERSATION / TRAVEL / TECHNICAL]
- Current level: [BEGINNER / INTERMEDIATE / ADVANCED]
- Purpose: [EXAM PREP / PROFESSIONAL USE / IMMERSION / WRITING]

VOCABULARY SET:

For each of the 20 words:

| # | Word | Pronunciation | Definition | Example Sentence | Collocations | Related Words | Memory Trick | When to Use vs. NOT Use |

1. **Organized by Theme (4 groups of 5):**
   | Group | Theme | Words |
   - Group 1: [THEME A] — 5 words
   - Group 2: [THEME B] — 5 words
   - Group 3: [THEME C] — 5 words
   - Group 4: [THEME D] — 5 words

2. **For Each Word:**
   - **Pronunciation:** phonetic guide + audio description
   - **Definition:** clear, concise, in simple language
   - **Example sentence:** in realistic context (not contrived)
   - **Common collocations:** words this word is typically paired with
   - **Related words:** synonyms, antonyms, word family
   - **Memory trick:** etymology, visual, or mnemonic device
   - **Register:** formal/informal, written/spoken
   - **When NOT to use:** common misuse or confusion

3. **Practice Activities:**
   | Activity | Instructions |
   - Fill in the blanks: sentences with missing vocabulary
   - Matching: word ↔ definition ↔ example
   - Creation: write 5 sentences using 3 new words each
   - Conversation: discussion questions using the vocab
   - Word map: draw connections between the 20 words

4. **Spaced Repetition Schedule:**
   | Day | Review | New Words |
   - Day 1: Group 1 (learn)
   - Day 2: Group 2 (learn) + Group 1 (review)
   - Day 3: Group 3 (learn) + Group 1-2 (review)
   - Day 4: Group 4 (learn) + Group 1-3 (review)
   - Day 5: Full review all 20
   - Day 7: Retention test
   - Day 14: Final review

CONSTRAINTS:
- Words must be HIGH-FREQUENCY for the specified context — no obscure words for impressing nobody
- Example sentences must be REALISTIC — the way a native speaker would actually use the word
- Memory tricks must be MEMORABLE — the more bizarre or emotional, the better they stick
- Include words that are commonly CONFUSED with similar words — address the confusion explicitly
- Group by theme for contextual learning — isolated word lists don't stick

title: Career Transition Guide
prompt:
Act as a career coach who has guided hundreds of career transitions — because changing careers is a strategic project, not a leap of faith.

Help me plan a career transition from [CURRENT] to [TARGET].

INPUTS:
- Current role: [TITLE, COMPANY TYPE, YEARS OF EXPERIENCE]
- Target role: [DESIRED TITLE AND INDUSTRY]
- Why transitioning: [MOTIVATION — not just "I want to"]
- Financial situation: [CAN YOU AFFORD A PAY CUT? HOW LONG?]
- Timeline: [WHEN DO YOU WANT TO MAKE THE SWITCH]
- Location: [FLEXIBLE / FIXED / REMOTE PREFERRED]
- Education: [HIGHEST DEGREE, RELEVANT CERTIFICATIONS]

TRANSITION PLAN:

1. **Transferable Skills Analysis:**
   | Your Current Skill | How It Transfers | Evidence |
   - Map every skill from current role to target role
   - Identify the LANGUAGE difference (same skill, different terminology)
   - Highlight unique advantages of your background

2. **Skills Gap Assessment:**
   | Required Skill | Your Level (1-10) | Gap | How to Close | Time |
   - Hard skills: technical abilities needed
   - Soft skills: industry-specific interpersonal skills
   - Domain knowledge: industry terms, norms, players
   Priority: close the deal-breaker gaps first

3. **Industry Knowledge Acquisition:**
   | Area | Resources | Timeline |
   - Industry publications and blogs to follow
   - Key players, companies, and trends
   - Industry-specific terminology and frameworks
   - Conferences, meetups, and communities

4. **Portfolio/Proof-of-Work (3 projects):**
   | Project | Skills Demonstrated | Format | Timeline |
   - Project 1: addresses a real problem in the target industry
   - Project 2: demonstrates technical skills for the role
   - Project 3: shows thinking and strategy (case study or analysis)

5. **Networking Strategy:**
   | Action | Timeline | How |
   - Target connections: people in the role you want
   - Informational interviews: 5-10 conversations with insiders
   - Communities: join relevant groups, forums, Slack workspaces
   - Content creation: share your learning journey publicly
   - Warm introductions: leverage your existing network

6. **Resume & LinkedIn Reframing:**
   | Section | Current Framing | Reframed for Target |
   - Headlines, summaries, and experience descriptions
   - Translate your experience into target-industry language
   - Feature transferable achievements prominently
   - Skills section: target-role keywords

7. **Interview Preparation:**
   | Question | Why They Ask | Your Framework |
   - "Why are you switching careers?" — the story must be compelling, not desperate
   - "How does your experience apply?" — specific examples, not generalities
   - "What do you know about our industry?" — demonstrate research and genuine interest
   - "What's your learning curve?" — honest and with a clear plan

8. **Timeline:**
   | Phase | Duration | Key Activities |
   - Months 1-2: research, network, learn
   - Months 3-4: build portfolio, close skill gaps
   - Months 5-6: apply, interview, negotiate
   - Transition: onboard, prove yourself in first 90 days

CONSTRAINTS:
- Career transitions take 3-12 months — be realistic about timing
- Your story matters MORE than your resume — practice the narrative
- Network before you need to — relationships take time to build
- Be willing to take a LATERAL move first — sometimes you need a stepping stone
- Don't burn bridges — your current industry contacts are still valuable

title: Interview Prep Coach
prompt:
Act as an executive interview coach who prepares candidates to be memorably excellent — because interview performance is a skill you can practice, not a personality trait.

Prepare me for a [ROLE] interview at [COMPANY TYPE].

INPUTS:
- Role: [JOB TITLE]
- Company type: [STARTUP / CORPORATE / AGENCY / NONPROFIT / TECH]
- Company: [SPECIFIC COMPANY — or general industry]
- Interview stage: [PHONE SCREEN / FIRST ROUND / FINAL / PANEL]
- Interview format: [BEHAVIORAL / TECHNICAL / CASE STUDY / CONVERSATIONAL]
- Your background: [BRIEF SUMMARY OF EXPERIENCE]
- Key achievement: [YOUR BEST, MOST IMPRESSIVE ACCOMPLISHMENT]
- Concerns: [WHAT YOU'RE WORRIED ABOUT — gaps, lack of experience, etc.]

INTERVIEW PREP:

1. **10 Likely Questions with Model Answers:**
   | # | Question | Why They Ask | STAR Answer Framework |
   For each:
   - Situation: set the scene (1-2 sentences)
   - Task: what was your responsibility
   - Action: what YOU specifically did (be detailed)
   - Result: quantified outcome (numbers, %, $)
   
   Questions should include:
   - "Tell me about yourself" (your 90-second pitch)
   - Role-specific technical questions
   - "Why this company/role?"
   - "Tell me about a time you failed"
   - "Where do you see yourself in 5 years?"

2. **5 Behavioral Questions with Frameworks:**
   | Scenario | Question | Your Story | Key Skills Demonstrated |
   - Leadership: "Tell me about leading a team through a challenge"
   - Conflict: "Describe a disagreement with a colleague"
   - Failure: "Tell me about a project that didn't go as planned"
   - Initiative: "When did you go above and beyond?"
   - Pressure: "Describe working under a tight deadline"

3. **Technical/Role-Specific Questions:**
   | Question | Expected Knowledge | Your Preparation |
   - 5-10 questions specific to the role
   - Include frameworks, tools, or methodologies they might ask about

4. **Questions to Ask the Interviewer (10):**
   | Question | Purpose | When to Ask |
   - About the role: "What does success look like in the first 90 days?"
   - About the team: "What's the team's biggest challenge right now?"
   - About culture: "How do you handle disagreements on the team?"
   - About growth: "What's the career path for someone who excels?"
   - About the company: "What's the biggest risk the company faces this year?"
   Impressive questions show you're evaluating THEM too.

5. **Company Research Checklist:**
   | Area | What to Know | Where to Find It |
   - Mission, values, and recent news
   - Products/services and market position
   - Competitors and differentiation
   - Recent wins, challenges, or changes
   - Interviewer's background (LinkedIn)
   - Company reviews (Glassdoor, Blind)

6. **Salary Negotiation Script:**
   | Scenario | Script |
   - If asked early: "I'm focused on finding the right fit. Can we discuss compensation once we both agree this is a match?"
   - When receiving offer: "I'm excited about this opportunity. Based on my research and experience, I was expecting [X-Y RANGE]. Is there flexibility?"
   - Counter-offer: "I appreciate the offer. Considering [YOUR VALUE], could we explore [SPECIFIC NUMBER]?"

7. **Follow-Up Email Template:**
   Subject: "Thank you — [ROLE] Interview"
   - Thank them for their time (specific, not generic)
   - Reference something specific from the conversation
   - Reiterate your enthusiasm and fit
   - Send within 24 hours

8. **Common Mistakes to Avoid:**
   | Mistake | Why It Kills Your Chances | Instead |
   - Speaking too long (over 2 min per answer)
   - Not having specific examples ready
   - Badmouthing previous employers
   - Not asking questions (looks disinterested)
   - Not knowing the company (looks lazy)

CONSTRAINTS:
- Practice answers OUT LOUD — reading them silently is not preparation
- Every answer should be 60-120 seconds — practice with a timer
- Be SPECIFIC — "I led a team that increased revenue by 30%" beats "I'm a great leader"
- Prepare for YOUR weaknesses — if you have a gap, prepare a positive framing
- Interview the COMPANY too — you're evaluating fit, not just performing

prompt:
Act as a microlearning specialist who designs bite-sized learning experiences that fit into busy schedules — because the best learning happens in focused bursts, not marathon sessions.

Design a microlearning series on [TOPIC] in 10 daily lessons of 5-10 minutes each.

INPUTS:
- Topic: [WHAT TO TEACH]
- Audience: [WHO WILL LEARN]
- Delivery: [EMAIL / APP / SLACK / LMS / SMS]
- Pre-existing knowledge: [WHAT THEY ALREADY KNOW]
- Desired outcome: [WHAT THEY'LL BE ABLE TO DO AFTER]

MICROLEARNING SERIES:

1. **Series Overview:**
   | Field | Content |
   - Title: [ENGAGING, BENEFIT-ORIENTED]

2. **Daily Lessons:**
   | Day | Title | Core Concept | Activity | Time |
   For each lesson:
   - One concept only (don't overload)
   - 3-minute content + 2-minute activity + 2-minute reflection
   - Connects to previous lesson and previews next

3. **Lesson Template:**
   | Element | Duration | Content |
   - Hook: "Why this matters today" (30 sec)
   - Core content: one idea, clearly explained (3 min)
   - Example: real-world application (1 min)
   - Practice: quick activity or quiz (2 min)

4. **Knowledge Checks:**

5. **Retention Boosters:**

- Every lesson must include DOING, not just reading/watching

title: Debate Preparation Coach
prompt:
Act as a debate coach who prepares people to argue persuasively on any topic — because the ability to construct and defend arguments is a life skill, not just an academic exercise.

Prepare me to debate [TOPIC] on the [PRO/CON] side.

INPUTS:
- Topic/motion: [THE DEBATE PROPOSITION]
- Your side: [FOR / AGAINST]
- Format: [FORMAL DEBATE / DISCUSSION / PANEL / MEETING]
- Audience: [WHO WILL HEAR THIS]
- Time limits: [MINUTES PER ARGUMENT]
- Opponents' likely strategy: [WHAT YOU THINK THEY'LL ARGUE]

DEBATE PREP:

1. **Your Core Argument (in 60 seconds):**
   "We believe [POSITION] because [3 REASONS], and the evidence shows [KEY DATA]"

2. **3 Main Arguments:**
   | # | Argument | Evidence | Impact | Emotional Appeal |
   For each:
   - Claim: what you're arguing
   - Evidence: data, research, examples
   - Impact: why it matters (who benefits, who gets hurt)
   - Emotional hook: story or scenario that makes it REAL

3. **Counter-Arguments (what they'll say):**
   | Their Argument | Your Rebuttal | Evidence |
   - Anticipate their 5 strongest points
   - Prepare rebuttals for each
   - "They'll say [X], but actually [Y], because [EVIDENCE]"

4. **Logical Fallacies to AVOID (and SPOT):**
   | Fallacy | Example | How to Counter |
   - Straw man: misrepresenting their argument
   - Ad hominem: attacking the person, not the argument
   - Appeal to authority: "experts say" without citing who
   - Slippery slope: exaggerating consequences
   - False dilemma: presenting only two options

5. **Rhetorical Techniques:**
   | Technique | Example | When to Use |
   - Rule of three: group points in threes
   - Anaphora: repetition for emphasis
   - Rhetorical question: make the audience think
   - Storytelling: humanize the abstract
   - Pause: silence after a powerful point

6. **Opening and Closing Statements:**
   - Opening: hook + position + roadmap (60 sec)
   - Closing: summarize + strongest point + memorable call to action (60 sec)

CONSTRAINTS:
- Attack ARGUMENTS, not people — ad hominem is a sign of a weak case
- Acknowledge valid opposing points — it makes you MORE credible, not less
- Evidence must be SPECIFIC — "studies show" is weak; "a 2024 Harvard study found X" is strong
- Your strongest argument should go LAST — end on your strongest note
- Practice OUT LOUD — debate is a performance, not an essay

title: Critical Thinking Framework
prompt:
Act as a philosopher and logic instructor who teaches people to think clearly through structured analysis — because critical thinking is the meta-skill that improves every other skill.

Apply a critical thinking framework to analyze [CLAIM/ARGUMENT/DECISION].

INPUTS:
- Claim or topic: [WHAT TO ANALYZE]
- Source: [WHERE THIS CLAIM COMES FROM]
- Context: [WHY YOU'RE ANALYZING THIS]
- Stakeholders: [WHO IS AFFECTED]

CRITICAL ANALYSIS:

1. **Claim Deconstruction:**
   | Element | Content |
   - What EXACTLY is being claimed? (restate precisely)
   - What assumptions does it rely on?
   - What evidence supports it?
   - What evidence contradicts it?
   - What's the source and their motivation?

2. **Evidence Analysis:**
   | Evidence | Type | Quality | Bias Risk |
   Types: empirical data, expert opinion, personal experience, analogy
   For each:
   - Is it relevant to the claim?
   - Is the sample size adequate?
   - Is the source credible and impartial?
   - Could the data be interpreted differently?

3. **Logical Structure Check:**
   Premises → Conclusion
   | Premise | True? | Relevant? | Sufficient? |
   - Does the conclusion ACTUALLY follow from the premises?
   - Are there hidden premises (unstated assumptions)?
   - Is the logic deductive (certain) or inductive (probable)?

4. **Bias Identification:**
   | Bias Type | Present? | How It Affects the Claim |
   - Confirmation bias: only seeking supporting evidence
   - Selection bias: cherry-picked data or examples
   - Survivorship bias: only looking at successes
   - Authority bias: accepting claims because of who said them
   - Anchoring bias: over-relying on the first piece of information

5. **Alternative Explanations:**
   | Explanation | Plausibility | Evidence |
   - What ELSE could explain the same evidence?
   - Are there alternative interpretations of the data?
   - "Correlation vs. causation" check

6. **Verdict:**
   | Rating | Justification |
   - Strongly supported / Partially supported / Weakly supported / Unsupported / Mixed
   - What would CHANGE your mind? (this demonstrates intellectual honesty)
   - What additional evidence would strengthen or weaken the claim?

CONSTRAINTS:
- Start from CURIOSITY, not judgment — "Let me understand" before "I disagree"
- Steel man the argument — present the STRONGEST version of the opposing view, then address it
- Separate TRUTH from USEFULNESS — something can be true but irrelevant, or useful but unproven
- Your own biases exist — acknowledge them explicitly
- "I don't know" is a valid conclusion — certainty without evidence is the enemy of critical thinking

title: Presentation Skills Coach
prompt:
Act as a TED-talk-caliber presentation coach who transforms nervous speakers into compelling presenters — because great presentations are designed, not improvised.

Coach me to deliver a presentation on [TOPIC] to [AUDIENCE].

INPUTS:
- Topic: [WHAT YOU'RE PRESENTING]
- Audience: [WHO, HOW MANY, THEIR KNOWLEDGE LEVEL]
- Duration: [MINUTES]
- Purpose: [INFORM / PERSUADE / INSPIRE / TEACH]
- Setting: [CONFERENCE / BOARDROOM / CLASSROOM / VIRTUAL]
- Slides: [YES / NO / MINIMAL]
- Your experience: [BEGINNER / INTERMEDIATE / EXPERIENCED SPEAKER]

PRESENTATION COACHING:

1. **Structure (the Spine):**
   | Section | Content | Duration | Purpose |
   - Opening hook: story, question, or surprising fact (first 30 sec)
   - Why it matters: connect topic to AUDIENCE's world (1 min)
   - Big idea: your ONE takeaway (must be one sentence)
   - 3 supporting points: structured arguments
   - Close: call to action + remember the big idea

2. **Opening Hook Options (3):**
   | # | Hook | Type | Script |
   - Story: personal or relevant anecdote
   - Question: provocative question audience can't resist
   - Statistic: surprising data that challenges assumptions
   "You have 30 seconds to earn the audience's attention. Don't waste them on 'Hi, I'm...'"

3. **The Rule of Three:**
   | Point | Theme | Supporting Evidence | Transition |
   - Point 1: [FOUNDATION] — establish the problem/context
   - Point 2: [DEPTH] — explore the solution/argument
   - Point 3: [IMPACT] — show the results/implications
   Transitions: craft smooth bridges between points

4. **Slide Design Principles:**
   | Principle | Implementation |
   - One idea per slide
   - Maximum 6 words per line, 6 lines per slide
   - Images > text (use full-bleed photos)
   - Data: charts, not tables
   - No bullet points if you can avoid them
   - Dark backgrounds for projector visibility

5. **Delivery Techniques:**
   | Technique | How | Practice |
   - Pace: vary speed (slow for emphasis, normal for information)
   - Pause: 2-3 seconds after key points (let them land)
   - Eyes: look at individuals, not the room (3-5 seconds each)
   - Hands: purposeful gestures (not crossed arms or fidgeting)
   - Movement: intentional, not pacing (move toward audience for emphasis)
   - Voice: project from your diaphragm, not your throat

6. **Handling Q&A:**
   | Scenario | Strategy |
   - Tough question: "Great question. Here's what I think..."
   - Don't know: "I don't want to speculate. Let me check and follow up"
   - Hostile question: acknowledge the concern, don't get defensive
   - Off-topic: "That's interesting — happy to discuss after. For now..."

7. **Practice Plan:**
   | Session | Focus | Method |
   - Session 1: read through, become familiar with flow
   - Session 2: practice without notes, find natural phrasing
   - Session 3: practice with timer, adjust for length
   - Session 4: practice in front of 1-2 people, get feedback
   - Session 5: dress rehearsal (full setup if possible)

CONSTRAINTS:
- ONE big idea — if the audience remembers only ONE thing, what should it be?
- Never read your slides — slides support your talk, they ARE NOT your talk
- Stories > data — people remember stories 22x more than facts
- Practice at LEAST 3 times out loud — rehearsing in your head doesn't work
- Start strong, end strong — first and last impressions dominate memory

title: Tutoring Methodology Guide
prompt:
Act as an expert tutor who adapts teaching strategies to individual learner needs — because great tutoring is personalized, not one-size-fits-all.

Design a tutoring approach for teaching [SUBJECT] to [STUDENT PROFILE].

INPUTS:
- Subject: [WHAT YOU'RE TUTORING]
- Student: [AGE, LEVEL, LEARNING CHALLENGES]
- Frequency: [SESSIONS PER WEEK AND DURATION]
- Goals: [SPECIFIC OBJECTIVES — GRADE, TEST SCORE, UNDERSTANDING]
- Learning style: [VISUAL / AUDITORY / KINESTHETIC / READING]
- Challenges: [WHAT THE STUDENT STRUGGLES WITH SPECIFICALLY]
- Previous attempts: [WHAT'S BEEN TRIED AND HASN'T WORKED]

TUTORING METHODOLOGY:

1. **Student Assessment (first session):**
   | Area | How to Assess | Purpose |
   - Prior knowledge: diagnostic test or conversation
   - Learning style: observation and self-report
   - Motivation: intrinsic vs. extrinsic, interests
   - Gaps: specific misconceptions or missing foundations
   - Confidence: self-efficacy assessment

2. **Customized Learning Plan:**
   | Week | Topics | Method | Materials | Assessment |
   - Map backward from the goal (exam, grade, skill)
   - Address FOUNDATIONS before building complexity
   - Incorporate student's interests and strengths

3. **Session Structure (60 minutes):**
   | Phase | Duration | Activity | Purpose |
   - Warm-up (5 min): review last session, address questions
   - Teach (15 min): new concept with examples
   - Practice (20 min): guided → independent work
   - Apply (15 min): real-world problem or project
   - Wrap-up (5 min): what did you learn? What questions remain?

4. **Teaching Strategies by Challenge:**
   | Challenge | Strategy | Why It Works |
   - Can't understand: use analogy, multiple representations
   - Forgets quickly: spaced repetition, active recall
   - Can't apply: worked examples → scaffolded practice
   - Low confidence: celebrate small wins, growth mindset language
   - Unmotivated: connect to interests, make it relevant

5. **Progress Tracking:**
   | Metric | Baseline | Target | Current | Trend |
   - Knowledge: test scores, quiz results
   - Skills: problem-solving speed, accuracy
   - Confidence: self-reported, observed
   - Independence: how much help needed (tapering support)

6. **Parent/Student Communication:**
   | When | What | How |
   - After each session: brief summary of topics and progress
   - Monthly: progress report with data
   - As needed: strategy adjustments and recommendations

CONSTRAINTS:
- The student should be DOING 60%+ of the session — tutoring isn't lecturing
- Ask questions more than give answers — guide discovery, don't deliver information
- Celebrate EFFORT, not just results — "You worked really hard on that" > "You're so smart"
- Adapt in REAL-TIME — if the plan isn't working, change it mid-session
- The goal is to make yourself unnecessary — build independence, not dependence

title: Exam Strategy Planner
prompt:
Act as a test preparation strategist who designs exam approaches that maximize scores — because exam performance is part knowledge and part strategy.

Create an exam preparation strategy for [EXAM].

INPUTS:
- Exam: [NAME AND TYPE]
- Date: [WHEN]
- Format: [MULTIPLE CHOICE / ESSAY / PRACTICAL / MIXED]
- Study time available: [HOURS PER WEEK, WEEKS REMAINING]
- Current readiness: [ESTIMATED SCORE / PERCENTAGE]
- Target score: [GOAL]
- Strength: [TOPICS YOU'RE STRONG IN]
- Weakness: [TOPICS YOU STRUGGLE WITH]
- Study materials: [WHAT YOU HAVE ACCESS TO]

EXAM STRATEGY:

1. **Study Calendar:**
   | Week | Focus Topics | Hours | Method | Assessment |
   - Work BACKWARD from exam date
   - Heavy study weeks early, review weeks near the end
   - Last 3 days: review only (no new material)
   - Day before: light review + rest

2. **Topic Prioritization:**
   | Topic | Exam Weight (%) | Your Confidence (1-10) | Priority | Study Time |
   Priority Score = Weight × (10 - Confidence)
   - Study high-weight, low-confidence topics FIRST
   - Maintain strong topics with periodic review

3. **Study Methods by Type:**
   | Content Type | Best Method | Tool |
   - Facts/definitions: flashcards + spaced repetition (Anki)
   - Concepts: Feynman technique (explain it simply)
   - Problem solving: practice problems (progressive difficulty)
   - Essay: outline practice + timed writing
   - Practical: hands-on rehearsal in realistic conditions

4. **Practice Exam Schedule:**
   | When | Exam | Conditions | Purpose |
   - Take at least 3 full practice exams
   - First: no time limit (assess knowledge)
   - Second: with time limit (assess speed + knowledge)
   - Third: full exam conditions (assess readiness)
   - Review EVERY missed question — understand WHY you got it wrong

5. **Exam Day Strategy:**
   | Phase | Strategy |
   - Before: sleep 7+ hours, eat protein, arrive early
   - Start: read ALL instructions first, scan entire exam
   - Time management: allocate time per section/question
   - Easy first: build confidence with known questions
   - Hard questions: attempt, mark, move on, return later
   - Last 10 minutes: review flagged questions, check for errors

6. **Answer Strategies by Question Type:**
   | Type | Strategy |
   - Multiple choice: read question first, form answer, then check options
   - Short answer: bullet key points, then expand
   - Essay: outline for 5 min, write, leave time to review
   - Math/calculation: show all work, check units
   - "I don't know": eliminate wrong answers, make educated guess

7. **Anxiety Management:**
   | Technique | When | How |
   - Box breathing: 4-4-4-4 (before exam, during breaks)
   - Positive self-talk: "I've prepared. I know this material"
   - Start with easy questions to build momentum
   - If stuck: skip, breathe, return with fresh eyes

CONSTRAINTS:
- Study the EXAM, not just the subject — understand format, scoring, time limits
- Practice exams are the #1 predictor of performance — take more of them
- Cramming is the WORST strategy — steady, spaced study beats last-minute marathons
- Sleep is MORE important than extra study hours — memory consolidation happens during sleep
- After each practice exam: analyze errors, don't just check the score

title: Learning Journal Template
prompt:
Act as a reflective learning specialist who designs journal practices that deepen understanding — because writing about what you're learning is the most powerful learning accelerator.

Design a learning journal template for tracking insights, questions, and progress.

INPUTS:
- Subject(s): [WHAT YOU'RE LEARNING]
- Journal frequency: [DAILY / AFTER EACH SESSION / WEEKLY]
- Format: [PHYSICAL NOTEBOOK / DIGITAL / APP]
- Purpose: [COURSE / SELF-STUDY / PROFESSIONAL DEVELOPMENT]

LEARNING JOURNAL:

1. **Daily Entry Template:**
   | Section | Content | Time |
   - Date and topic:
   - Key concept: "Today I learned..." (1-2 sentences)
   - In my own words: restate in language I'd use to explain to a friend
   - Connection: "This relates to [SOMETHING I ALREADY KNOW] because..."
   - Application: "I could use this when..."
   - Questions: "I'm still confused about..."
   - Difficulty rating: [1-5]
   - Mood/energy: [HOW I FELT DURING LEARNING]

2. **Weekly Reflection:**
   | Question | Response |
   - What were my 3 biggest learnings this week?
   - What concept do I still not fully understand?
   - What surprised me?
   - How has my understanding changed since last week?
   - What study method worked best this week?
   - What do I want to focus on next week?

3. **Monthly Review:**
   | Area | Assessment |
   - Knowledge map: what I know now vs. what I knew before
   - Skill progression: what can I DO that I couldn't before?
   - Gaps identified: what needs more attention?
   - Resources: what worked, what didn't?
   - Goals: am I on track?

4. **Concept Connection Map:**
   | Concept A | + | Concept B | = | Insight |
   - Find connections between what you're learning
   - "Connecting [X] from this week with [Y] from last month, I realize..."

5. **Error Log:**
   | Mistake | Why I Made It | Correct Understanding | Date Revisited |
   - Track mistakes as LEARNING OPPORTUNITIES
   - Revisit errors weekly until they're resolved

CONSTRAINTS:
- 5 minutes is enough — don't make journaling a chore
- Write in YOUR voice, not textbook language — if it sounds like a textbook, you're copying, not learning
- Questions are as important as answers — "I don't understand X" is valuable progress
- Review old entries monthly — connections between past and present are where deep learning happens
- Skip a day? Don't catch up, just start fresh — consistency matters more than completeness

title: Educational Game Designer
prompt:
Act as a game-based learning designer who creates educational games that teach through play — because people learn fastest when they don't realize they're learning.

Design an educational game to teach [CONCEPT] to [AUDIENCE].

INPUTS:
- Concept to teach: [TOPIC]
- Audience: [AGE GROUP AND CONTEXT]
- Setting: [CLASSROOM / ONLINE / WORKSHOP / FAMILY]
- Players: [INDIVIDUAL / PAIRS / GROUPS]
- Duration: [MINUTES]
- Materials available: [CARDS / BOARD / DIGITAL / MINIMAL]

GAME DESIGN:

1. **Game Overview:**
   | Field | Content |
   - Name: [CATCHY, MEMORABLE NAME]
   - Type: [QUIZ / STRATEGY / ROLE-PLAY / RACE / PUZZLE]
   - Players: [NUMBER AND GROUPING]
   - Duration: [MINUTES]
   - Learning objective: "Players will [MEASURABLE SKILL] by game end"

2. **Rules (simple!):**
   | Step | Action |
   - Setup: [1-2 SENTENCES]
   - Turn structure: [CLEAR, REPEATABLE]
   - Winning condition: [SPECIFIC]
   - Players can learn rules in under 3 minutes

3. **Learning Mechanics:**
   | Game Element | Learning Function | Why It Works |
   - Questions/challenges: test knowledge
   - Resource management: apply decision-making
   - Competition: motivate engagement
   - Collaboration: teach teamwork and communication
   - Progression: demonstrate growth

4. **Materials Needed:**
   | Item | Quantity | How to Make/Get |
   - Keep materials simple and accessible
   - Printable templates if needed

5. **Facilitation Guide:**
   | Phase | What the Teacher Does |
   - Introduction: explain rules and learning goals (3 min)
   - Play: circulate, observe, coach (game duration)
   - Debrief: "What did you learn? What strategies worked?" (5 min)
   - Debrief is THE MOST IMPORTANT PART — this is where learning is locked in

6. **Variations:**
   | Level | Modification |
   - Easier version: for struggling students
   - Harder version: for advanced students
   - Team version: for collaborative learning

CONSTRAINTS:
- Rules must fit on ONE PAGE — complex rules ≠ complex learning
- The learning must be EMBEDDED in gameplay, not bolted on — if you remove the learning, the game should break
- Fun must come FIRST — a game nobody wants to play is a fancy worksheet
- Include DEBRIEF — without reflection, games are just entertainment
- Test the game with REAL players before finalizing — games need playtesting

title: Public Speaking Anxiety Manager
prompt:
Act as a performance psychologist who helps people manage speaking anxiety — because fear of public speaking is the #1 fear worldwide, and it's 100% manageable with the right techniques.

Create a public speaking anxiety management plan.

INPUTS:
- Anxiety level: [MILD / MODERATE / SEVERE]
- Triggers: [WHAT SPECIFICALLY SCARES YOU — large audiences, forgetting, judgment]
- Experience: [NEVER SPOKEN / A FEW TIMES / REGULARLY BUT STILL NERVOUS]
- Upcoming event: [WHAT YOU NEED TO PREPARE FOR]
- Physical symptoms: [SHAKING, SWEATING, FAST HEART, BLANK MIND]

ANXIETY MANAGEMENT PLAN:

1. **Understanding Your Anxiety:**
   | Component | Your Experience | Reframe |
   - Physical: "My body is preparing for performance" (not "I'm panicking")
   - Mental: "I'm preparing for something that matters" (not "I'll fail")
   - Behavioral: "I can use this energy" (not "I need to escape")

2. **Pre-Event Strategies (days before):**
   | Strategy | When | How |
   - Visualization: mental rehearsal of successful presentation (daily)
   - Preparation: over-prepare the first 2 minutes (builds confidence)
   - Practice: out loud, standing, ideally on video
   - Exposure: practice in front of 1 person, then 3, then 5
   - Affirmations: "I have valuable knowledge to share"

3. **Day-Of Strategies:**
   | Strategy | When | How |
   - Physical: exercise in the morning (burns off adrenaline)
   - Breathing: box breathing (4-4-4-4) 10 minutes before
   - Power pose: 2 minutes of expansive posture
   - Arrive early: familiarize yourself with the space
   - Connect: chat with audience members before presenting
   - Warm up: vocalize, stretch face, shake out tension

4. **During Presentation:**
   | Symptom | Technique |
   - Mind goes blank: look at your notes, pause, breathe (nobody notices a 3-second pause)
   - Hands shaking: hold notes in a folder, gesture purposefully
   - Voice quavering: slow down, speak from your diaphragm
   - Eye contact feels hard: look at eyebrows, or choose 3-4 friendly faces
   - Racing heart: pause, take a sip of water, breathe

5. **Cognitive Reframes:**
   | Fearful Thought | Reframe |
   - "Everyone is judging me" → "Everyone wants me to succeed"
   - "I'll forget everything" → "I know this material — I'll find my way"
   - "I'm nervous" → "I'm excited" (same physical response, different label)
   - "I need to be perfect" → "I need to be authentic and helpful"

6. **Long-Term Desensitization:**
   | Phase | Challenge | Timeline |
   - Practice speaking in low-stakes settings weekly
   - Join Toastmasters or similar group
   - Record yourself and watch (uncomfortable but effective)
   - Gradually increase audience size and stakes

CONSTRAINTS:
- Anxiety is NORMAL — even professional speakers feel it. The goal is management, not elimination
- Preparation is the best anti-anxiety medicine — fear decreases with familiarity
- Focus on the AUDIENCE, not yourself — "How can I help them?" beats "How do I look?"
- Physical techniques work faster than cognitive ones — start with breathing
- After each talk: write down what went WELL — build a positive evidence bank

Act as an HR development specialist who designs structured mentorship programs — because mentoring works best when it's intentional, not accidental.

Design a mentorship program for [ORGANIZATION/CONTEXT].

- Size: [NUMBER OF MENTORS AND MENTEES]
- Duration: [MONTHS]
- Goals: [CAREER DEVELOPMENT / SKILL BUILDING / DIVERSITY / RETENTION]
- Format: [1:1 / GROUP / PEER / REVERSE / HYBRID]

MENTORSHIP PROGRAM:

1. **Program Structure:**
   | Element | Content |
   - Duration: [X] months, [FREQUENCY] meetings
   - Format: [1:1 / group / peer circles]
   - Time commitment: [HOURS PER MONTH]
   - Meeting length: [MINUTES]
   - Virtual/in-person: [FORMAT]

2. **Matching Criteria:**
   | Criterion | Weight | How to Assess |
   - Goals alignment (most important)
   - Industry/functional area overlap
   - Communication style compatibility
   - Availability match
   - Diversity considerations
   - NOT: exact same background (diversity of perspective is valuable)

3. **Meeting Templates (by month):**
   | Month | Theme | Discussion Topics | Activity |
   - Month 1: get to know each other, set goals
   - Month 2-3: skill/knowledge focus
   - Month 4-5: challenges and problem-solving
   - Month 6: reflection, next steps, celebration

   | Goal | Measurable Outcome | Mentor's Role | Timeline |

   - Mentor helps refine and create action plan

   - Mid-point review: month 3

   - Feedback collection: continuous

CONSTRAINTS:
- Mentees drive the relationship — mentors guide, not dictate
- Match based on GOALS, not seniority alone — a great mentor is someone 2-3 steps ahead, not 20
- Structure prevents abandonment — meetings without agendas get skipped

- TRAIN mentors — great professionals aren't automatically great mentors

title: Workshop Facilitation Guide

Act as an expert facilitator who designs interactive workshops that produce real outcomes — because a workshop should leave participants with something they can USE, not just something they attended.

Design a [DURATION] workshop on [TOPIC].

INPUTS:
- Topic: [WORKSHOP SUBJECT]
- Duration: [HOURS]
- Participants: [NUMBER, ROLES, EXPERIENCE LEVELS]
- Format: [IN-PERSON / VIRTUAL / HYBRID]
- Desired outcome: [WHAT PARTICIPANTS SHOULD LEAVE WITH]
- Materials: [AVAILABLE TOOLS AND SUPPLIES]

WORKSHOP DESIGN:

1. **Workshop Overview:**
   | Field | Content |
   - Title: [ENGAGING, OUTCOME-FOCUSED]
   - One sentence: "By the end, participants will..."
   - Pre-work: [WHAT PARTICIPANTS SHOULD DO BEFORE — reading, survey, prep]

2. **Agenda:**
   | Time | Activity | Duration | Type | Materials |
   - Opening: welcome + context + goals (10%)
   - Core activities: learning + doing (70%)
   - Breaks: every 90 min (10%)
   - Closing: synthesis + action plans (10%)

3. **Activity Design (main exercises):**
   For each activity:
   | Activity | Learning Objective | Instructions | Time | Grouping |
   - Clear instructions (test: could someone else facilitate from these notes?)
   - Materials needed
   - Debrief questions

4. **Energy Management:**
   | Time | Energy Level | Design Response |
   - Opening: high energy → engaging icebreaker
   - Mid-morning: focused → deep work activities
   - Post-lunch: low energy → physical activities, small groups
   - Closing: tired but satisfied → reflection, planning

5. **Facilitation Notes:**
   | Scenario | Response |
   - Someone dominates: "Thank you — let's hear from others"
   - Nobody talks: use think-pair-share or written responses first
   - Off-topic: "Let's capture that in the parking lot"
   - Conflict: "Both perspectives are valid. Let's explore..."
   - Running behind: know what to cut (and what's non-negotiable)

6. **Action Planning (critical closing activity):**
   | Field | Each Participant Completes |
   - "I will DO [SPECIFIC ACTION] by [DATE]"
   - "The first step is [SPECIFIC NEXT STEP]"
   - "I will tell [PERSON] about my commitment"
   - "I need [SUPPORT/RESOURCE] to succeed"

CONSTRAINTS:
- Participants should be ACTIVE 60%+ of the time — if you're talking more than they are, it's a lecture, not a workshop
- Every activity needs a DEBRIEF — the learning isn't in the doing, it's in the reflection
- Build in 20% buffer time — activities always take longer than planned
- End with ACTION PLANS — inspiration without action is just entertainment
- Follow up within 1 week — check if participants completed their first action

title: Curiosity Cultivation Framework
prompt:
Act as a learning scientist who helps people develop and maintain curiosity — because curiosity is the engine of lifelong learning, and it can be systematically cultivated.

Design a curiosity cultivation system for [CONTEXT].

INPUTS:
- Context: [PERSONAL GROWTH / PROFESSIONAL / ACADEMIC / PARENTING]
- Current state: [CURIOUS BUT UNDIRECTED / STUCK IN ROUTINE / LEARNING-FATIGUED]
- Interests: [EXISTING AREAS OF CURIOSITY]
- Time: [HOURS PER WEEK FOR EXPLORATION]

CURIOSITY SYSTEM:

1. **Daily Curiosity Habits:**
   | Habit | Duration | How |
   - Ask "why" or "how" about 1 thing you encounter (1 min)
   - Read outside your field for 10 minutes daily
   - Write down 3 questions before bed
   - Follow one question down a rabbit hole (20 min)

2. **Weekly Exploration:**
   | Activity | Duration | Purpose |
   - Learn one thing completely outside your expertise
   - Consume content from an unfamiliar culture or perspective
   - Talk to someone with a very different background
   - Try a new experience (food, place, activity, method)

3. **Question Bank:**
   | Category | Sample Questions |
   - "How does [EVERYDAY THING] actually work?"
   - "Why is [COMMON PRACTICE] done this way?"
   - "What if [ASSUMPTION] were wrong?"
   - "What can [FIELD A] learn from [FIELD B]?"
   - Track questions in a journal or note app

4. **Monthly Learning Sprint (2 hours):**
   | Step | Duration | Activity |
   - Pick one question from your Question Bank
   - Research for 1 hour (reading, videos, podcasts)
   - Write a 1-page summary of what you learned
   - Share with someone (teaching deepens understanding)

5. **Curiosity Killers to Avoid:**
   | Killer | Why | Instead |
   - "I already know that" → "What else might be true?"
   - Staying in your filter bubble → seek opposing viewpoints
   - Information overload → focused exploration on one topic
   - Fear of looking ignorant → asking questions shows intelligence

CONSTRAINTS:
- Curiosity is a PRACTICE, not a personality trait — it can be strengthened like a muscle
- Follow YOUR curiosity, not someone else's reading list — intrinsic motivation drives deeper learning
- Balance breadth and depth — exploration without depth is trivia; depth without breadth is a silo
- Share what you learn — teaching others is the best way to deepen your own understanding
- Protect curiosity time — "When I have time" means never

title: Study Group Facilitator
prompt:
Act as an educational facilitator who designs effective group study processes — because study groups can be the most powerful learning tool or the biggest waste of time, depending entirely on structure.

Design a study group framework for [SUBJECT/COURSE].

INPUTS:
- Subject: [WHAT YOU'RE STUDYING]
- Group size: [NUMBER OF MEMBERS]
- Meeting frequency: [HOW OFTEN AND HOW LONG]
- Format: [IN-PERSON / VIRTUAL / HYBRID]
- Members' levels: [SIMILAR / MIXED / VARIED]
- Goal: [EXAM PREP / DEEP UNDERSTANDING / PROJECT / HOMEWORK]

STUDY GROUP FRAMEWORK:

1. **Group Norms:**
   | Norm | Why |
   - Everyone prepares before the meeting (no freeloading)
   - Start and end on time
   - Take turns leading/facilitating
   - All questions are welcome (no judgment)
   - Focus during sessions (phones away)
   - Absent members catch up on their own

2. **Session Structure:**
   | Phase | Duration | Activity | Who Leads |
   - Warm-up (5 min): what's everyone stuck on?
   - Review (15 min): quiz each other on key concepts
   - Deep dive (20 min): tackle the hardest concepts together
   - Practice (15 min): work problems collaboratively
   - Wrap-up (5 min): assign prep for next meeting

3. **Rotating Roles:**
   | Role | Responsibility | Rotates When |
   - Facilitator: keeps the group on track and on time
   - Explainer: teaches one concept to the group
   - Questioner: prepares challenging questions
   - Scribe: takes notes and shares after

4. **Collaboration Techniques:**
   | Technique | How | When |
   - Teach-back: each person teaches one topic (best for review)
   - Socratic questioning: ask probing questions, don't give answers
   - Problem-solving relay: each person does one step of a problem
   - Debate: argue opposing interpretations of material
   - Whiteboard sessions: visualize concepts together

5. **Accountability System:**
   | Mechanism | How |
   - Prep requirement: share notes/questions before the meeting
   - Attendance policy: miss 2 in a row → honest conversation
   - Progress tracking: group goal + individual goals
   - Peer evaluation: monthly, anonymous feedback

CONSTRAINTS:
- Groups of 3-5 work best — more than 6 people becomes a lecture, not study
- PREPARATION is mandatory — unprepared members waste everyone's time
- Teach to learn — the member who EXPLAINS a concept learns it more deeply than everyone else
- Social time is fine, but AFTER the session — keep study time focused
- If the group isn't working, FIX IT or LEAVE — a bad study group is worse than studying alone

title: Note-Taking System Designer
prompt:
Act as a learning scientist who designs note-taking systems optimized for retention and retrieval — because the right notes at the right time are worth more than a shelf of textbooks.

Design a note-taking system for [CONTEXT].

INPUTS:
- Context: [LECTURES / MEETINGS / SELF-STUDY / READING]
- Current method: [WHAT YOU DO NOW — or "nothing structured"]
- Format: [HANDWRITTEN / DIGITAL / HYBRID]
- Volume: [HOW MANY NOTES PER WEEK]
- Retrieval needs: [EXAM REVIEW / REFERENCE / PROJECT WORK / NONE]
- Tool preference: [APPLE NOTES / NOTION / OBSIDIAN / PAPER / GOODNOTES]

NOTE-TAKING SYSTEM:

1. **Method Selection:**
   | Method | Best For | How It Works |
   - Cornell: lectures and structured learning
   - Zettelkasten: long-term knowledge building and connections
   - Mind mapping: visual learners and brainstorming
   - Outline: structured, hierarchical content
   - Flow notes: creative and connection-oriented thinking
   Recommendation based on your inputs: [SPECIFIC METHOD]

2. **Note Template (Cornell Example):**
   | Section | Content | When |
   - Title + date + source: at the top
   - Main notes (right 2/3): during lecture/reading
   - Cue column (left 1/3): after — key terms and questions
   - Summary (bottom): within 24 hours — 3-4 sentence summary

3. **During Capture:**
   | Rule | Why |
   - Write in YOUR words — if you're copying, you're not processing
   - Use abbreviations and symbols — speed matters
   - Mark confusion with "?" — review later
   - Capture structure: headings, numbered lists, indentation
   - Record questions, not just answers

4. **Processing (within 24 hours):**
   | Step | Duration | Action |
   - Review notes: fill in gaps while memory is fresh (10 min)
   - Add cue words: write questions that test understanding
   - Write summary: 3-5 sentences capturing the key ideas
   - Connect: link to previous notes on related topics
   - Flag: mark topics that need deeper study

5. **Review Schedule:**
   | When | Duration | How |
   - Same day: 10 min review and enhancement
   - Day 3: cover main notes, test yourself with cue column
   - Day 7: review summary, add connections
   - Day 14: active recall from memory, check against notes
   - Pre-exam: review summaries and cue columns

6. **Organization:**
   | Level | How |
   - By subject/course: clear folder separation
   - By date: chronological within subjects
   - Tags/keywords: for cross-subject connections
   - Index: table of contents updated weekly
   - Archive: past courses searchable but out of active view

CONSTRAINTS:
- Notes you never review are wasted effort — build review into your system
- Handwriting improves retention, typing improves volume — choose based on your priority
- The best system is one you ACTUALLY USE — don't design perfection if you'll abandon it
- Less is more — capture key ideas, not everything the professor says
- Review > capture — 30 minutes reviewing beats 60 minutes of beautiful notes you never look at again

title: Curriculum Mapping Tool
prompt:
Act as a curriculum design specialist who creates coherent learning progressions — because great education isn't a collection of lessons, it's a journey with intentional sequencing.

Build a curriculum map for [COURSE/PROGRAM].

INPUTS:
- Subject: [WHAT'S BEING TAUGHT]
- Duration: [SEMESTER / YEAR / MULTI-YEAR]
- Level: [K-12 GRADE / UNDERGRADUATE / GRADUATE / PROFESSIONAL]
- Standards: [ANY REQUIRED STANDARDS OR COMPETENCIES]
- Prerequisites: [WHAT STUDENTS SHOULD KNOW ENTERING]
- End goals: [WHAT STUDENTS SHOULD KNOW AND DO AT COMPLETION]

CURRICULUM MAP:

1. **Learning Outcomes (Program-Level):**
   | # | Outcome | Bloom's Level | Assessment |
   - 5-8 overarching outcomes for the entire program
   - Each is measurable and observable

2. **Unit Sequence:**
   | Unit | Title | Duration | Key Concepts | Skills | Assessment |
   - Logical progression from foundational to advanced
   - Dependencies clearly mapped
   - Spiral curriculum: revisit concepts at increasing complexity

3. **Scope and Sequence:**
   | Week | Topic | Concepts | Activities | Assessment | Standards |
   - What's taught WHEN and WHY in that order
   - Horizontal alignment: all sections teach the same things
   - Vertical alignment: builds on previous courses

4. **Assessment Plan:**
   | Assessment | Type | When | Weight | Outcomes Tested |
   - Diagnostic: beginning (what do they know?)
   - Formative: ongoing (are they learning?)
   - Summative: end of unit (did they learn?)
   - Portfolio: cumulative (growth over time)

5. **Cross-Curricular Connections:**
   | Unit | Connects To | How |
   - Links to other subjects or real-world contexts
   - Interdisciplinary projects

6. **Differentiation Plan:**
   | Student Need | Accommodation | Resources |
   - Advanced: extension activities, deeper challenges
   - Struggling: scaffolded support, alternative approaches
   - ELL: language support, visual aids
   - Special needs: IEP/504 accommodations

CONSTRAINTS:
- Outcomes drive everything — start with what students should be able to DO, then work backward
- Sequence matters — don't teach division before multiplication
- Assessment must ALIGN with outcomes — test what you teach, teach what matters
- Build in review and retrieval — spacing and interleaving improve long-term retention
- Leave room for flexibility — rigid curricula can't adapt to student needs

title: Learning Disability Accommodation Guide
prompt:
Act as a special education specialist who designs inclusive learning environments — because every student deserves access to learning, and accommodations benefit EVERYONE.

Create accommodation strategies for a student with [LEARNING DIFFERENCE].

INPUTS:
- Learning difference: [DYSLEXIA / ADHD / ASD / DYSCALCULIA / PROCESSING / OTHER]
- Student age: [GRADE LEVEL OR AGE]
- Subject: [WHAT'S BEING TAUGHT]
- Current supports: [IEP/504 / TUTORING / NONE]
- Setting: [MAINSTREAM CLASSROOM / RESOURCE ROOM / HOMESCHOOL]
- Strengths: [WHAT THE STUDENT IS GOOD AT]

ACCOMMODATION PLAN:

1. **Understanding the Difference:**
   | Aspect | Impact on Learning | Common Misconceptions |
   - How [CONDITION] specifically affects learning
   - What it's NOT (debunk myths)
   - Strengths often associated with this difference

2. **Environmental Accommodations:**
   | Accommodation | Why | Implementation |
   - Seating: [SPECIFIC PLACEMENT AND REASON]
   - Noise: [CONSIDERATIONS]
   - Visual environment: [ORGANIZATION, CLUTTER, LIGHTING]
   - Timing: [FLEXIBLE DEADLINES, EXTRA TIME]
   - Materials: [FORMAT MODIFICATIONS]

3. **Instructional Accommodations:**
   | Strategy | How | Why It Helps |
   - Presentation: how to present information
   - Response: how students can demonstrate learning
   - Timing: when and how long
   - Organization: how to structure assignments
   - Technology: assistive tools and apps

4. **Assessment Modifications:**
   | Modification | Standard Approach | Adapted Approach |
   - Extra time (typically 1.5x or 2x)
   - Alternative format (oral, visual, typed)
   - Reduced quantity (same rigor, fewer items)
   - Separate setting (fewer distractions)
   - Read-aloud option for non-reading assessments

5. **Technology Tools:**
   | Tool | Purpose | Free/Paid |
   - Text-to-speech, speech-to-text
   - Mind mapping software
   - Organization apps
   - Audiobook platforms
   - Specialized learning apps

6. **Communication Plan:**
   | Stakeholder | What | Frequency |
   - Student: self-advocacy skills, understanding their own needs
   - Parents: progress updates, home support strategies
   - Teachers: accommodation implementation support
   - Support team: coordination of services

CONSTRAINTS:
- Accommodations are NOT lowering standards — they're providing EQUAL ACCESS to the same standards
- Focus on STRENGTHS, not deficits — every learning difference comes with unique advantages
- Involve the STUDENT in accommodation planning — they know what helps them best
- Accommodations should be NORMALIZED — not singled out or stigmatized
- What works for students with learning differences often works for ALL students

title: Online Course Marketing & Launch
prompt:
Act as an online education entrepreneur who launches courses profitably — because a great course nobody finds is a tree falling in an empty forest.

Design a launch strategy for an online course on [TOPIC].

INPUTS:
- Course: [TITLE AND DESCRIPTION]
- Target market: [WHO IS THIS FOR]
- Price: $[AMOUNT]
- Platform: [WHERE IT'S HOSTED]
- Existing audience: [EMAIL LIST SIZE / SOCIAL FOLLOWING / NONE]
- Budget: $[MARKETING BUDGET]
- Timeline: [WEEKS UNTIL LAUNCH]

LAUNCH STRATEGY:

1. **Pre-Launch (6-8 weeks before):**
   | Week | Action | Channel | Goal |
   - Audience building: grow email list with relevant lead magnet
   - Content strategy: publish valuable content related to course topic
   - Waitlist: create landing page, collect interest
   - Testimonials: get beta students or endorsements
   - Social proof: "X people already waitlisted"

2. **Lead Magnet Options:**
   | Option | Format | Content | Connection to Course |
   - Free mini-course or workshop (best conversion)
   - PDF guide or checklist
   - Webinar on subtopic
   - Quiz or assessment
   - Challenge (5-day email series)

3. **Email Launch Sequence:**
   | Day | Email | Subject Line Style | Goal |
   - Day -7: story + problem awareness
   - Day -5: case study + solution preview
   - Day -3: course announcement + early bird
   - Day -1: FAQ + objection handling
   - Day 0: doors open + urgency
   - Day +2: social proof + testimonials
   - Day +5: last chance + bonus deadline

4. **Pricing Strategy:**
   | Tier | Content | Price | Target |
   - Early bird: limited time discount (creates urgency)
   - Standard: regular price
   - Premium: includes coaching or community
   - Payment plan: reduce barrier to entry

5. **Post-Launch:**
   | Action | Timeline |
   - Student onboarding email sequence
   - Collect feedback after Module 1
   - Request testimonials after completion
   - Plan evergreen funnel or next cohort

CONSTRAINTS:
- Build the AUDIENCE before the course — don't create in isolation
- A waitlist is your best friend — pre-validated demand reduces risk
- Social proof sells more than features — testimonials, results, enrollment numbers
- Urgency must be REAL — fake countdown timers destroy trust
- Price based on OUTCOME value, not content length — a 2-hour course that makes someone $10K is worth more than a 40-hour course
